  • 18h •
  • 2 TB of RAM costs roughly $40,000.
  • Adding a Redis cache in front of your existing DB costs roughly nothing.
  • That's the actual lesson interviewers are testing when they ask "improve the latency." Not whether you can spend money.
  • Whether you understand where the milliseconds are actually going.

    A network hop is ~1ms.
    An SSD read is ~100µs.
    A RAM read is ~100ns.

    Every layer of your architecture is a bet on which of those you're touching, and how often. The senior engineer's answer is never "scale vertically."

    It's "where is the request actually spending its time, and which layer can I move it closer to the user?"

    We've built a structured session on latency and throughput step by step.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Feed post number 2

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1d •
  • Half the internet thinks this AI is dumb.
  • The other half thinks it's correct.
  • Both are right. The question gave four line segments a name "sides." It then asked how many remain after removing two.
  • The AI did the literal maths: 4 minus 2 equals 2.

    We were silently assuming a second rule the question never stated: "it must still be a rectangle at the end."

    That gap between what was literally asked and what was meant is exactly the gap that ships bugs in production every day.

    APIs return 200 OK with empty bodies because the spec didn't say "must have data."

    Migrations succeed with zero rows touched because nobody specified the WHERE clause was mandatory. Cron jobs run forever because nobody defined "done."

    The senior engineer is the one who writes specs the dumbest possible interpreter can't get wrong.

    That's the actual skill. Precision over assumption.

    We've built a structured session on API design and system contracts step by step. Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Feed post number 3

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2d •
  • Stripe processes 13,000 requests per second.
  • Cloudflare processes 50 million requests per second.
  • Discord processes 4 billion messages per day.
  • None of them drop a single one to overload.

  • The mechanism is a token bucket. Every API key gets a bucket of N tokens. Each request consumes one.

    Tokens refill at a fixed rate. Burst when you have them, throttled when you don't. The counter lives in Redis with atomic INCR, replicated across regions, decisions in under a millisecond.

    That's it. Same pattern behind every API you've ever hit.

    Senior interviews push exactly here in system design interviews: "Design a rate limiter for 1M users across 5 regions."

    Most candidates freeze at counter consistency.
    We've built a structured session on rate limiting step by step.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • DSA gets you shortlisted. But it’s system design that gets you the big offers, especially in 2026, where the focus is primarily LLD and HLD.
  • Here are the best questions on essential LLD, so you can cut down the time and learn fast. These are from various interview experiences I came across during my research.
  • Here is the list, grouped by question patterns so you know exactly what to practice.

  • Also, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    1. Resource and booking systems

    Pattern: limited resources, time slots, no double booking, fair allocation.

    - Design a Parking Lot
    - Design a Parking Lot - Multi Threaded
    - Design a Simple Elevator System - Multiple Lifts
    - Design Elevator Management System - Single Lift
    - Design an Elevator System - Request Feasibility (Single Lift)
    - Design a Train Platform Management System
    - Design a Meeting Room Reservation System
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler - List Bookings
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler for Recurrent Meetings
    - Design a Car Rental System
    - Design Locker Management System for Warehouse Packages
    - Design a Movie ticket booking system like BookMyShow

    2. Money, commerce and pricing flows

    Pattern: orders, payments, balances, revenue split, leaderboards around money or points.

    - Design a restaurant food ordering system like Zomato, Swiggy, DoorDash
    - Design expense sharing app like Splitwise
    - Design Stock Trading Platform like Zerodha, Groww
    - Design a Shopping Cart
    - Design a Payment Wallet like PayPal
    - Design an order and inventory management system - Multi Threaded
    - Design Product Plan Cost Explorer
    - Design a customer support agent rating leaderboard
    - Design a Leaderboard for Fantasy Teams
    - Design Last Lap Hero (F1 Race Tracker)

    3. Editors, spreadsheets and office tools

    Pattern: document state, operations on content, formulas, undo or recalculation.

    - Design a Text Editor or Word Processor like Microsoft Word
    - Design a Text Editor with Undo and Redo
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations and Basic Editing
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations
    - Design spreadsheet like Microsoft Excel
    - Design Excel Sum Formula

    4. File systems, search and storage

    Pattern: hierarchical paths, metadata, indexing, queries on files or logs.

    - Design Unix `find` command for file search
    - Design Unix `find` command with boolean predicates
    - Design File System (cd with `-`)
    - Design File System
    - Design In Memory File System
    - Design File Collections Tracker
    - Design Log Storage System

    5. Search, lookup and dictionaries

    Pattern: key based lookup, tries, prefix search.

    - Design Dictionary App to store words and their meanings
    - Design Phone Directory
    - Design Google Search Autocomplete

  • If your production database leaked tonight, how bad would it actually be? Be honest.
  • Not "we'd handle it." Actually, what's in there?
  • Plaintext passwords?
  • Unsalted hashes?
    PII in clear text?
    API keys checked into env tables?
    Session tokens that don't expire?

    Most engineers have never audited their own DB for this. They assume someone else did.

    Senior interviewers know and "design a user auth system" is how they find out you don't.

    We've built a structured session on database fundamentals and the design choices that decide whether a breach is a tweet or a company-ender.

  • Dear backend engineers, repeat after me:
  • A retry is not safe by default.
  • A 200 OK is not a guarantee it ran once.
  • "The client will handle duplicates" is not an architecture.
  • Idempotency is the one pattern standing between your service and a payments disaster.

    Senior interviewers know this. That's why "design a checkout flow" always ends in "what happens if the request times out?"

    Fumble it and you lose in the round. Nail it and you sound like a pro who's actually shipped money-moving code.

    We've built a structured session on idempotency step by step.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Feed post number 7

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1w •
  • Imagine you're interviewing for the SDE-II role at Amazon, you're in a system design round and the interviewer asks you: “You can scroll Instagram Reels for hours and almost never see the same reel twice. How would you design that?”
  • 1. Reframe the requirement
  • Goal: For a given user, while they are actively scrolling Reels, they should almost never see the same reel again. It is ok if:

  • - A reel reappears after some days or weeks
    - A reel reappears if there was a serious backend failure

    So I want a per user, sliding window “seen set” that filters candidates before ranking.

    Also, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    2. High-level flow

    For one swipe:

    1. Client event

    -User watches reel R123 for longer than a threshold or interacts with it.
    -Client sends an impression event to backend.

    2. Impression logging

    -Event hits a write API which pushes into a log stream (Kafka style).
    -From that stream we update:
    +Online store for fast “has this user seen X recently”.
    +Data warehouse for long term analytics and model training.

    3. Next reel request

    -Client calls GET /reels/next?userId=U.
    -Ranking service collects a large candidate set (thousands) from many sources: follow graph, interest graph, trending, exploration, ads.
    -Before scoring, it filters out anything that appears in user U’s seen set.
    -Rank, pick top 1, send to client, prefetch the next few.

    Key piece is how we build that seen set.

    3.1 Seen set design

    Constraints:

    -Users might watch thousands of reels per day.
    -We do not need perfect history for years, only enough to avoid dupes in a session or short horizon.
    -Needs to be cheap and fast. I am ok with an occasional repeat if it keeps infra simple.

    Design:

    -For each user U, maintain SeenReels[U] which stores the last N reel ids, with TTL.
    +N may be 20k to 50k. That already covers many hours of scrolling.

    -Store it in a distributed in memory key value store like Redis or Meta equivalent.
    +Key: user id
    +Value: compact structure of reel ids

    -To compress, use one of:
    +A ring buffer of 64 bit ids, evict oldest on insert
    +Or a Bloom filter per day for approximate checks

    -On impression processing, we append the reel id into SeenReels[U].
    -In ranking, we fetch SeenReels[U] once per batch and filter candidates with a fast membership check.

    This gives:

    -O(1) check per candidate
    -Memory bounded per user
    -Natural sliding window from TTL and ring buffer eviction

    Continued below 👇

  • 20 Quant Companies Ranked by Pay Scale From 80 LPA CTC To 5Cr+:
  • ➤ 5Cr+ CTC potential (Numbers sourced from levels(dot)fyi)
  • Jane Street
  • Hudson River Trading
  • Citadel Securities
    Jump Trading
    XTX Markets

    ➤ 3Cr to 5Cr potential
    Two Sigma
    D. E. Shaw
    Optiver
    IMC Trading
    DRW

    ➤ 1.5Cr to 3Cr potential
    Tower Research
    Akuna Capital
    SIG
    Millennium
    WorldQuant

    ➤ 80L to 1.5Cr potential
    Graviton Research
    NK Securities
    Alphagrep
    Quadeye
    Trexquant

    The pay scale is insane.
    But so is the work.

    HFT and quant firms do not pay this kind of money because you can solve 500 LeetCode questions.

    The core skills are very different from normal software roles:

    a) Strong DSA and problem solving
    b) Low-level programming and performance thinking
    c) Probability, statistics, and mental math
    d) System design for low latency systems
    e) Databases, networking, concurrency, and distributed systems
    f) Ability to debug fast when real money is moving

    If you are serious about high-paying engineering roles, system design cannot be an afterthought anymore.

    If you're preparing for system design interviews or learning for the first time check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    There's a full list ready for you to get started.

    Quant pay is crazy.
    But the bar is crazier.

  • Feed post number 10

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1w •
  • 2021: 500 LeetCode Qs solved, 20 SD Questions - 50+ LPA CTC.
  • 2026: 1000 LeetCode Qs solved, 450+ job applications, 30+ SD mock interviews - you might get 2 or 3 interview calls.
  • The hiring bar has moved. Raw problem count does not guarantee anything anymore because companies are no longer testing only “can you solve?”

  • Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    Here is how I would prepare now:

    [1] DSA + LLD: Start building implementation depth.

    DSA is still important because it filters for problem-solving speed.

    The advantage is being able to take a messy prompt, identify the pattern, write clean code, explain tradeoffs, and handle follow-up constraints without falling apart.

    Along with DSA, LLD matters a lot more now.

    Can you design clean classes?
    Can you separate responsibilities?
    Can you model entities properly?
    Can you write extensible code?
    Can you explain why your design will not become a mess after 6 months?

    A candidate who solves a graph problem and can also design a clean parking lot, rate limiter, elevator system, notification service, or splitwise-style app will stand out more than someone who only remembers LeetCode templates.

    [2] HLD: Learn systems like they break in real life.

    Interviewers want to know how you think when the system is under stress.

    What happens when traffic spikes?
    What happens when the DB slows down?
    Where do you cache?
    How do you invalidate?
    What needs async processing?
    Where can duplicates happen?
    What needs idempotency?
    How do you rollback safely?
    What metrics will tell you something is broken?

    Anthropic is the biggest examples of this, they have so many outages and these things all get used on a daily basis.

    This is where strong candidates separate themselves. They design for failure, scale, cost, latency, recovery, and operational reality.

    [3] Communication + Product Thinking + AI-enabled shipping

    This is becoming the hidden interview layer.
    Can you explain your thoughts clearly?
    Can you ask better questions before jumping into the solution?
    Can you understand why the product needs this feature?
    Can you push back when the requirement is unclear?
    Can you use AI tools to move faster without blindly trusting the output?

    The best engineers in this market will not just be good coders.

    They will be people who can understand the problem, design the system, communicate the tradeoffs, use AI to speed up execution, and still own the final quality.

    So yes, keep practicing DSA.

    But do not stop there.

    The new bar is:
    DSA for filters.
    LLD for code quality.
    HLD for system thinking.
    Communication for trust.
    Product thinking for direction.
    AI tools for speed.

    Stop counting problems. Start building the kind of judgment that makes companies trust you with large scale systems.

  • Sometimes a backend engineer is just a person

  • refreshing the deploy log

    That moment is the entire reason availability patterns exist.

    Circuit breakers, bulkheads, failover, graceful degradation. every one of them was invented by someone who lived through that exact night.

  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Meta, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Google, you’ll be grilled on SD and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Amazon, they’ll again grill on SD
  • System design is that one skill that levels up your:
  • – pay scale
    – responsibilities
    – impact on the company

    I asked 5+ L5 and equivalent engineers at Uber, Google, Meta, and Microsoft which problems they would ask in a mock interview to candidates if they had to test how well they knew system design.

    This is the list I came up with, grouped into 8 themes, 5 problems each.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI tutor that teaches you all of system design by collaborating with you.

    You can voice chat about your questions, get feedback in real time and improve. You can even learn concepts and practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner.

    1. Social feeds and content platforms

    ↬ Design Twitter
    ↬ Design Instagram
    ↬ Design Facebook’s Newsfeed
    ↬ Design a Platform Like Reddit
    ↬ Design Youtube or Netflix

    2. Messaging and real time collaboration

    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design a Live Comment System
    ↬ Design a Video Conferencing system
    ↬ Design a Collaboration Tool for Team Communication
    ↬ Design Google Doc

    3. E commerce, payments, and logistics

    ↬ Design an E-commerce Service
    ↬ Design Shopify
    ↬ Design an Online Payment Service
    ↬ Design a Digital Wallet
    ↬ Design a Food Delivery Service

    4. Core infra and storage primitives

    ↬ Design a Key Value Store
    ↬ Design a Web Cache
    ↬ Design a Distributed File System
    ↬ Design a Global Content Distribution Network
    ↬ Design a Load Balancer

    5. Events, queues, and rate limiting

    ↬ Design an API Rate Limiter
    ↬ Design a Distributed Messaging System
    ↬ Design a Distributed Counter
    ↬ Design a Task Scheduler
    ↬ Design a Webhook Notification Service

    6. Data, analytics, and logging

    ↬ Design a Web Analytics Tool
    ↬ Design a Metrics Monitoring and Alerting System
    ↬ Design a Log Collection and Analysis System
    ↬ Design an Ad Click Aggregation System
    ↬ Design a Top-K Request Analysis System

    7. Ids, consistency, and coordination

    ↬ Design a Distributed Unique Id Generator
    ↬ Design a Distributed Locking System
    ↬ Design a Resource Allocation Service
    ↬ Design a Distributed Tracing System
    ↬ Design a Database Batch Auditing Service

    8. Boss fight problems

    ↬ Design Uber Backend
    ↬ Design Ticketmaster
    ↬ Design Google Search
    ↬ Design a Real Time Stock Trading Platform
    ↬ Design a Container Orchestration System

    Pick any one from each category and you already have a solid system design interview loop to practice.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2w •
  • Imagine you're interviewing for an SDE-III role at CRED that offers 70 LPA+ CTC. They ask you this question in the system design round to check how well you think as a backend engineer.
  • I've cleared interviews at Flipkart and Google. This is how I'd think about it:
  • At 2,000 TPS, the payment gateway can theoretically clear 600,000 transactions in 5 minutes. The real problem is correctness under retries, failures, gateway limits, and reconciliation.

  • [1] I would never trigger all autopays at midnight

    Even if the gateway allows 2,000 TPS, I should not push the entire load at once.

    I would create scheduled payment jobs before midnight and spread execution over the 6-hour window using a controlled dispatcher.

    For example:
    - create one autopay job per due bill
    - assign execution windows between 12 AM and 6 AM
    - prioritize by due date, bank cutoff, user risk, and historical failure rate
    - throttle outbound calls below gateway limits, maybe 1,500 TPS instead of 2,000 TPS
    - keep buffer for retries and gateway slowness

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    [2] Every payment needs a state machine

    I would model each bill payment as:

    SCHEDULED → PROCESSING → PAYMENT_INITIATED → SUCCESS / FAILED / RETRY_PENDING

    The gateway may charge the card, but our DB update may fail.
    The webhook may arrive before the API response.
    The user may retry manually while autopay is already running.

    So the system needs one source of truth for the payment attempt, not random status updates across services.

    [3] Idempotency is non-negotiable

    Every bill should have a unique idempotency key like:

    user_id + bill_id + due_month

    That key goes to the payment gateway and is also enforced in our database.

    If a worker crashes and retries, the same logical payment cannot create a second charge.
    If the gateway sends duplicate webhooks, we process them once.

    If the user opens the app and clicks “Pay Now,” we check whether an autopay attempt is already active before starting a new one.

    This is how you avoid double charging.

    [4] Retries must be controlled, not emotional

    A failed payment should not be retried instantly by 10 workers.

    I would use retry queues with exponential backoff, jitter, and max attempts.

    Transient failure? Retry.
    Insufficient funds? Mark failed and notify.
    Gateway timeout? Check payment status before retrying.

    In payments, timeout does not mean failure. It means unknown.

    [5] Reconciliation is the safety net

    By 6 AM, I would run reconciliation:
    - gateway success but DB pending
    - DB success but no gateway confirmation
    - duplicate attempts
    - stuck processing states
    - webhook received but not applied

    Any mismatch goes into a repair workflow or manual review queue.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 2w •
  • Anthropic has 454 open roles. They are hiring SWEs at $320K to $405K. And yet, their CEO keeps saying software engineering is going away.
  • The contradiction only exists if you think software engineering means “writing code.”
  • Anthropic is paying engineers who can think through systems.

  • Because when AI writes more code, the skill becomes knowing what system should exist, how it should behave under pressure, and what can break when real users show up.

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can voice chat, get real-time feedback, learn concepts, and practice HLD questions even if you're starting from scratch.

    Here are 35 questions to train that kind of thinking:

    1. How would you prevent double booking in BookMyShow?
    2. How would you make payment retries idempotent?
    3. How would you design Razorpay webhooks at scale?
    4. How would you avoid duplicate order creation?
    5. How would you design rate limits for UPI APIs?

    6. How would you design Instagram’s feed fanout?
    7. When would you choose fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read?
    8. How would you store unread counts in WhatsApp?
    9. How would you handle message ordering in chat?
    10. How would you design “last seen” without overloading writes?

    11. How would you build YouTube video upload processing?
    12. How would you support resumable large file uploads?
    13. How would you design Netflix playback resume?
    14. How would you cache thumbnails globally?
    15. How would you invalidate cache after profile updates?

    16. How would you design search autocomplete for Amazon?
    17. How would you rank search results for freshness?
    18. How would you shard a URL shortener?
    19. How would you avoid short URL collisions?
    20. How would you design Google Docs live collaboration?

    21. How would you handle conflicts in collaborative editing?
    22. How would you design Uber driver location updates?
    23. How would you reduce location write load?
    24. How would you match riders and drivers in real time?
    25. How would you design Swiggy order tracking?

    26. How would you handle restaurant-side failures?
    27. How would you design notification retries?
    28. How would you avoid sending the same email twice?
    29. How would you design a job queue with priorities?
    30. How would you recover stuck background jobs?

    31. How would you design audit logs for fintech?
    32. How would you protect sensitive customer data?
    33. How would you detect a bad deployment quickly?
    34. How would you rollback without losing data?
    35. How would you find the bottleneck when latency spikes?

    AI can generate code for an API.

    But someone still has to decide consistency, retries, caching, queues, sharding, observability, rollback, and failure handling.

    Coding is changing.
    System design is becoming the moat.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 2w •
  • Dear backend engineers, repeat after me:
  • Redis is not a strategy.
  • TTL is not a strategy.
  • "We'll handle it on writes" is not a strategy.

  • A cache without an invalidation plan is just a faster way to ship bugs.

    Senior interviewers know this. That's why "add a cache" never ends the question, it starts it.

    What gets cached? For how long? What happens on a write? What about a hot key? Two services, same record, who wins?

    Fumble these and say bye-bye to the offer. Nail them and you sound like someone who's actually been on-call.

    We've built a structured session that walks you through caching strategies and invalidation patterns step by step (you can block me on LinkedIn if you don't find value in this session)

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Rate limiting sounds like a one-liner.
  • if requests > 100: reject. Done.
  • Then you actually build it and realise:
  • where do you store the counter? Redis?
  • What if Redis is down fail open or fail closed?
    Per-user or per-IP? What about users behind the same NAT?
    Sliding window or fixed? Token bucket or leaky bucket?

    And how do you do any of this across 5 regions without every request becoming a cross-region round trip?

    Every senior interview will poke at this.
    Most people memorise "token bucket" and pray nobody asks a follow-up.

    We've built a structured session that walks you through rate limiting algorithms and the trade-offs that actually come up in interviews, so you stop guessing.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Feed post number 18

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3w •
  • A friend of my colleague from Google, who is based in the US, finished interviews for a SWE Product E5 role at Meta London last year.
  • Sharing his interview experience for everyone preparing right now.
  • Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor: https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

  • You can:
    - voice chat about your questions
    - get feedback in real time and improve
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    1. Phone screen – pure DSA
    45 minutes, classic Meta style

    Q1: 3Sum
    - Solved it cleanly with a clear two-pointer approach
    - Dry ran on examples and called out edge cases
    - Got a follow-up about avoiding duplicates, and he explained the idea, but did not fully implement the follow-up

    Q2: Simplify Path style problem
    - Variant of canonical path simplification, current directory plus `cd`
    - He struggled at the start, asked plenty of clarifying questions
    - With the interviewer’s nudges, he got to the optimal stack based solution and dry ran it

    This was still enough to clear the screen, mainly because of clarity, communication and his willingness to ask questions instead of guessing.

    2. Onsite Coding Round 1

    Q1: Merge Sorted Array
    - Straightforward, did in place merging from the end
    - No hints, clean dry run, good variable naming and explanation

    Q2: Diameter of Binary Tree
    - Used DFS with height calculation and a global max diameter
    - Again, no hints needed, walked through tree examples and explained why the solution is O(n)

    3. Onsite Coding Round 2

    Q1: Sliding window average
    - Given a list of integers, return list of averages for window size k
    - He reached the optimal sliding window solution quickly
    - No hints, careful dry run, spoke through overflow and edge cases

    Q2: Missing Element in Sorted Array

    - Had seen it before but forgot the optimal approach
    - Spent time asking questions, then realised sorted input and that binary search is the right tool
    - While coding, a few bugs popped up during dry run
    - Interviewer gave a hint, he fixed it, but the clock ran out

    4. Product Architecture / System Design

    Prompt: Design Dropbox or Google Drive
    - Interviewer pushed on synchronization between multiple machines
    - He walked through trade offs between
    - Request response
    - Server sent events
    - WebSockets
    - Talked about eventual consistency and conflict resolution at a high level

    5. Behavioral Round 1 and 2

    - Two separate behavioral interviews, 45 minutes each
    - Recruiter hinted that one might be a training round for the interviewer, but did not say which
    - He went in with clear stories mapped to ownership, conflict, failure, and cross team work
    - Both rounds felt like natural conversations
    - They asked fewer follow ups
    - He could tie back every story to impact and learning

    His guess is both rounds landed well from a signal point of view, but he will only know for sure at the end of team match.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 3w •
  • After working at Google and Flipkart for the last 6 years of my career, and building a system design learning platform with 30,000+ users. This is how I'd frame the answer:
  • [1] Separate "restaurant discovery" from "restaurant truth"
  • The first mistake people make is treating search and availability as the same problem.

  • They are not.

    When you search, the system is trying to answer:
    - which restaurants are relevant for this user and location
    - which ones should rank higher
    - which ones look open and serviceable right now

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    That path must be extremely fast.

    But the final truth for ordering is a different question:
    - is this restaurant still accepting orders
    - is this dish available
    - can this order actually be placed right now

    That second check happens later and more strictly.

    [2] Search results under 500ms come from denormalized read models

    The restaurant listing page is usually served from a read-optimized layer such as:
    - a search index
    - geo-indexed store
    - Redis / in-memory cache
    - precomputed restaurant documents

    Each document already contains most of what the UI needs:
    - restaurant id
    - name, rating, ETA, delivery fee
    - cuisine, menu summary
    - open / closed hint
    - serviceability zone
    - popularity signals

    So the search path is usually:

    location → geo filter → ranking → top 100 or 200 docs

    That is not 200 random DB round trips.

    It is usually 1 query to a search-serving layer, or a small number of shard queries merged together.

    [3] Availability is usually pushed into cache, not pulled 200 times from DB

    During lunch rush, restaurant status changes constantly.

    You do not want every search request to ask 200 restaurant databases: "Are you open now?"

    Instead, restaurant systems emit events like:
    - store opened / paused / closed
    - kitchen overloaded
    - item unavailable
    - delivery temporarily disabled

    Those updates flow through Kafka or similar pipelines into a fast serving layer.

    Now the search service can read availability from cached state or from fields already embedded in the search document.

    So for 200 restaurants, it is often:
    - 1 search query
    - plus cached availability filters
    - plus maybe some batched shard-local fetches

    Not 200 separate OLTP reads.

    [4] Why this works

    Because the listing page only needs fresh enough truth, not perfect truth.

    If one restaurant closed 3 seconds ago and the listing still shows it briefly, that is acceptable.

    But when the user taps "Place Order," the system does a stricter revalidation against the source of truth. That is where correctness matters more than speed.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 3w •
  • Half the internet went down when AWS had a DNS failure in us-east-1.
  • Half the internet got disrupted when Cloudflare went down last year.
  • Snapchat, Atlassian, Slack, Coinbase, Reddit, and many other services were affected because one cloud region’s DNS problem became everyone’s production problem.
  • Even AWS services like DynamoDB became hard to reach because clients could not reliably resolve the right service endpoints.

    DNS is the first dependency of almost every request.

    Before your API gateway.
    Before your load balancer.
    Before your database.
    Before your cache.

    The browser first asks: “Where is this service?”
    And DNS answers with an IP address.

    A simple request looks like this:
    1. User types     app.com
    2. Browser checks DNS cache
    3. Resolver finds the authoritative server
    4. DNS returns the IP
    5. TCP, TLS, and HTTP begin

    This is why DNS matters in system design interviews.

    If DNS is slow, your app feels slow.
    If DNS is misconfigured, your app disappears.
    If TTL is too high, failover becomes painful.
    If TTL is too low, resolvers get hammered.

    System Design starts here. Most candidates jump straight to Kafka, Redis, Kubernetes, and microservices.

    But the real request starts before all of that.
    It starts with DNS.

    I’ve built a structured session that walks you through system design fundamentals step by step, so you can be done with these core topics once and for all and never get confused again.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Feed post number 21

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3w •
  • WhatsApp serves 2 billion users by optimizing for tiny, millisecond-fast messages. Anthropic is building for the exact opposite. They handle single high end requests that stay open for 30+ seconds and cost $0.25 - $0.50 each in compute power.
  • Anthropic is not testing system design like a lazy FAANG clone. They want to see if you can build when the constraints are expensive and the requirements keep changing and do all of that at full speed.
  • If you are prepping for Anthropic level, keep 3 things in mind. Btw, to help you master all the fundamental building blocks of system design, check out our AI Tutor: layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • -pick a topic, practice from a real-life use case
    - ask questions with voice chat, literally ask why Kafka and why not
    - get detailed feedback right when the session ends.

    [1] Prioritize compute density over user traffic

    In a standard interview, you worry about how many users hit your database. At Anthropic, the bottleneck is the GPU memory and the cost of inference.

    - You need to understand how data flows to a model, not just how it sits in a cache.
    - Every architectural choice has a massive price tag attached to it.
    - If your design doubles the latency, you are burning millions of dollars in compute time.

    They want to see if you understand the hardware limits. If you treat a GPU cluster like a standard web server, it shows you haven't done the math.

    [2] Choose simplicity over engineering flexes

    I’ve seen many engineers fail because they try to show off. They draw 15 microservices for a problem that could be solved with a simple script.

    - Complexity is the enemy of safety.
    - The person who suggests the simplest, most boring solution often wins.

    Ask yourself: "Does this feature actually need this level of scale right now?" If the answer is no, do not build it.

    [3] Solve for changing constraints in real-time

    AI moves faster than any other sector in tech. Your interviewer will likely change the constraints halfway through the round.

    - "What if the model size triples tomorrow?"
    - "What if we lose access to this specific cloud provider?"
    - "How does your design change if inference becomes 10x cheaper?"

    They aren't looking for the "right" diagram because that diagram might be obsolete in three months. They are looking for your reasoning process under pressure.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 4w •
  • Back-of-the-envelope estimation cheat sheet for system design interviews
  • Memory
  • - 1 KB = 10³ bytes
  • - 1 MB = 10⁶ bytes
  • - 1 GB = 10⁹ bytes
    - 1 TB = 10¹² bytes
    - 1 ms = 10⁻³ s
    - 1 day = 86.4K s
    - 100 QPS = 8.64M req/day
    - 1K QPS = 86.4M req/day
    - 10K QPS = 864M req/day

    Latency
    - RAM: ~100 ns
    - SSD: ~100 μs
    - Network hop: ~1 ms
    - Disk seek: ~10 ms

    System Design starts here. If you cannot estimate traffic, storage, and latency quickly, you will miss bottlenecks before you even draw the first box.

    If you’ve built systems or sat through system design rounds, you already know: these small calculations decide big architecture choices.

    Yet most people never actually get good at it, they just guess and face problems.

    We’ve built a structured session that walks you through back-of-the-envelope estimations step by step, so you can be done with this topic once and for all and never get confused ever.

    Try it here:     layrs.me/tutor-topics

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • OpenAI is offering $400k - $800k total compensation for Senior SWE roles right now, and yet the interview-to-offer rate is at an all-time low.
  • If you are targeting a role at OpenAI, understand that these interviews go way beyond the usual database scaling or SQL vs. NoSQL debates. You are playing a different game here.
  • I’ve been looking into their recent interview loops, and here are three things you need to change in your prep right now:

  • [1] The system design evaluation starts early

    In a typical big tech loop, you might get a dedicated system design round in the 4th or 5th interview. At OpenAI, high-level architectural thinking often leaks into the very first technical screen.

    - You won't just write a function.
    - You'll be asked how that function behaves inside a distributed inference pipeline.
    - You’ll have to explain the cost and latency trade-offs of your logic immediately.

    They aren't looking for someone who can just "make it work." They want to see if you understand how your code impacts the entire cluster's resources from the first minute.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get live feedback and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are just starting out

    [2] Product behavior is part of the infrastructure

    In traditional backend roles, "system design" usually means keeping the site up. For OpenAI, it means managing behavior that isn't always predictable.

    - How do you handle a model that gives different answers to the same question?
    - How do you design a cache that works for semantic similarity instead of exact keys?
    - What happens to the user experience when the LLM takes 10 seconds to respond?

    If you spend 40 minutes talking about Kafka partitions but forget to mention token limits or context window management, you’ve already lost the room.

    [3] Prepare for the "Mutation Test"

    OpenAI interviewers love to change the constraints of the problem while you are halfway through drawing your diagram. This isn't to annoy you; it’s to see if your design is actually adaptable.

    - "What if the model size triples tomorrow?"
    - "What if we move from batch processing to real-time streaming for this feature?"
    - "How does your design change if we lose 30% of our GPU capacity?"

    They need to know that when the technology shifts (which happens every week in AI), you won't have to rewrite your entire architecture from scratch.

    The market is shifting toward these AI-native engineering roles. The compensation is massive, but the bar for how you think about systems is much higher.

    Move past the "backend plumber" mindset. You need to think like an architect who manages systems that aren't always predictable.

    The models might be messy, but your engineering logic shouldn't be.

  • Feed post number 26

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • A username box looks like a tiny frontend detail. You type, Sameer. Gmail says: “That username is taken.”
  • Looks simple. But at Google scale, this is not a database lookup problem. It is a “how do we stop billions of noisy, half-finished checks from touching the database” problem.
  • If I were designing this, I would think about it in layers.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get live feedback and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are just starting out

    [1] The frontend should reduce noise before the backend ever sees it

    When user types sameer123, the system should not check:
    s
    sa
    sam
    same
    samee
    sameer

    Most of those are useless intermediate states. So the client waits for a short pause, maybe 300 to 500 ms, before sending the availability check. If the user keeps typing, the timer resets.

    This one small debounce rule can save millions of unnecessary requests. A lot of expensive backend optimization starts with one boring frontend decision: stop asking the server questions the user has not finished asking yet.

    [2] Normalize the username before it becomes a cache key

    Before the backend checks anything, it should clean the input. Sameer, sameer, sameer , and SAMEER should collapse into one normalized value.

    The same goes for invalid characters, reserved words, blocked names, and length rules. This matters because caching only works when identical things look identical to the system.

    If normalization is weak, your cache gets fragmented, your hit rate drops, and your database starts getting hit for things the system should have answered much earlier.

    [3] The database should be the last read path, not the first

    Once the request reaches the backend, I would check cheaper layers first.

    For example:
    - recently checked usernames in cache
    - reserved usernames like admin, support, billing
    - negative cache for recently available names
    - Bloom filter for fast existence checks

    A Bloom filter is useful because it can quickly say: “This username definitely does not exist.”

    If it says the name may exist, then you do a stronger lookup. That means a huge number of unique signup attempts can skip the main database completely.

    [4] The final lookup should be narrow and indexed

    When the system finally checks persistent storage, it should only ask one question: “Does normalized username sameer exist?”

    It should hit a dedicated index on the username field. It should not fetch the user profile, preferences, devices, photos, sessions, or anything unrelated.

    The fastest database query is usually the one that asks the smallest possible question against the right index.

    Continued ↓

  • Feed post number 27

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • You are in a system design interview for a L5 Sr. Backend role at Amazon. and get asked:
  • “Your app has 1 million users. Every morning at exactly 9:00 AM, the server crashes. Nothing obvious is wrong. The dashboards are green. What do you check first?”
  • Companies love this question because it exposes how engineers think under ambiguity.

  • The junior answer is usually:
    - “Add more servers.”
    - “Check database logs.”
    - “Maybe autoscaling is slow.”

    But if everything breaks at exactly 9:00 AM, this is probably not a normal scaling problem.

    It is a synchronization problem.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get live feedback and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are just starting out


    [1] First, I would stop trusting the dashboard blindly

    Dashboards can lie when the aggregation window is too wide.

    If CPU, memory, latency, and error rate are averaged over 1 minute, a 5-second spike can disappear inside a normal-looking chart.

    The system may look healthy at 9:01, while it was completely melting from 9:00:00 to 9:00:07.

    So I would immediately check:

    - p99 and p999 latency
    - request rate per second, not per minute
    - connection pool usage
    - queue depth
    - rejected requests
    - thread pool saturation
    - error logs around 8:59:55 to 9:00:10

    The first job is to zoom into the exact failure window.

    [2] Then I would look for scheduled work

    Organic users rarely behave with millisecond precision.

    Machines do.

    If a crash happens at exactly 9:00 AM every day, I would look for cron jobs, batch pipelines, daily reports, cache warmers, billing jobs, notification jobs, analytics exports, or background workers waking up at the same time.

    The dangerous part is that each job may look harmless alone.

    But 40 workers starting together, all reading the same tables, all opening DB connections, all calling the same downstream service, can create a very short but violent spike.

    That spike may not show up as “high average CPU.”

    It may show up as:

    - DB connection exhaustion
    - lock contention
    - timeout storms
    - retry amplification
    - worker queue backlog

    [3] Cache expiry would be my next suspect

    A very common failure pattern is this:

    A heavy query is cached for 24 hours.
    The cache was created yesterday at 9:00 AM.
    Today at 9:00 AM, it expires for everyone.

    Now thousands of requests miss the cache at the same time and hit the database directly.

    That is a thundering herd.

    The server may not be crashing because traffic increased.
    It may be crashing because the cache stopped absorbing traffic for a few seconds.

    The fix is usually not “add more servers.”

    The fix is:

    - add TTL jitter
    - use stale-while-revalidate
    - use request coalescing
    - warm critical caches before peak time
    - protect the database with backpressure

    Continued ↓

  • Feed post number 30

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • You’re in a Google L5 Level interview. The interviewer draws a 5GB file on the whiteboard and says: "The user is at 49%. Their internet drops. How do we resume the upload without starting from zero?"
  • The interviewer wants to know how you resume the process without losing a single byte.
  • If you've only ever worked with high-level libraries, this is a nightmare scenario. You have to move past the API calls and design a state machine that tracks 5MB chunks across a broken connection.

  • Here is the architecture for a resumable upload system.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:    https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get feedback in real time and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are a complete beginner

    [1] Chunking the payload

    A 5GB file should never be sent as one giant stream. If the pipe breaks at 4.9GB, the bandwidth of the user and the server is gone.

    - The client slices the file into pieces (5MB to 10MB each).
    - Each piece gets a sequence number and a unique Upload ID.
    - This allows for parallel uploads and ensures that a single 10MB failure does not kill the session.

    [2] Managing the session state

    The backend needs to track which pieces of the puzzle it already has. This state usually sits in a fast store like Redis.

    - When the upload starts, the backend generates a Session ID.
    - As chunks arrive, the backend marks those sequence numbers as Received.
    - If the internet drops, the client queries the backend: "I have Session ID 123. Which chunks are missing?"

    [3] Bypassing the application server

    Processing 5GB of raw data through your app server is expensive. It slows down every other request and consumes memory.

    - Your backend generates pre-signed URLs for each chunk.
    - The client uploads these chunks directly to S3 or GCS.
    - The storage provider handles the data ingestion while your server only manages the metadata.

    This keeps the infrastructure lean and lets specialized cloud services handle the transfer.

    [4] Checksums and integrity

    Network noise can corrupt bits during a transfer. You have to ensure the file on the server is bit-for-bit identical to the local one.

    - The client calculates an MD5 checksum for every chunk before sending it.
    - The backend verifies that checksum as soon as the chunk arrives.
    - If it does not match, the chunk is discarded immediately so the client can retry.

    [5] Final assembly

    Once all chunks are stored, the client sends a Finalize signal.

    - The backend triggers a worker to stitch the chunks into a single object.
    - In S3, this is a Multipart Upload Complete call.
    - The worker verifies the final hash and cleans up the temporary chunks.

    Code works when the network is stable. The architecture matters once the user walks into an elevator and the 4G signal dies.

    Reliability is built for the moments the connection falls apart.

  • Feed post number 31

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • Google handles over 8.5 billion searches every single day. For every letter you type, they have less than 30 milliseconds to predict what you are thinking.
  • To put that in perspective, a human blink takes about 100 milliseconds. Google is predicting your search three times faster than you can blink.
  • At this scale, even the fastest SQL index would crawl to a halt. That’s why what you’re really building here is a high-concurrency, low-latency prediction engine.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:    https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get feedback in real time and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are a complete beginner

    [1] The core data structure

    A normal search index is too heavy for keystroke-level latency. The usual starting point is a Trie, but in production I would say a compressed Trie or FST-style structure.

    The important idea is the same:

    - lookup cost depends on prefix length, not total corpus size
    - each prefix node stores the top K suggestions
    - reads stay in memory, not on disk

    That turns “find suggestions for `interv`” into a fast memory lookup instead of a ranking job on every keystroke.

    [2] Ranking should be built offline, not computed live

    The read path must stay boring and fast.

    So query logs, click-through data, freshness, geography, and trend signals should be processed in a background pipeline. That pipeline periodically rebuilds or updates prefix weights and publishes a new in-memory serving snapshot.

    This is the key tradeoff:
    you accept a little staleness so the serving path stays extremely fast and predictable.

    [3] The real latency battle is network, not code

    Even a perfect backend loses if every keystroke crosses an ocean.

    So I would combine:

    - client-side debouncing
    - browser caching for common prefixes
    - edge POPs or region-local replicas
    - prefetching likely next prefixes

    That way, many lookups are either local, edge-served, or at least region-close.

    [4] The gaps most candidates miss

    A good answer should also cover:

    - Hot prefixes
    “a”, “i”, “news”, “weather” get hammered. Replicate popular prefix shards aggressively.

    - Personalization
    Global popularity is not enough. Rank should blend global signals with language, location, recent history, and device context.

    - Safe rollout
    Build new snapshots offline, validate them, then atomically swap serving versions. Never mutate the live structure blindly.

    - Fallback behavior
    If the freshest ranking snapshot is delayed, serve the last good snapshot. Typeahead can be slightly stale. It cannot be slow.

  • 40 niche system design problems that will teach you 80% of the important concepts that keep repeating across system design interviews at Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, Uber and other top companies.
  • System design gets a lot easier once you know the fundamentals. It gets even better when you learn them by solving problems and seeing how pieces fit together.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor: https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

  • You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get live feedback and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are just starting out

    [1] Set 1 – Request and data flow

    - Design a URL shortener API
    ➸ Client server model, REST APIs, reverse proxy, load balancer

    - Design a file upload service for web and mobile
    ➸ REST APIs, API gateway, rate limiting

    - Design internal RPC calls between microservices
    ➸ gRPC, service registry, load balancer

    - Design a public partner API platform
    ➸ API gateway, authentication, rate limiting

    - Design an edge layer for mobile clients across regions
    ➸ Reverse proxy, load balancer, request routing

    - Design service discovery inside a Kubernetes cluster
    ➸ Service registry, health checks, client server

    - Design a multi version backend for old app clients
    ➸ API gateway, routing, backward compatibility

    - Design throttling for a “send OTP” endpoint
    ➸ Rate limiting, API gateway, reverse proxy

    [2] Set 2 – Storage and data modeling

    - Design user profile storage for 500M users
    ➸ Relational databases, indexes, normalization

    - Design a product catalogue with flexible attributes
    ➸ NoSQL databases, document modeling, secondary indexes

    - Design a sharded order database for an ecommerce app
    ➸ Sharding, data partitioning, relational DB

    - Design read heavy analytics views on top of OLTP data
    ➸ Read write splitting, replication, materialized views

    - Design cross region replication for a payments ledger
    ➸ Replication, consistency, distributed transactions

    - Design an audit log that never loses events
    ➸ Append only tables, event sourcing, retention strategy

    - Design a time series store for metrics
    ➸ Partitioning by time, indexes, cold vs hot storage

    - Design soft delete and restore for user generated content
    ➸ Data modeling, secondary indexes, retention policies

    [3] Set 3 – Caching and performance

    - Design caching for a product detail page
    ➸ In memory cache, cache aside pattern

    - Design a trending feed that updates every few minutes
    ➸ Cache aside, write through cache, TTL strategy

    - Design a “recently viewed items” widget
    ➸ In memory cache, eviction policy, pagination

    - Design CDN support for image and video delivery
    ➸ Content delivery network, origin shield, cache keys

    - Design rate limited search suggestions
    ➸ In memory cache, connection pooling, back pressure

    - Design leaderboard queries at scale
    ➸ Sorted set cache, pagination, cache aside

  • Feed post number 33

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Uber. The interviewer leans in and asks: “How do you guarantee a real-time notification eventually reaches a device that is currently offline?”
  • A lot of candidates immediately say, “I would use APNs or Firebase.”
  • That is only part of the answer. Push providers help you reach devices.

  • They do not guarantee your business event survives long enough to reach a device that was offline, unreachable, or temporarily disconnected.

    That is the actual design problem.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real time
    - get live feedback and improve fast
    - learn concepts and practice HLD even if you are just starting out

    Here is how I would think about the architecture.

    [1] Persist before you push

    The first rule is simple: Never treat the push provider as your source of truth.

    The notification should first be written to durable storage or a reliable queue. That gives you a record that this event exists, who it belongs to, which devices are targets, and how long it is still useful.

    I would store:
    - user ID
    - device tokens
    - payload or payload reference
    - status
    - creation time
    - TTL / expiry

    That way, “device is offline” becomes a delivery state, not a lost event.

    [2] Separate event creation from delivery

    Creating a notification and delivering it should be two different steps.

    One service creates the notification.
    Another service consumes pending notifications and attempts delivery through APNs or FCM.

    Why does this matter? Because once delivery is decoupled, retries become safe and controlled.

    Your write path stays fast, and your delivery path can handle outages, rate limits, and transient failures without blocking the business action that created the notification.

    [3] Retry carefully, and make duplicates harmless

    Mobile networks fail all the time.
    Push providers can reject temporarily.
    Devices reconnect late.

    So retries are normal.

    But retries without idempotency create duplicate alerts and bad UX.

    Every notification should carry a unique idempotency key.
    The server uses it to avoid duplicate scheduling.
    The client uses it to ignore the same notification if it arrives twice.

    I would also use exponential backoff with limits, because retrying aggressively during an outage just turns a failure into a storm.

    [4] Handle offline comeback intelligently

    If a phone reconnects after hours offline, sending 40 old alerts one by one is rarely the right experience.

    The system should coalesce pending messages when possible.

    Instead of pushing every single event, it can send:
    “You have 15 new updates”

    That reduces device load, improves UX, and avoids a reconnect spike turning into a notification flood.

  • Feed post number 35

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • 4.3 billion IPv4 addresses. 8.1 billion people. Over 20 billion connected devices. Mathematically, the internet should have died 10 years ago, yet you’re reading this post without a hitch.
  • The reality is that we’ve built layers of abstraction to hide the fact that we are technically out of space. Here is the breakdown:
  • [1] Private Address Spaces

  • We don't give every device a unique public identity. Instead, we reuse the same "private" ranges (like 192.168.x.x) millions of times across different local networks.

    - Your home IP is likely the same as your neighbor's home IP.
    - These addresses are only meaningful within your own four walls.
    - They are completely invisible to the public internet.

    This simple reuse meant we didn't need a billion IPs for a billion lightbulbs. We just needed a few million "gateways" to manage them.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real-time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    [2] Network Address Translation (NAT)

    NAT is the traffic cop that makes the private-public transition work. It allows an entire office building or an apartment complex to hide behind a single public IPv4 address.

    - It maps internal private IPs to a single external public IP.
    - It uses "Port Mapping" to remember which device asked for what data.
    - It acts as a basic security layer by hiding internal network structures.

    When a packet comes back from a server, NAT looks at the port number and knows exactly which laptop in the building to send it to.

    [3] Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT)

    This is where things get aggressive. Your ISP (Internet Service Provider) doesn't even give your house a public IP anymore. They put thousands of households behind a single public IP.

    - It adds another layer of translation at the provider level.
    - It’s effectively "NAT 2.0" to squeeze every last drop out of the IPv4 pool.
    - It can occasionally break things like port forwarding or online gaming.

    If you’ve ever wondered why you can’t host a web server from your home connection easily, CGNAT is usually the reason.

    [4] IPv6 Transition

    We are finally moving to a 128-bit system, which provides 340 undecillion addresses. That is enough to give every atom on earth its own IP address.

    - It eliminates the need for complex NAT hacks.
    - It simplifies routing and improves packet processing.
    - Adoption is slow because it requires a complete hardware and software overhaul.

    We didn't solve the scarcity problem by having more resources. We solved it by becoming incredibly efficient at sharing the ones we already had..

  • Feed post number 36

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • Netflix manages 260 Million users while ensuring that not a single frame of its $100 Billion library ever leaks into your computer's main memory.
  • If you tried to scrape your RAM to find a movie frame, you’d find nothing but encrypted junk. This is a hardware-level lockdown by design.
  • This is how Netflix doesn’t let you take screenshots while playing a movie..

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    [1] Digital Rights Management

    Netflix sends an encrypted stream that requires a specific hardware key to unlock. This is handled by systems like Widevine or FairPlay.

    - The decryption keys never touch your main OS.
    - Decryption happens inside a "Trusted Execution Environment" (TEE).
    - Your browser's regular memory never sees the raw, decrypted data.

    The TEE is essentially a secure vault inside your processor. Since your Operating System can't peek into that vault, it can't hand the pixels over to a screenshot tool.

    [2] The Secure Video Path

    In a standard application, the CPU decodes a video and stores the pixels in RAM. In a Secure Video Path, the CPU is bypassed entirely for the actual frames.

    - Encrypted data goes straight to a protected part of the GPU.
    - The GPU decodes the frames in a locked memory buffer.
    - Those frames are sent directly to the display controller.

    When your screenshot software asks the OS for the screen content, the OS checks that protected region and finds nothing it’s allowed to share. It returns zeros. You get to see the movie, but your software sees a void.

    [3] The HDCP Handshake

    What if you try to bypass the OS with an external HDMI capture card? Netflix uses the HDCP protocol to secure the physical cable.

    - Your laptop "talks" to your monitor before the first frame plays.
    - They establish an encrypted link between the HDMI ports.
    - If the monitor or the cable can't prove it’s a "safe" device, the stream is blocked.

    This is why cheap HDMI splitters often fail. If the "chain of trust" is broken at the port level, Netflix drops you to 480p or kills the stream entirely.

    [4] Deterrence vs Perfection

    No system is unhackable. Professional pirates use specialized hardware to bypass these checks every day.

    But for 99% of users, these hardware boundaries are enough to stop casual recording. High-scale security is really about making the "cost" of the hack higher than the reward.

  • A Senior Engineer at Amazon in India (L6) has 1Cr+ CTC
  • A Senior Engineer at Microsoft in India (L64) has 1Cr+ CTC
  • A Senior Engineer at Google in India (L5) has 1Cr+ CTC
  • Tech salaries at the higher levels are absolutely crazy, but so is the standards and hiring bar for these roles. (Salary numbers source: Levels(dot)fyi app)

  • If you're beginning to learn system design, I would say the best way to do it is learn by doing and keep it practical. You can also check out our AI tutor feature on Layrs as you're learning:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    With this, you can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    Here's a list of 75 questions to test yourself and being your learning with:

    [1] Consumer & Social Platforms
    • Design a URL shortener
    • Design a pastebin/service for sharing text files
    • Design Twitter (feed/timeline)
    • Design Instagram (photo sharing)
    • Design YouTube (video streaming)
    • Design a chat application (1:1 and group)
    • Design a real-time collaborative editor
    • Design an online multiplayer game backend
    • Design a ride-hailing service (like Uber)
    • Design a delivery system (logistics)
    • Design a restaurant reservation system
    • Design an e-commerce website
    • Design a photo storage service with thumbnails
    • Design a document collaboration platform (Google Docs-like)
    • Design a system to stream live video
    • Design a user activity feed with personalization
    • Design a polling system for online surveys
    • Design a scalable comment system

    [2] Search, Feed & Recommendation Systems
    • Design a scalable web crawler
    • Design a search engine (basic)
    • Design autocomplete for search
    • Design a recommendation system (basic)
    • Design a full-text search service (e.g., for documents)
    • Design a distributed search for social feed
    • Design a graph-based social network recommendation engine
    • Design a service for geospatial queries (nearby search)

    [3] Core Infrastructure & Distributed Systems
    • Design rate limiter for APIs
    • Design a distributed cache
    • Design a key-value store
    • Design a relational database at scale
    • Design a time-series database
    • Design a message queue system (like Kafka)
    • Design a task scheduler (cron at scale)
    • Design a content delivery network (CDN)
    • Design a distributed lock service
    • Design a file upload service at scale
    • Design a URL routing layer for microservices
    • Design a distributed graph storage
    • Design a backup and restore system
    • Design a distributed configuration service
    • Design a service mesh for microservices
    • Design an event sourcing system
    • Design a CQRS-based system for high-write workloads
    • Design a distributed transaction coordinator
    • Design a global database with low latency
    • Design a schema-less document store
    • Design a system to handle large file diffs (e.g., git hosting)

  • Feed post number 38

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • After working at Google and Flipkart for the last 6 years of my career, and building a system design learning platform with 30,000+ users. This is how I’d frame the answer:
  • [1] Separate “restaurant discovery” from “restaurant truth”
  • The first mistake people make is treating search and availability as the same problem.

  • They are not.

    When you search, the system is trying to answer:
    - which restaurants are relevant for this user and location
    - which ones should rank higher
    - which ones look open and serviceable right now

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    That path must be extremely fast.

    But the final truth for ordering is a different question:

    - is this restaurant still accepting orders
    - is this dish available
    - can this order actually be placed right now

    That second check happens later and more strictly.

    [2] Search results under 500 ms come from denormalized read models

    The restaurant listing page is usually served from a read-optimized layer such as:

    - a search index
    - geo-indexed store
    - Redis / in-memory cache
    - precomputed restaurant documents

    Each document already contains most of what the UI needs:

    - restaurant id
    - name, rating, ETA, delivery fee
    - cuisine, menu summary
    - open / closed hint
    - serviceability zone
    - popularity signals

    So the search path is usually:

    location → geo filter → ranking → top 100 or 200 docs

    That is not 200 random DB round trips.
    It is usually 1 query to a search-serving layer, or a small number of shard queries merged together.

    [3] Availability is usually pushed into cache, not pulled 200 times from DB

    During lunch rush, restaurant status changes constantly.

    You do not want every search request to ask 200 restaurant databases:
    “Are you open now?”

    Instead, restaurant systems emit events like:

    - store opened / paused / closed
    - kitchen overloaded
    - item unavailable
    - delivery temporarily disabled

    Those updates flow through Kafka or similar pipelines into a fast serving layer.

    Now the search service can read availability from cached state or from fields already embedded in the search document.

    So for 200 restaurants, it is often:

    - 1 search query
    - plus cached availability filters
    - plus maybe some batched shard-local fetches

    Not 200 separate OLTP reads.

    [4] Why this works

    Because the listing page only needs fresh enough truth, not perfect truth.

    If one restaurant closed 3 seconds ago and the listing still shows it briefly, that is acceptable.

    But when the user taps “Place Order,” the system does a stricter revalidation against the source of truth.

    That is where correctness matters more than speed.

  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • Imagine you’re in a system design interview at Google for an L5 role, and the interviewer asks: “If 10M users hit your API at the same time and your rate limiter allows 1000 req/sec, what happens to the other 9.99M?”
  • This is a classic overload-control + retry-amplification problem.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor: https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

  • You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    Here is how I would break it down.

    [1] Clarify what we actually need to build

    This is not just “return 429 when over the limit.”

    It is:
    - protect the backend from overload
    - keep latency stable for the requests we do accept
    - avoid retry storms from rejected clients
    - give clients a fair chance to recover
    - degrade gracefully instead of turning 10M requests into 20M

    So the core problem is not only rate limiting. It is admission control plus controlled recovery behavior.

    [2] The other 9.99M cannot all get immediate retries

    If all rejected requests get a 429 and retry immediately, the limiter becomes part of the problem.

    A better model is:

    - accept up to the allowed rate
    - reject excess traffic quickly
    - return backoff hints like `Retry-After`
    - force clients and SDKs to use exponential backoff + jitter
    - optionally queue a small bounded overflow only if the business case justifies it

    The key idea is simple: do not turn rejection into amplification.

    [3] High-level flow

    A reasonable design would be:

    - clients hit edge load balancers / API gateway
    - request first passes through a distributed rate limiter
    - accepted requests move to the backend
    - rejected requests get a fast 429 or graceful degradation response
    - clients retry later using backoff, not instantly
    - observability layer tracks rejection rate, retry rate, queue depth, and user impact

    The limiter is only one part. The client behavior matters just as much.

    [4] What should happen to the rejected traffic?

    This depends on the API.

    For example:

    - interactive read APIs: reject fast, retry later
    - write APIs: maybe accept into a bounded queue if loss is costly
    - idempotent operations: safer to retry
    - non-critical traffic: drop or degrade early
    - premium / internal traffic: separate priority buckets

    So the answer is not “all 9.99M get blocked.”
    The answer is “different classes of traffic may be handled differently.”

    [5] The tradeoffs interviewers care about

    This is where the answer gets interesting:

    - immediate 429 is cheap, but dangerous if clients retry badly
    - queues smooth bursts, but can increase latency and memory pressure
    - token bucket handles bursts better than a strict per-second counter
    - fairness matters so one tenant or region does not starve everyone else
    - backoff with jitter is critical to avoid synchronized retries
    - if the limiter itself fails, fail-open vs fail-closed depends on the API

  • Feed post number 41

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • You solved 2 Leetcode medium questions in under 40 minutes in your first interview after applying for months. Graphs. DP. Good enough.
  • Then in the system design round, you came up with a great design, explained a few tradeoffs, handled the discussion reasonably well, and still got a NO HIRE.
  • That feels brutal. But it also explains the market we are in right now.

  • The interview bar has moved higher.

    For a long time, one DSA sheet and designing CRUD apps was enough.

    Now, interviews are also checking: Can I trust how this person thinks when the problem is unclear?

    They do in this interview itself by various methods:
    1. They change one important constraint
    2. They ask why you chose this approach over another
    3. They ask what breaks first under scale
    4. They ask what happens when one dependency slows down or fails
    5. They ask you to simplify, prioritize, or recover from bad assumptions

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real-time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    A candidate with surface-level prep can often get to the first answer. The problem starts after that.

    3 layers in your preparation everyone should add right now:

    [1] Depth of understanding

    For many people, doing fewer problems but understanding them much better gives better returns.

    You should be able to ask:
    a) Why does this approach work well here?
    b) Why is the obvious alternative weaker?
    c) What changes when constraints change?
    d) What assumption is this solution quietly depending on?

    The same applies to DSA and design both. A medium problem solved once is not enough. A design diagram drawn once is not enough. You need to understand why the solution holds, where it bends, and where it breaks.

    [2] Depth of communication

    Interviews score the quality of reasoning you can make visible. If your ideas are stuck in your head, jumpy in delivery, or hard to follow, the interviewer experiences that as uncertainty.

    This is why good communication matters so much now. I don’t mean fancy words. Here I’m talking about your reasoning feels organized, deliberate, and easy to work with.

    [3] Depth of systems thinking

    Good questions to answer that make for strong signals:
    - Why add this now?
    - What does this solve first?
    - What new complexity does it introduce?
    - What is the cheaper alternative?
    - What happens when traffic, writes, or failure patterns change?

    So yes, this market is harsher but if you understand what companies are looking for, you’ll be in a much better place:

    Focus on:
    a) answer accuracy
    b) explanation
    c) judgment
    d) communication
    e) adaptability under pressure
