  • You are in a system design interview at Stripe, interviewing for a senior role, and the Senior Engineer interviewing looks at you and goes:
  • “How does a browser know your credit card is fake before you hit 'Submit'?”
  • You might mistake this for a simple frontend validation problem.If so, then its a definite no-hire.

  • It is a layered validation problem in which cheap, deterministic checks occur as early as possible, and expensive, high-risk checks are pushed to the backend.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real-time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    Back to the topic, here is how I would break it down.

    [1] First, the browser is not checking all possible card numbers

    This is the part where most junior engineers fumble, Yes, there are billions of possible 16-digit combinations. But the first check the client does isn’t to ask if your credit card is legit.

    It asks much simpler questions like:

    – does this number match a known card issuer pattern
    – does the length look valid for that issuer
    – does it pass the checksum rule

    [2] Issuer and BIN checks happen early

    The first few digits of a card already tell you a lot. Those prefixes, often called BIN or IIN ranges, identify the network and issuer family.

    For example, some prefixes map to Visa, some to Mastercard, some to Amex.

    So when you type the first few digits, the client can already say:

    – this looks like Visa
    – expected length is probably 16
    – CVV and expiry formatting should follow this path

    If the prefix is impossible or the length is wrong, the client can reject immediately.

    [3] Then comes the Luhn check

    This is the classic checksum used by most card networks.

    Very simplified:

    – starting from the right, double every second digit
    – if doubling gives you 2 digits, subtract 9
    – add everything
    – total should be divisible by 10

    You might be like, “ Wait a minute..,Why do this on the client side”
    Because it’s a quick, low-cost way to filter out obvious invalid numbers before they go up the chain.

    That gives you 3 wins:

    – better UX because users get immediate feedback
    – lower backend load because bad inputs never hit your servers
    – cleaner fraud and auth pipelines because you do not waste expensive checks on obviously invalid numbers

    [4] But this is still not  authorization

    Passing Luhn does not mean the card is .
    – It does not mean the account is open.
    – It does not mean the customer has funds.
    – It does not mean the transaction is safe.

    The backend still has to do the  work:
    – tokenization so raw PAN is not handled everywhere
    – payment processor / network authorization
    – AVS, CVC, expiry validation
    – fraud scoring
    – velocity checks
    – risk rules
    – 3DS or step-up authentication where needed

  • Feed post number 45

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • 50+ software engineers tried this exact system design question in our free mock interview tool on Layrs and got stuck on the same follow-up: You are designing an email service like Gmail. It has 1.8B+ users.
  • How do you make sure every username is unique, and still check availability instantly while the user is typing?
  • This is a read-scaling question. You need to avoid millions of unnecessary database lookups while still guaranteeing uniqueness.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    Let’s break it down:

    1. Start with the source of truth

    The final guarantee should come from the primary database. Put a unique index on the username. That is the hard guarantee.

    So even if two people try to claim sameer123 at the same time, only one write succeeds.

    2. Then solve the fast-check problem

    You do not want every keystroke to hit the database. If millions of users are checking usernames, that becomes wasteful very quickly.

    So put a Bloom filter in front.

    3. What a Bloom filter does

    A Bloom filter is a very compact in-memory data structure used for membership checks.

    You ask: “Have we probably seen this username before?”

    It gives 2 kinds of answers:
    – definitely not present
    – maybe present

    That is the key.

    If it says “definitely not present,” you can skip the expensive DB read.
    If it says “maybe present,” then you check Redis or the database.

    4. Why this works well here

    For username availability, false positives are okay.

    If the Bloom filter says “maybe taken” for a name that is actually available, you just do one extra lookup.

    False negatives would be bad, and Bloom filters do not produce them if built correctly.

    5. Final flow

    – user types a username
    – app checks Bloom filter
    – if “definitely not present,” show likely available
    – if “maybe present,” check Redis or DB
    – on signup, DB unique constraint makes the final decision
    – after successful signup, update Redis and Bloom filter

    A few important notes:

    Bloom filters do not enforce uniqueness. They only reduce unnecessary reads before the real uniqueness check happens.

    Also, Bloom filters are especially useful for skipping lookups for usernames that are definitely not taken yet. They are not the final authority.

    And since Bloom filters do not support deletions well, they work best when usernames are permanent, or when false positives are acceptable and the filter is rebuilt periodically.

    That is the pattern:
    DB unique index for correctness.
    Bloom filter for cheap membership checks.
    Redis for fast follow-up reads.
    DB write path for the final decision.

  • Feed post number 46

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1mo •
  • Imagine you’re in a system design interview at Meta for an E4 role, and the interviewer asks: “How would you design WhatsApp’s typing indicator for 2 billion users?”
  • The core of the problem is sending very small, very short-lived signals across a massive real-time system with low latency, low cost, and without treating ephemeral state like durable data.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor: https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW, completely free to use :)

  • You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    Here is how I would break it down.

    [1] Clarify what we actually need to build

    Typing indicators are ephemeral. If I type for 2 seconds and stop, nobody cares whether that event is stored forever.

    What matters is:

    - the other user sees “typing…” almost instantly
    - it disappears quickly when I stop
    - occasional drops are acceptable
    - message delivery remains more important than typing signals

    So this is a low-latency presence problem, not a primary database problem.

    [2] Do not send an event for every keystroke

    That would be wasteful at WhatsApp scale.

    A better model is:

    - send `typing_started` when user begins typing
    - send `typing_stopped` after a short inactivity timeout
    - also send stop on message send, chat close, or app background

    This keeps event volume much lower while preserving the UX.

    [3] High-level flow

    A simple design would be:

    - sender has a persistent WebSocket connection to the chat gateway
    - client emits `typing_started` for chatId X
    - gateway forwards it to a realtime presence service
    - presence service checks whether recipient is online and on which active connections
    - service pushes the signal to recipient’s device sessions
    - recipient client shows “typing…”

    If no fresh signal arrives for a few seconds, the client clears it automatically.

    [4] Why WebSockets make sense here

    Could you do this with polling?
    Yes.

    Would it feel good?
    No.

    Typing indicators are one of those features where even a small delay makes the UX feel broken. This is exactly why apps like WhatsApp keep a massive number of long-lived connections open.

    You are paying the connection cost to buy real-time behavior.

    [5] Where should the state live?

    Do not write typing events into your main chat database. That would be silly.

    This state belongs in a fast in-memory layer with short TTLs.

    Think:
    - userId -> active connection mapping
    - chatId -> users currently typing
    - TTL of a few seconds
    - automatic expiry if `typing_stopped` never arrives

    That keeps the system self-cleaning and cheap.

    Continued in the comments ↓

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you're in a L5 system design interview at Google (1Cr+ role) and the interviewer asks: Google Maps tells you there’s traffic ahead in real time. There are no sensors on the road. So how does it know?
  • This is a classic large scale location aggregation + speed estimation problem.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI Tutor: https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW , completely free to use :)

  • You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    [1] Clarify what we actually need to detect

    This is not “understand the whole road network.”

    It is:
    - collect live location pings from millions of phones
    - map those pings to actual road segments
    - estimate current speed on each segment
    - compare current speed vs expected baseline
    - mark slowdowns as traffic and update ETAs

    So the core problem is real-time speed estimation from noisy mobile GPS data, not road sensors.

    [2] High level approach

    Use a streaming pipeline plus map-matching and aggregation.

    - phones send periodic location + speed + heading updates
    - system snaps raw GPS points to likely road segments
    - updates are grouped by segment and time window
    - average speed on each segment is computed
    - if current speed drops below normal baseline, mark traffic
    - routing and ETA systems consume this live traffic layer

    [3] Ingestion and map matching

    Raw GPS is noisy, so first we clean it up.

    - ingest pings from opted-in mobile devices
    - discard low-quality or stale points
    - use heading, prior movement, and nearby roads to map-match each point
    - convert “lat/lon from phone” into “vehicle is likely on road segment X”

    Why is this step important?

    - GPS drift can place cars on the wrong street
    - cities have parallel roads, flyovers, service lanes
    - without map matching, traffic estimates become garbage

    [4] Speed estimation phase

    Now estimate traffic on each road segment.

    - bucket pings by road segment and small time window
    - compute median / weighted average speed
    - smooth sudden spikes with rolling windows
    - compare against historical baseline for that segment, weekday, and hour
    - if live speed is much lower than normal, classify as congestion

    This is why Google Maps knows “traffic ahead.”
    It is seeing lots of devices moving unusually slowly on the same road.

    [5] Scale and system design details

    - use stream processing, not batch
    - partition events by geo region / road segment
    - keep hot state in memory for active windows
    - store historical baselines separately for comparison
    - push updates to routing systems every few seconds / minutes

    At scale, this becomes a distributed stream processing + geospatial aggregation problem.

  • At this point, this question is probably older than I am, and it's been asked 1000s of times in interviews. So, let's put this to rest forever. This post will be the deepest anybody has ever gone into what happens when you type Google dot com in your browser.

    [1] The Action

    - The Enter key bottoms out
    https://lnkd.in/gMm9--EC

    https://lnkd.in/gPR7nGBa

    https://lnkd.in/gGgnG6F4

    [2] The Browser Understands the Input

    - Parse URL
    https://lnkd.in/gyqspN2s
    - Is it a URL or a search term
    https://lnkd.in/g5dA69uA
    - Convert non-ASCII Unicode characters in the hostname
    https://lnkd.in/gsCCq5t3
    - Check HSTS list
    https://lnkd.in/gcfAnvmp

    [3] DNS and Network Resolution

    - DNS lookup
    https://lnkd.in/gXNicvAq
    - ARP process
    https://lnkd.in/gt2_g_JV
    - Opening of a socket
    https://lnkd.in/gEFFZBMY
    - TLS handshake
    https://lnkd.in/gf5m7TXc

    [4] The Request Reaches the Server

    - HTTP protocol
    https://lnkd.in/gX_m9-ca

    https://lnkd.in/gUFiuMnX

    [5] The Browser Renders the Page

    - Behind the scenes of the browser

  • Feed post number 50

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Meta, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Google, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Amazon, they’ll again go deep into your system design
  • System design is that one skill that levels up your:
  • – pay scale
    – responsibilities
    – impact on the company

    I asked 5+ L5 and equivalent engineers at Uber, Google, Meta, and Microsoft which problems they would ask in a mock interview to candidates if they had to test how well they knew system design.

    This is the list I came up with, grouped into 8 themes, 5 problems each.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, we've just rolled out another feature on Layrs, it's called AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW, it's completely free to try out right now :)

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    1. Social feeds and content platforms

    ↬ Design Twitter
    ↬ Design Instagram
    ↬ Design Facebook’s Newsfeed
    ↬ Design a Platform Like Reddit
    ↬ Design Youtube or Netflix

    2. Messaging and real time collaboration

    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design a Live Comment System
    ↬ Design a Video Conferencing system
    ↬ Design a Collaboration Tool for Team Communication
    ↬ Design Google Doc

    3. E commerce, payments, and logistics

    ↬ Design an E-commerce Service
    ↬ Design Shopify
    ↬ Design an Online Payment Service
    ↬ Design a Digital Wallet
    ↬ Design a Food Delivery Service

    4. Core infra and storage primitives

    ↬ Design a Key Value Store
    ↬ Design a Web Cache
    ↬ Design a Distributed File System
    ↬ Design a Global Content Distribution Network
    ↬ Design a Load Balancer

    5. Events, queues, and rate limiting

    ↬ Design an API Rate Limiter
    ↬ Design a Distributed Messaging System
    ↬ Design a Distributed Counter
    ↬ Design a Task Scheduler
    ↬ Design a Webhook Notification Service

    6. Data, analytics, and logging

    ↬ Design a Web Analytics Tool
    ↬ Design a Metrics Monitoring and Alerting System
    ↬ Design a Log Collection and Analysis System
    ↬ Design an Ad Click Aggregation System
    ↬ Design a Top-K Request Analysis System

    7. Ids, consistency, and coordination

    ↬ Design a Distributed Unique Id Generator
    ↬ Design a Distributed Locking System
    ↬ Design a Resource Allocation Service
    ↬ Design a Distributed Tracing System
    ↬ Design a Database Batch Auditing Service

    8. Boss fight problems

    ↬ Design Uber Backend
    ↬ Design Ticketmaster
    ↬ Design Google Search
    ↬ Design a Real Time Stock Trading Platform
    ↬ Design a Container Orchestration System

    Pick any one from each category and you already have a solid system design interview loop to practice.

  • Feed post number 52

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • "If cache is faster than database, why not store everything in cache?" This question sounds dumb. Until it shows up in your system design interview.
  • FAANGs don't ask this to test if you know Redis is fast. That's a common fact. They ask it to see how deep your understanding actually goes.
  • It's the same energy as asking Two Sum in a DSA round. Simple on the surface. But the follow-ups will expose every gap you have.

  • So let's actually answer it.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, we've just rolled out another feature on Layrs, it's called AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW, it's completely free to try out right now :)

    1. Cache is volatile, but the Database is durable.

    Cache lives in RAM. RAM loses everything when the power goes out. Your database writes to disk. If your entire data layer is cache and your server restarts, congratulations, you now have a very fast system with zero data.

    2. RAM is expensive. The disk is cheap.

    Storing 1 TB in Redis? That'll cost you roughly 30-50x more than storing it in PostgreSQL on disk. At scale, "just cache everything" is a budget request that'll get you laughed out of the room.

    3. Cache is not designed for complex queries.

    Try running a JOIN across three entities in Redis. Or a full-text search. Or an aggregation pipeline. Caches are key-value stores optimized for lookups, not for relational logic. Databases exist because data has relationships.

    4. Consistency becomes a nightmare.

    The moment you have two sources of truth: cache AND database, you're managing invalidation. And as Phil Karlton said: "The two hardest problems in computer science are cache invalidation and naming things." When your cache says one thing and your DB says another, which one is right?

    5. Not all data needs to be fast.

    Your user's profile picture URL? Cache it. The audit log from 2019? That can stay on cold storage. Speed is a spectrum. Treating all data equally is bad architecture.

    So what's the real answer in the interview?

    Cache is an optimization layer, not a storage layer. You use it to reduce latency on hot, frequently-accessed data. You don't use it to replace your persistence layer. The two solve fundamentally different problems.

    The interviewer isn't checking if you know cache is fast. They're checking if you understand why we still need databases even though cache exists.

    If you can explain the tradeoffs between cost, durability, consistency, and query complexity in under 2-3 minutes, you've passed.

    If you just say "because cache is temporary" you've given a surface answer to a depth question. And that's exactly the gap this question is designed to find.

  • Feed post number 53

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Two Software engineers with 5+ years of experience interviewed at 𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗴𝗹𝗲 for an L5 Senior Engineer role. They both were given the same system design problem: Design YouTube
  • One was rejected after the system design round.
  • The other was hired and given “Strong Hire” after his system design round.
  • What was the difference? This 8-layer thinking approach:

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, we've just rolled out another feature on Layrs, it's called AI Tutor:     https://lnkd.in/gcWfR7jW, it's completely free to try out right now :)

    You can:
    - voice chat about your questions in real-time
    - get feedback in real time and improve with these sessions.
    - learn concepts, practice HLD questions even if you're a complete beginner

    1. Requirements

    ↪︎ What exactly are you building, and for whom?
    ○ List core features (upload, stream, view videos).
    ○ Ask for scale: daily uploads, views, max file size.
    ○ Clarify: is instant consistency needed? Or is delay OK?
    ○ Separate functional from non-functional requirements early.

    2. Core Entities

    ↪︎ What are the main building blocks?
    ○ Identify main nouns: video, user, metadata.
    ○ Split raw video (bytes) and metadata (title, creator, etc).
    ○ Consider: Do you need to persist more than just videos (comments, playlists)?
    ○ Keep schema flexible for scale and new features.

    3. API Design
    ↪︎How do clients interact with your system?

    ○ Define endpoints for upload, watch, fetch metadata.
    ○ Support chunked uploads for large files (don’t just POST giant blobs).
    ○ Use pre-signed URLs for secure direct uploads to storage.
    ○ Remember: GET for streaming should handle different resolutions.

    4. High-Level Flow
    ↪︎ Can you sketch the basic path from client to storage?

    ○ API Gateway routes requests to microservices.
    ○ Video metadata goes to a database (e.g., DynamoDB, Postgres).
    ○ Raw video files go straight to blob storage (e.g., S3, GCS).
    ○ Store references/URLs for everything in metadata.

    5. Uploading at Scale
    ↪︎ Can your design handle 256GB files and millions of uploads?

    ○ Use multi-part uploads, clients break videos into chunks.
    ○ S3/GCS stitches chunks after upload.
    ○ Metadata is updated only after a full upload is confirmed.
    ○ Avoid routing huge files through your API gateway, offload to storage.

    6. Streaming & Playback
    ↪︎ How do you get pixels on the screen fast?

    ○ Chunk videos into small playback segments (2–10s each).
    ○ Transcode into multiple resolutions/bitrates (4K, 1080p, 240p).
    ○ Use manifest files to organize chunks for adaptive bitrate streaming.
    ○ Support protocols like HLS or DASH for smooth delivery.

    7. Latency, Reliability, and CDN
    ↪︎ How do you ensure speed and global access?

    ○ Cache popular videos/chunks in regional CDNs.
    ○ Manifest files & video data live close to the user.
    ○ Adaptive streaming: client auto-switches quality based on bandwidth.
    ○ System favours availability over instant consistency; eventual propagation is fine.

  • System design gets a lot easier after you learn these 40 major concepts that keep repeating in interviews and design discussions.

  • - voice chat about your questions in real-time

    [1] Request and data flow basics
    - Client-server model
    - REST APIs
    - gRPC
    - Load balancer
    - Reverse proxy
    - API gateway
    - Service registry
    - Rate limiting

    [2] Storage and data modeling

    - Relational databases
    - NoSQL databases
    - Sharding
    - Indexes
    - Replication
    - Read write splitting
    - Distributed transactions
    - Event sourcing

    [3] Caching and performance

    - In memory cache
    - Cache aside pattern
    - Write through cache
    - Write back cache
    - Content delivery network
    - Connection pooling
    - Pagination
    - Back pressure

    [4] Reliability and fault tolerance

    - Health checks
    - Heartbeats
    - Leader election
    - Replication factor
    - Data partitioning
    - Quorum reads
    - Circuit breaker
    - Retry with exponential backoff

    [5] Distributed systems and messaging

    - Message queues
    - Publish subscribe pattern
    - Idempotency
    - Exactly once semantics
    - Eventual consistency
    - Saga pattern
    - Distributed locks
    - Observability pillars

  • Feed post number 57

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Anthropic (owner of Claude) pays over $582k (~5 Cr+) average salary to their software engineers, and this is the type of system design questions they ask: “Design an inference API for serving large language models.”
  • This sounds like an API question. It is actually a GPU scheduling, batching, and memory management question.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • Design an inference API for serving large language models.

    Here is the clean high-level breakdown:

    1. What are we building?

    We need an API that can handle:
    - variable-length prompts
    - variable-length outputs
    - streaming responses
    - concurrent requests
    - request priority
    - low latency for chat use cases
    - high throughput at scale

    So the goal is not just to return tokens. It is to do that fast, fairly, and without wasting GPU capacity.

    2. High-level architecture

    I’d split the system into:
    - API Gateway for auth, rate limits, validation
    - Request Router to tag requests with model, priority, estimated token count
    - Priority Queues for premium, standard, and batch traffic
    - Scheduler to decide batching order
    - Inference Workers running on GPUs
    - KV Cache Manager to track runtime memory
    - Metrics + Autoscaling for queue depth, latency, throughput

    3. Core API

    POST /v1/inference
    Request includes:

    - model
    - prompt
    - max_tokens
    - stream: true/false
    - priority

    Support:
    - SSE / WebSocket streaming
    - cancellation
    - timeouts

    4. The main challenge: batching

    This is the heart of the design.

    If you serve one request at a time, GPU utilization is terrible.
    If you batch everything together, latency gets terrible.

    So use dynamic batching:

    - same model
    - similar prompt length
    - similar output expectations
    - fit within GPU memory budget

    Also call out the two phases:

    - Prefill: process full prompt, heavy compute, easier to batch
    - Decode: generate token by token, harder because requests finish at different times

    That is a strong signal in the interview.

    5. When do you flush a batch?

    Flush when:

    - batch reaches max size
    - wait time crosses a threshold
    - GPU / KV cache budget is about to fill

    This is the real tradeoff:
    better batching vs lower latency.

    6. KV cache management

    Every active request consumes KV cache memory.
    Long prompts and long outputs increase memory pressure fast.

    So admission control must check:

    - compute capacity
    - KV cache footprint

    This is why raw GPU utilization is not enough.

    7. Queueing and priority

    Do not use one FIFO queue.

    Use:
    - priority queues
    - weighted fairness
    - per-tenant throttling

    That way premium chat traffic is protected from slow batch jobs.

    8. Autoscaling

    Scale on:

    - queue depth
    - queue wait time
    - estimated queued tokens
    - time to first token
    - KV cache pressure

    Best point to mention:queue depth weighted by estimated token count is a better scaling signal than raw GPU utilization.

  • Feed post number 58

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you're sitting in an interview for an L63 Sr. Backend SWE role at Microsoft, and you're asked this question: How does Netflix prevent users from taking screenshots or screen recording while a movie is playing in the app?
  • Here’s how I would break it down if I were the one being interviewed. There are a lot of directions you can take this, but I’ll focus on the high-level design with a few senior-level deep dives.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • 1. Requirements

    This is not really a “video streaming” problem. It is a content protection + playback pipeline problem.

    The real pain points are:

    - premium content must not be trivially captured through screenshots or screen recording
    - the app should still work smoothly across devices, browsers, phones, tablets, TVs
    - prevention should be as strong as possible, but we must accept that 100% prevention is impossible
    - system should integrate with DRM, secure video paths, and device capabilities

    Functional requirements
    - User can play licensed movies and shows
    - System should block or degrade screenshots / screen recording where platform support exists
    - System should securely deliver encrypted video segments
    - Player should only decrypt content inside trusted playback environments
    - Different device classes may get different protection levels

    Out of scope
    - piracy detection on camcorder recordings
    - legal takedown workflows
    - watermark-based leak tracing
    - recommendation / search / playback quality adaptation
    - subscription and billing

    Non-functional requirements
    - low-latency playback startup
    - high availability for playback and license delivery
    - strong content protection for premium titles
    - graceful fallback on devices with weaker protection
    - support for millions of concurrent playback sessions

    Important clarification

    A good candidate should say this early: Netflix does not really “stop screenshots” with one universal trick.

    It combines:
    - DRM
    - encrypted media delivery
    - secure video rendering
    - OS-level screenshot protection where possible
    - policy decisions based on device trust level

    So this is a defense-in-depth system, not one feature flag.

    2. Core Entities and API

    Before jumping into architecture, identify the key entities.

    Core entities
    - User(id, subscription, device_list, region)
    - Device(id, platform, DRM_capability, security_level)
    - Content(id, title, drm_policy, allowed_resolutions, license_rules)
    - PlaybackSession(id, user_id, device_id, content_id, status)
    - License(id, session_id, key_id, expiry, policy)
    - VideoSegment(content_id, bitrate, segment_id, encrypted_blob_url)

    Rest of the breakdown is here:     https://lnkd.in/g65nFZsT

  • Activate to view larger image,

  • Sameer Bhardwaj and 164 others

  • Feed post number 59

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo • Edited •
  • Imagine you're in an E4 system design interview at Meta (80LPA+ role) and the interviewer asks: If apps like Instagram are free, how do they store billions of photos and videos without charging users anything?
  • This is a classic large-scale storage + serving problem. Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • [1] Clarify what we actually need to solve

  • This is not just “save files somewhere.”

    It is:
    - store billions of user-generated photos and videos durably
    - serve them globally with low latency
    - handle huge spikes in uploads and views
    - keep storage cost low enough that the free product still works as a business
    - support metadata, privacy settings, deletes, thumbnails, and multiple video qualities

    So the real problem is cheap durable blob storage + fast global delivery, not just databases.

    [2] High level approach

    Use an object storage architecture, very similar to S3-style thinking, plus a CDN.
    - user uploads media through an upload service
    - media gets stored as objects in distributed blob storage
    - metadata goes into a separate database
    - background workers generate thumbnails, compressed variants, and multiple resolutions
    - users fetch media through CDN edges, not directly from storage every time

    That separation is the key:

    - blobs in object storage
    - metadata in databases
    - delivery through CDN

    [3] Upload and storage flow

    When a user uploads a photo or video:

    - client asks backend for an upload URL
    - backend authenticates user and creates a media record
    - client uploads file to object storage
    - storage returns object key like `media/user123/abc.jpg`
    - metadata DB stores:
    - owner_id
    - object_key
    - media_type
    - visibility
    - created_at
    - processing_status

    Why not store media in a database?

    - media files are huge
    - databases are expensive for large blobs
    - object stores are cheaper, simpler, and built for this exact use case

    [4] Processing pipeline

    After upload, run async workers:

    - image resize
    - thumbnail generation
    - video transcoding into multiple bitrates
    - format conversion
    - moderation / safety checks

    This is queue-based because video processing is expensive and should not block the upload request.

    [5] How they keep it free

    The short answer: ads fund the storage bill.

    The technical answer:

    - object storage is much cheaper per GB than traditional databases
    - cold media can move to lower-cost storage tiers
    - CDN reduces repeated origin reads
    - media is compressed aggressively
    - old videos/photos are rarely accessed compared to new ones, so caching and tiering cut costs a lot

    At this scale, storage is not free. It is just optimized so well that ad revenue more than covers it.
  • Activate to view larger image,
  • Activate to view larger image,
  • 790
  • Sameer Bhardwaj and 789 others
  • 48 comments
  • 22 reposts
  • Like

  • Repost
  • Send

  • Feed post number 60

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Meta for an IC4 role. The interviewer leans in and asks: "If a photo is already on my phone, how can WhatsApp still delete it when the sender taps Delete for Everyone?"
  • Here is how you should break it down 👇
  • A lot of people think “Delete for Everyone” means WhatsApp somehow reaches into your gallery and erases a file from your device. That is not really what is happening.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out my free mock interview tool on Layrs. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    The real answer depends on where the photo is stored, how the chat app references it, and what exactly the app is allowed to delete.

    1. First clarify the product behavior

    Before jumping into architecture, say this clearly in the interview:

    There are two different cases here.

    a) The photo exists only inside the chat app’s managed storage
    b) The photo has already been saved to the user’s gallery / camera roll / filesystem

    This distinction is everything.

    If the photo is only inside WhatsApp’s controlled storage, the app can remove the local file and remove the message reference from the chat UI.

    If the photo was exported or auto-saved to the gallery, WhatsApp usually cannot reliably delete that copy, because that file is now outside the app’s normal ownership boundary.

    So “Delete for Everyone” is mostly about:

    - deleting the message record
    - deleting the media attachment reference
    - deleting any app-managed cached copy
    - syncing that deletion event to all participants

    It is not magic remote file deletion across the whole phone.

    2. High-level idea

    The clean way to think about this is:

    A chat message is metadata plus optional media.

    For a photo message, the system usually has:

    - message_id
    - chat_id
    - sender_id
    - media_id
    - timestamp
    - status
    - optional encryption metadata

    And the actual photo itself may live in:

    - encrypted object storage on server for temporary delivery
    - app sandbox / local database / media cache on device
    - optionally the user’s gallery if exported

    When sender taps Delete for Everyone, the system does not chase every byte everywhere.

    It sends a deletion command tied to the message_id.

    3. What happens when the photo is first sent

    Here is the send flow:

    1. User picks a photo
    2. App encrypts media and prepares message metadata
    3. App uploads encrypted media blob or sends it through media pipeline
    4. App sends message metadata referencing that media
    5. Receiver downloads the encrypted media
    6. Receiver stores it locally in app-managed storage
    7. Chat UI shows the message by resolving message_id -> media_id -> local file

    Read rest of the post:     https://lnkd.in/gpv-wGze

  • Feed post number 61

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • In the last 6 years, this software engineer has moved from 16 LPA to 27 LPA to now 65+ LPA. Read on and I'll tell you the exact resources he used for his latest switch.
  • Also, if you are preparing for system design or coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool here, it’s free to use: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T (hope it helps :)
  • ➤ Prep time
  • Around 4 to 5 months

    He had not touched LeetCode seriously since the 2019 placements, so the first few weeks were rough. That part is important. People see the final 65+ LPA number, but forget how bad it feels when you restart after years.

    ➤Resources he used

    DSA
    - NeetCode 150
    - Daily LeetCode, around 220 problems

    HLD
    - Hello Interview
    - Alex Xu books
    - Also, try out Layrs :)

    LLD
    - Shreyansh Jain

    Behavioral
    - Focused a lot on communication and storytelling
    - Tightened resume narratives so his work sounded clearer and more impactful in interviews

    Applications and interview stats
    - Applied to around 70 to 80 companies
    - Got around 20 interviews
    - Reached 3 final rounds
    - Got 2 offers
    - Got ghosted by 1 company after the final round

    One thing he said helped a lot was this:

    After every interview, he did a retrospective.
    - What went well
    - What went badly
    - What needed to improve before the next round

    That is the difference between “giving interviews” and actually getting better at them.

    Big takeaway? If you are restarting prep after years, the beginning will feel terrible. Your speed will be bad. Your confidence will be shaky. You will wonder how you ever solved problems before.

    That part is normal. Stay consistent long enough, and it starts coming back.

  • Feed post number 63

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you're sitting in an interview for an L63 Senior Software Engineer role at Microsoft, and you're asked this question: "Design Spotify for a global scale."
  • Here’s how I would break it down if I were the one being interviewed. There are a lot of directions you can take this, but I’ll focus on the high-level design with a few senior-level deep dives.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  •  1. Requirements

    Spotify is not just “play a song.”

    The real pain points are:

    - Serving audio with low startup latency across millions of concurrent listeners
    - Personalizing recommendations and search without slowing down the core playback flow
    - Handling massive fan-out when popular artists drop new songs at the same time
    - Keeping playback state consistent across devices

    Functional requirements

    - User can search for songs, albums, artists, playlists
    - User can play, pause, skip, seek, and resume tracks
    - User can create playlists and like songs
    - User can get recommendations based on listening history
    - User can continue playback across devices
    - Artists can upload songs and metadata

    Out of scope

    - Podcast/video support
    - Ads system
    - Lyrics
    - Social feed and collaborative playlists
    - Royalty/payment settlement

    Non-functional requirements

    - Low startup latency for playback, ideally under 300 ms to first audio chunk
    - High availability for playback and search
    - Eventual consistency is okay for likes, playlists, play counts
    - Strong durability for song metadata and media assets
    - Global scale with regional delivery
    - High throughput during new releases

    If you want anchor numbers:

    - 700M total users
    - 250M DAU
    - 100M concurrent listeners during peaks
    - Tens of millions of songs
    - Millions of playback events per second globally

    A note on estimation:
    Like in good interviews, I would only do math when it influences a decision. For example, concurrent listeners and chunk size matter for CDN design. Exact daily search bandwidth does not need over-analysis upfront.

    2. Core Entities and API

    Before jumping into architecture, identify the core objects and the APIs around them.

    Core entities

    - User(id, profile, subscription_type, preferences)
    - Song(id, artist_id, album_id, metadata, duration, codec, storage_url)
    - Playlist(id, owner_id, songs[], metadata)
    - PlaybackSession(id, user_id, device_id, current_song_id, position, state)
    - Like(user_id, song_id, timestamp)
    - PlayEvent(user_id, song_id, timestamp, device, duration_played)

    Rest of the breakdown is here:     https://lnkd.in/gDS4RvY2

  • Feed post number 64

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you're in a L5 system design interview at Google (1Cr+ role) and the interviewer asks: How does YouTube detect copyrighted music inside millions of uploaded videos daily?
  • This is a classic large scale content matching problem.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • [1] Clarify what we actually need to detect

    This is not “understand the whole video.”

    It is:
    - detect whether uploaded audio matches known copyrighted audio
    - work even if the song is trimmed, compressed, pitch shifted a bit, or mixed with speech
    - do it at upload scale with low latency
    - allow some false positives/negatives, but keep them low enough for policy actions

    So the core problem is robust audio fingerprint matching, not full ML understanding.

    [2] High level approach

    Use a fingerprinting pipeline plus a distributed lookup system.

    - rights holders upload reference tracks
    - system converts each track into compact audio fingerprints
    - every uploaded video goes through the same fingerprinting pipeline
    - fingerprints are matched against a massive index
    - if similarity crosses a threshold, trigger a claim/review flow

    This is basically Content ID style thinking.

    [3] Ingestion and fingerprint generation

    For both reference songs and uploaded videos:

    - extract audio track from video
    - normalize audio a bit to reduce noise from encoding differences
    - split into small time windows
    - convert each window into spectral features using FFT/spectrogram style processing
    - generate fingerprints from stable peaks, not raw waveform

    Why fingerprints?

    - much smaller than raw audio
    - robust to compression and small edits
    - fast to search at scale

    [4] Matching phase

    Now match upload fingerprints against the copyright index.

    - hash fingerprint features into an inverted index
    - retrieve candidate songs with overlapping hashes
    - align matches by time offset
    - if many fingerprints line up with a consistent offset, it is likely the same song

    That offset consistency is important.
    Random overlaps happen.
    Real matches line up over time.

    [5] Scale and system design details

    - shard fingerprint index by hash range
    - process uploads asynchronously through a queue
    - parallelize fingerprint extraction across workers
    - cache hot tracks because popular songs get matched often
    - store only compact hashes plus metadata, not huge raw blobs in the serving path

    For millions of uploads, this becomes a distributed search problem over fingerprints.

  • Feed post number 65

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you’re in a Google L5 system design interview. You’ve been asked to design a distributed job scheduler API for something like delayed emails, payment retries, webhook retries, or background report generation.
  • You walk through the design.
  • Things look good.
  • Then the interviewer interrupts and asks: “Your API works perfectly with 1K users. It falls apart at 100K. You can’t change the infrastructure. What would you use to fix it?”

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Here’s how I’d answer it.

    1. Reframe the problem

    If infra is fixed, I should not think “add more servers.”

    I should think:
    - reduce load per request
    - smooth traffic spikes
    - remove unnecessary work
    - fail gracefully under pressure
    - protect critical paths first

    So this is no longer an infra scaling problem.
    It is now an application efficiency + traffic control problem.

    2. First, identify what is breaking

    For a job scheduler API, common failure points at 100K users are:
    - too many synchronous writes
    - duplicate job submissions
    - retry storms
    - expensive reads on job status
    - hot partitions for scheduled jobs
    - DB getting hammered by polling clients

    So before “fixing,” I’d isolate whether the bottleneck is:

    - write path
    - read path
    - retry path
    - bad access pattern

    3. What I’d use to fix it

    If I cannot change infra, I would use:

    - Caching
    Cache job status reads so clients are not hitting DB every second.

    - Rate limiting
    Protect create-job and status endpoints from abuse and accidental floods.

    - Idempotency keys
    Prevent duplicate job creation when clients retry.

    - Queue buffering
    Accept requests fast, push work asynchronously, return quickly.

    - Batching
    Process status updates or job fetches in batches instead of one-by-one.

    - Backpressure
    If the system is overloaded, reject or defer low-priority requests early.

    4. API-level fixes I’d explicitly mention

    For example:

    - POST /jobs should support an Idempotency-Key
    - GET /jobs/:id/status should be cached or eventually consistent
    - clients should not poll every second, prefer long polling or webhook callback
    - retries should use exponential backoff with jitter
    - job submission should validate payload size and reject bad requests early

    So I am scaling the API by improving behavior, not by adding machines.

    5. What this shows in an interview

    At L5, the interviewer wants to see that you do not panic and say “add Kubernetes” to every scaling problem.

    Sometimes the best fix is:

    - less duplicate work
    - fewer round trips
    - smarter retries
    - better API contracts
    - stronger protection at the edges

    That is how I’d answer it.

    Because if the infra is fixed, the only real lever left is efficiency.

  • Feed post number 69

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • This question was asked by a Senior Research Scientist @ Google, who is also an adjunct faculty at IIT Madras and IIT Bombay. It got so viral it became Twitter “news”.
  • And Raj himself basically said: “TFW I just wanted to introduce people to MapReduce and it becomes news.”
  • So yes, this is a classic MapReduce-style system design problem.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Question: You have 5TB worth of text data, and you want to count the total number of words. How would you do it?

    Here is a solid way to break it down.

    [1] Clarify the one thing that makes or breaks the answer
    Define what a “word” is.

    Because “word count” is not one universal definition.

    You need to decide things like:

    - Is a word whitespace delimited or do we treat punctuation as separators
    - Do we count “don’t” as 1 word or 2
    - What about hyphenated words like “state-of-the-art”
    - Case sensitivity, unicode, emojis, numbers
    - What about HTML tags, markdown, logs, JSON blobs inside the text

    In real systems, this definition matters more than the distributed compute part.

    [2] High level approach
    Use a distributed batch job.

    - Split 5TB into chunks across many machines
    - Each machine processes its chunk independently
    - Each machine outputs a local count
    - Aggregate local counts into a global count

    This is exactly the MapReduce mental model.

    [3] Map phase
    Each mapper gets a chunk of input (say 128MB blocks if using HDFS style storage).

    For each chunk:

    - Stream through the file, don’t load into memory
    - Tokenize based on your “word” definition
    - Count tokens
    - Emit (key = “word_count”, value = local_count)
    or even simpler: just output the integer

    Important detail:
    You must handle chunk boundaries. A word can get split between two blocks.

    Common fix:

    - Overlap a small window between blocks
    - Or let each mapper “peek” slightly into next block and adjust
    - Or enforce splitting on newline boundaries when possible

    [4] Reduce phase
    Reducer just sums integers.

    Total = Σ local_count

    This part is intentionally boring, because the work is in the map stage.

    [5] Reliability and correctness checks
    This is what interviewers like to see beyond the basic MapReduce answer.

    - Fault tolerance: if a mapper fails, rerun that chunk
    - Exactly-once vs at-least-once: avoid double counting by ensuring each chunk is processed once, or make output idempotent
    - Data integrity: validate input blocks with checksums
    - Monitoring: track how many chunks processed, lagging workers, skewed partitions
    - Sampling: run word count on a small subset and compare results across two implementations to catch tokenizer bugs

    [6] Performance knobs

    - Increase parallelism until IO becomes the bottleneck
    - Use compressed input if storage is compressed, but measure CPU cost
    - Prefer streaming parsing
    - Avoid emitting each word (that would explode shuffle) since we only need total count

  • Feed post number 70

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 2mo •
  • Imagine you're interviewing for the E4 role at Meta, you're in a system design round and the interviewer asks you: “You can scroll Instagram Reels for hours and almost never see the same reel twice. How would you design that?”
  • 1. Reframe the requirement
  • Goal: For a given user, while they are actively scrolling Reels, they should almost never see the same reel again. It is ok if:

  • - A reel reappears after some days or weeks
    - A reel reappears if there was a serious backend failure

    So I want a per user, sliding window “seen set” that filters candidates before ranking.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

     2. High-level flow

    For one swipe:

    1. Client event

    -User watches reel R123 for longer than a threshold or interacts with it.
    -Client sends an impression event to backend.

    2. Impression logging

    -Event hits a write API which pushes into a log stream (Kafka style).
    -From that stream we update:
    +Online store for fast “has this user seen X recently”.
    +Data warehouse for long term analytics and model training.

    3. Next reel request

    -Client calls GET /reels/next?userId=U.
    -Ranking service collects a large candidate set (thousands) from many sources: follow graph, interest graph, trending, exploration, ads.
    -Before scoring, it filters out anything that appears in user U’s seen set.
    -Rank, pick top 1, send to client, prefetch the next few.

    Key piece is how we build that seen set.

    3.1 Seen set design

    Constraints:

    -Users might watch thousands of reels per day.
    -We do not need perfect history for years, only enough to avoid dupes in a session or short horizon.
    -Needs to be cheap and fast. I am ok with an occasional repeat if it keeps infra simple.

    Design:

    -For each user U, maintain SeenReels[U] which stores the last N reel ids, with TTL.
    +N may be 20k to 50k. That already covers many hours of scrolling.

    -Store it in a distributed in memory key value store like Redis or Meta equivalent.
    +Key: user id
    +Value: compact structure of reel ids

    -To compress, use one of:
    +A ring buffer of 64 bit ids, evict oldest on insert
    +Or a Bloom filter per day for approximate checks

    -On impression processing, we append the reel id into SeenReels[U].
    -In ranking, we fetch SeenReels[U] once per batch and filter candidates with a fast membership check.

    This gives:

    -O(1) check per candidate
    -Memory bounded per user
    -Natural sliding window from TTL and ring buffer eviction

    Continued below 👇

  • Feed post number 72

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • In 2026, founders in BLR are getting blocked from building and even using their own products.
  • At this point, nothing surprises me anymore.
  • Supabase and Firebase going dark for Indian users is not a “small outage.”
  • Thousands of developers have used these tools to:

    – Host production APIs and databases
    – Power student projects and hackathon apps
    – Run early stage startup backends
    – Ship MVPs for clients and side projects
    – Store auth, logs, analytics and more behind “serverless” buttons

    All of that is now hanging by a DNS thread.

    The worst part is not even the block. It is the zero communication, zero transition plan, and zero regard for the builders who trusted this stack.

    For a lot of young founders, the message is loud and clear: “You can build here, but do not expect stability under your feet.”

    What you can do for now:
    – Use alternative DNS or a VPN to get urgent access.
    – Export backups of your data the moment you can reach your project.
    – Document every external dependency your product has.

  • If I travel back to my Interview Days, I will study these 40 Articles to learn system design Fundamentals. These articles and videos will save you 50+ hours of hopping to resources and wasting time.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • All of these topics covered in the image are mentioned here in great detail:

  • [1] HLD Basics:     https://lnkd.in/gmbzpdjV

    [2] Core Concepts & Trade-offs: https://lnkd.in/gUFeCcpT

    [3] Networking & DNS: https://lnkd.in/gENhQVgS

    [4] Load Balancing & Scaling: https://lnkd.in/giQkSPc7

    [5] Application Architecture: https://lnkd.in/gx8NQwKG

    [6] Databases: https://lnkd.in/gX9VK-MM

    [7] Caching: https://lnkd.in/gtcfXJAH

    [8] Asynchronous Processing:https://lnkd.in/gYeJ2hca

    [9] Communication Protocols: https://lnkd.in/giSSdMJR

  • Feed post number 74

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Meta, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Google, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Amazon, they’ll again go deep into your system design
  • System design is that one skill that levels up your:
  • – pay scale
    – responsibilities
    – impact on the company

    I asked 5+ L5 and equivalent engineers at Uber, Google, Meta, and Microsoft which problems they would ask in a mock interview to candidates if they had to test how well they knew system design.

    This is the list I came up with, grouped into 8 themes, 5 problems each.

    Btw, If you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:    http://layrs.me/interviews

    1. Social feeds and content platforms

    ↬ Design Twitter
    ↬ Design Instagram
    ↬ Design Facebook’s Newsfeed
    ↬ Design a Platform Like Reddit
    ↬ Design Youtube or Netflix

    2. Messaging and real time collaboration

    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design a Live Comment System
    ↬ Design a Video Conferencing system
    ↬ Design a Collaboration Tool for Team Communication
    ↬ Design Google Doc

    3. E commerce, payments, and logistics

    ↬ Design an E-commerce Service
    ↬ Design Shopify
    ↬ Design an Online Payment Service
    ↬ Design a Digital Wallet
    ↬ Design a Food Delivery Service

    4. Core infra and storage primitives

    ↬ Design a Key Value Store
    ↬ Design a Web Cache
    ↬ Design a Distributed File System
    ↬ Design a Global Content Distribution Network
    ↬ Design a Load Balancer

    5. Events, queues, and rate limiting

    ↬ Design an API Rate Limiter
    ↬ Design a Distributed Messaging System
    ↬ Design a Distributed Counter
    ↬ Design a Task Scheduler
    ↬ Design a Webhook Notification Service

    6. Data, analytics, and logging

    ↬ Design a Web Analytics Tool
    ↬ Design a Metrics Monitoring and Alerting System
    ↬ Design a Log Collection and Analysis System
    ↬ Design an Ad Click Aggregation System
    ↬ Design a Top-K Request Analysis System

    7. Ids, consistency, and coordination

    ↬ Design a Distributed Unique Id Generator
    ↬ Design a Distributed Locking System
    ↬ Design a Resource Allocation Service
    ↬ Design a Distributed Tracing System
    ↬ Design a Database Batch Auditing Service

    8. Boss fight problems

    ↬ Design Uber Backend
    ↬ Design Ticketmaster
    ↬ Design Google Search
    ↬ Design a Real Time Stock Trading Platform
    ↬ Design a Container Orchestration System

    Pick any one from each category and you already have a solid system design interview loop to practice.

  • Feed post number 75

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Someone is hitting LeetCode streaks because of their girlfriend.
  • Someone is getting offers from Meta and Google because they got their heart broken.
  • But everyone is grinding Leetcode & if you’re preparing for interviews, chances are, you are too.
  • I cleared interviews at Flipkart, Google, and multiple startups between leaving Flipkart and joining Google. With my experience, I can tell you for a fact:  You don’t need to solve 500 DSA problems to crack good companies.

    I have seen people solve 500 and still fail.
    I have seen people cross 700 and still fail.
    I have also seen people solve 200 to 300 and convert strong offers.

    So what actually matters? A common and reliable benchmark is this: You should feel comfortable solving any medium-level problem in under 20-30 minutes without external help.

    Btw, I have a free tool live on my platform, Layr,s for free, to help you sharpen your skills. It works for both system design and Coding interviews, check it out here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    The exact number does not matter.
    The outcome matters.

    If you still panic at mediums after 400 problems, your issue is not volume. It is how you practiced.

    Your brain works like a cache. What matters is which patterns are currently cached, not how many total problems you once solved.

    That is why this happens to so many working engineers coming back to DSA:

    - Problems you solved before suddenly feel unfamiliar
    - Confidence drops even after hundreds of solved questions
    - You waste hours on random lists, thinking you are progressing
    - Syntax feels rusty
    - Memory feels unreliable

    This is normal.
    DSA is not a once-and-done skill.
    It decays without focused retrieval and pattern reinforcement.

    If you are confused about how many problems you should solve, use this simple filter:

    Do not ask,
    “How many problems have I solved?”

    Ask,
    “Can I solve unfamiliar medium problems calmly within 20 to 25 minutes?”

    If the answer is no, keep practicing.
    If the answer is yes, you are interview-ready.

    Volume without pattern control creates false confidence.
    Pattern control creates real offers.

    If you are restarting DSA prep after years in a full-time job, you are not behind.
    You just need a better system than brute force repetition.

    And yes, your brain really is just an LRU cache.
    The goal is to keep the right patterns hot.

  • Feed post number 76

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • A candidate interviewing for a Senior Engineer @ Meta was asked to design a Distributed Stream Processing System like Kafka. Another candidate at Google's L5 loop got hit with the same question.
  • If this question shows up in your system design interview, you are not being tested on config flags. You are being tested on whether you understand when to use it and what tradeoffs you are making.
  • Btw, If you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:http://layrs.me/interviews

  • Here is how I would structure a clear answer. (Note: I am not going into nuances, this is a very high-level explanation)

    1. What problem are we solving

    At a high level, Kafka is my go-to when I need:

    - A central backbone for events or logs coming from many services
    - High throughput writes with ordering per key
    - Consumers that can read at their own pace and be added or removed independently
    - The ability to replay history using offsets

    Typical use cases I call out:

    - Asynchronous workflows
    - Central logging and metrics
    - Ad click or analytics streams
    - Pub sub for notifications or chat

    That already tells the interviewer I know -why- Kafka is in the picture.

    2. Requirements

    Functional

    - Accept events from many producers in different services and regions
    - Preserve order for events that share the same key
    - Allow many independent consumers
    +some doing real time processing
    +some doing batch jobs or analytics
    - Support replay from an offset for backfills and reprocessing

    Non functional
    - Handle very high write rates
    - Scale horizontally by adding machines
    - Survive broker failures without losing committed data
    - Let us tune retention for cost vs replay needs

    Rest of the breakdown:     https://lnkd.in/gmg7u-ku

  • Feed post number 77

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • If you copy Netflix’s system design to build YouTube, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy WhatsApp’s backend to build Slack, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Flipkart’s database architecture to build Amazon Web Services, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Twitter’s real-time feed design to build LinkedIn, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Zomato’s scaling approach to build Swiggy’s delivery engine, it will 100% fail.

  • Why?

    Because two products can look similar from the outside, but beneath the UI, their workloads, scaling challenges, and priorities are totally different.

    Let’s break it down with an example:

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design or coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Netflix vs. YouTube might look similar. But they’re not even close.

    Netflix

    ↳ Long-form, pre-produced content (think 30–120 min episodes)
    ↳ Content is published at known intervals, rarely “bursts”
    ↳ 99% of the time, everyone is watching the same files (“Stranger Things” S3E1 is the same everywhere)
    ↳ Heavy pre-caching via global CDNs can predict demand days in advance
    ↳ Personalization drives what you see, but not what you watch, the catalog is mostly static

    YouTube

    ↳ Wild mix: 30-second shorts to 12-hour live streams
    ↳ Anyone, anywhere, can upload anytime, real “unpredictable” traffic
    ↳ Viral spikes: one new video can draw 50M views in hours, worldwide
    ↳ UGC (User Generated Content) = constant writes, real-time metadata, comments, notifications
    ↳ CDN can help, but can’t pre-cache a trending video that didn’t exist an hour ago
    ↳ Personalization, search, and real-time engagement are mission-critical

    So, even though both platforms stream video at scale:
    Netflix can “cheat” by pre-caching and optimizing for a mostly fixed catalog.

    YouTube must handle unpredictable uploads, real-time spikes, and millions of tiny metadata writes per second. Copy Netflix’s approach, and YouTube collapses the next time a viral video drops.

    System design is not “CTRL+C, CTRL+V.”


    It’s a deep analysis:
    – What are my real bottlenecks?
    – What’s predictable vs. spiky?
    – Is my traffic read-heavy or write-heavy?
    – What must be consistent vs. eventually consistent?
    – Where do my users’ needs change week to week?

    If you build for someone else’s scale, problems, and team,
    You’ll inherit their tradeoffs, and your system will break in ways you never anticipated.

    Never build by imitation. Build by understanding.

  • Feed post number 78

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • 40 niche system design problems that will teach you 80% of the important concepts that keep repeating across system design interviews at Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, Uber and other top companies.
  • System design gets a lot easier once you know the fundamentals. It gets even better when you learn them by solving problems and seeing how pieces fit together.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design or coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • [1] Set 1 – Request and data flow

    - Design a URL shortener API
    ➸ Client server model, REST APIs, reverse proxy, load balancer

    - Design a file upload service for web and mobile
    ➸ REST APIs, API gateway, rate limiting

    - Design internal RPC calls between microservices
    ➸ gRPC, service registry, load balancer

    - Design a public partner API platform
    ➸ API gateway, authentication, rate limiting

    - Design an edge layer for mobile clients across regions
    ➸ Reverse proxy, load balancer, request routing

    - Design service discovery inside a Kubernetes cluster
    ➸ Service registry, health checks, client server

    - Design a multi version backend for old app clients
    ➸ API gateway, routing, backward compatibility

    - Design throttling for a “send OTP” endpoint
    ➸ Rate limiting, API gateway, reverse proxy

    [2] Set 2 – Storage and data modeling

    - Design user profile storage for 500M users
    ➸ Relational databases, indexes, normalization

    - Design a product catalogue with flexible attributes
    ➸ NoSQL databases, document modeling, secondary indexes

    - Design a sharded order database for an ecommerce app
    ➸ Sharding, data partitioning, relational DB

    - Design read heavy analytics views on top of OLTP data
    ➸ Read write splitting, replication, materialized views

    - Design cross region replication for a payments ledger
    ➸ Replication, consistency, distributed transactions

    - Design an audit log that never loses events
    ➸ Append only tables, event sourcing, retention strategy

    - Design a time series store for metrics
    ➸ Partitioning by time, indexes, cold vs hot storage

    - Design soft delete and restore for user generated content
    ➸ Data modeling, secondary indexes, retention policies

    [3] Set 3 – Caching and performance

    - Design caching for a product detail page
    ➸ In memory cache, cache aside pattern

    - Design a trending feed that updates every few minutes
    ➸ Cache aside, write through cache, TTL strategy

    - Design a “recently viewed items” widget
    ➸ In memory cache, eviction policy, pagination

    - Design CDN support for image and video delivery
    ➸ Content delivery network, origin shield, cache keys

    - Design rate limited search suggestions
    ➸ In memory cache, connection pooling, back pressure

    - Design leaderboard queries at scale
    ➸ Sorted set cache, pagination, cache aside

    - Design write heavy notification counters
    ➸ Write back cache, batching, durability trade offs

    Continued ↓

  • The Avg. Comp for Meta E5 Level in India is over 75 Lakhs+, including bonuses and stocks, it goes over 1Cr+, and this is just at the Sr. Software Engineer level.
  • That is what Meta puts on the table if you can clear the process and it’s not too complicated:
  • - There are 2 DSA Rounds
  • - There is 1 System Design Round
  • - There is 1 Managerial / Behavioural Round

    Note: They’re also testing a new AI-based round, where you’re free to use AI but have to deliver a full functioning app in limited time.

    But they will grill you beyond what you can imagine, and that’s why you need bulletproof fundamentals, especially with DSA and System Design.

    If I were targeting Meta E5, these are the resources I would use to study up on my fundamentals for system design:

    Before we get into resources, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • Feed post number 80

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo • Edited •
  • Imagine you're sitting in an interview for an SDE-III role at Amazon, and you're asked: "Design a social network like Facebook."
  • Here's how I'd walk through it. We won't cover everything, just the high-level design that shows you understand the hard problems.
  • Btw, if you are preparing for coding or system design interviews, you can use our mock interview tool for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  •  1. Requirements

    The classic pain points in social networks:

    - The News Feed is stale or irrelevant because the system can't rank and personalize posts efficiently at scale
    - Fan-out is brutal. When a celebrity posts, millions of followers need to see it almost instantly
    - Media is slow because images and videos aren't served from the right layer
    - Notifications feel delayed because the eventing system isn't designed for real-time delivery

    Functional requirements:
    - User registration, profile creation, and updates
    - Add friends / follow users (bidirectional vs. unidirectional relationships)
    - Create posts: text, images, videos, links
    - News Feed: a personalized, ranked stream of posts from friends and followed pages
    - Like, comment, and share on posts
    - Real-time notifications (new likes, comments, friend requests)
    - Search for users, pages, and posts

    Non-functional requirements:
    - Low latency (the Feed should load in under a second)
    - Horizontal scalability (billions of users, peaks during global events)
    - High availability, Facebook going down is a news story
    - Eventual consistency is acceptable (a like count that's off by a few for a few seconds is fine)
    - Strong consistency where it matters (friend relationships, privacy settings)

    Rough numbers to anchor the design:
    - ~3 billion monthly active users, ~2 billion daily
    - Hundreds of millions of posts per day
    - Trillions of News Feed loads per day
    - Read-to-write ratio is extremely skewed toward reads (~100:1 or higher)
    - Petabytes of media storage

    2. High-Level Architecture

    Clean service boundaries behind a single entry point:

    - Client (web, iOS, Android)
    - API Gateway (routing, auth, rate limiting)
    - User Service (profiles, friend graph)
    - Post Service (create, store, retrieve posts)
    - Media Service (image/video upload and delivery via CDN)
    - Feed Service (generate and serve personalized News Feeds)
    - Notification Service (real-time and push notifications)
    - Search Service (full-text search over users and posts)

    Every client request hits the Gateway first. It validates the JWT token, enforces rate limits, and routes to the right service. Services talk to each other through well-defined internal APIs, not directly to the client.

    Rest of the post is here:     https://lnkd.in/gJRdhKuw

  • Feed post number 81

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Back in October,  my friend’s colleague interviewed for the SDE-1 role at Microsoft & the interview process was shocking!! (LLD + DSA question in Managerial rounds)
  • He did not get the offer in the end, but his breakdown of the process will be helpful for anyone preparing right now.
  • Btw, if you are preparing for coding or system design interviews, you can use our mock interview tool for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • ➦ Interview Timeline

    Total duration: ~3 to 4 weeks from application to final result
    Applied through the Microsoft careers portal
    Got an Online Assessment link within about a week

    ➦ Round structure

    ➸Online Assessment – 2 DSA questions

    Format:
    - 2 coding questions
    - Focus on data structures and algorithms
    - One string-based problem
    - One stacks and queues style problem

    ➸ Round 1 – DSA interview (Senior engineer)

    Topic (high level):
    > Given a matrix of 0s and 1s, you can flip at most k zeroes to ones. Find the maximum area of 1s you can get after the operations.

    Notes:
    - Interviewer was a senior developer
    - Round lasted about 60 minutes
    - Interviewer was not very interactive
    - Limited hints, very straight face

    What went wrong (his view):
    - Got nervous midway
    - Found it harder to explain thinking without feedback from the interviewer
    - Knew parts of the approach, struggled to land it cleanly in time

    ➸ Round 2 – Low Level Design (LLD)

    Interviewer: Senior developer

    Prompt (high level):
    > Design a Search URL system.

    What they looked for:
    - How he models data and entities
    - How he thinks about APIs and flows
    - How he handles scale at a low level
    - Ability to reason about edge cases and extensibility

    This round went much better. The interviewer was friendly, asked follow up questions, and the conversation felt like a real design discussion instead of a quiz.

    Round 3 – Managerial plus DSA (Principal engineer)

    First half – Managerial/behavioural:
    - Discussion on past projects
    - How he works with teams
    - How he handles pressure and conflict
    - How he approaches problem solving in general

    Second half – Surprise DSA question:
    > Two strings a and b.
    > From each string, you can form a palindrome using its characters.
    > Then you pick one palindrome from a and one from b, combine their characters and form a new palindrome.
    > Find the length of the largest palindrome you can form.
    > Constraints: 1 ≤ m, n ≤ 100000

    What happened:
    - He did not expect a DSA question in a managerial round
    - Got stuck under time pressure
    - Realised the count array-based solution five minutes after the call

    Overall feeling:
    - Managerial conversation felt strong
    - DSA performance pulled the overall signal down

    ➦ Result

    - Email after ~10 days
    - Not selected

    Disappointing, but also very clear where the gaps were.

  • Feed post number 82

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • If I go back to my interview days, I will study these 40 questions to get ready for system design concepts that interviewers grill you on, especially at Senior level roles at MAANG+ companies.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, do check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • 1. What is difference between API Gateway and Load Balancer?
  • 2. What is difference between Reverse Proxy and Forward Proxy?
    3. What is difference between Horizontal scaling and vertical scaling?
    4. What is difference between Microservices and Monolithic architecture?
    5. What is difference between vertical and horizontal partitioning?
    6. What is Rate Limiter? How does it work?
    7. How does Single Sign On (SSO) works?
    8. How does Apache Kafka works? Why is it so fast?
    9. Difference between Kafka, ActiveMQ, and RabbitMQ?
    10. Difference between JWT, OAuth, and SAML?
    11. What is difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
    12. How does Content Delivery Network (CDN) work?
    13. What is difference between Synchronous and Asynchronous communication?
    14. How does Database Sharding work?
    15. What is difference between REST and GraphQL?
    16. How does Caching work? What are caching strategies?
    17. What is difference between Strong and Eventual Consistency?
    18. How does Message Queue work?
    19. What is difference between TCP and UDP?
    20. How does Database Replication work?
    21. What is difference between Stateful and Stateless architecture?
    22. How does Circuit Breaker pattern work?
    23. What is difference between Authentication and Authorization?
    24. How does Distributed Tracing work?
    25. What is difference between ACID and BASE properties?
    26. How does Service Discovery work?
    27. What is difference between Push and Pull architecture?
    28. How does Database Indexing work?
    29. What is difference between Batch and Stream processing?
    30. How does Consistent Hashing work?
    31. What is difference between RPC and REST?
    32. How does Leader Election work in distributed systems?
    33. What is difference between Optimistic and Pessimistic locking?
    34. How does Two Phase Commit work?
    35. What is difference between CAP theorem components?
    36. How does Bloom Filter work?
    37. What is difference between WebSocket and HTTP?
    38. How does Service Mesh work?
    39. What is difference between Blue Green and Canary deployment?
    40. How does Distributed Cache work?

    Bookmark this. Review before your next system design interview.

    What other questions would you add to this list?
