  • Feed post number 83

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • I’m going to be honest: system design is hard, and it feels worse when someone says, “Design Instagram or YouTube in 45 minutes.”
  • Because your brain goes, wait… entire teams spent years scaling this thing, and I am supposed to rebuild it in one meeting?
  • And I get it, but SD rounds are built this way for a reason.

  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, do check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    They are checking if you can:
    - Ask sharp questions and cut the scope
    - Break a messy product into clear components
    - Pick sane building blocks for the scale they mention
    - Talk through tradeoffs when asked to defend your choices

    In your day job, you will not wake up and design WhatsApp from scratch. You will usually own a slice of a bigger system - a feed service, a notification pipeline, a storage layer, an API gateway, a search feature.

    So the game in system design interviews is not “invent a brand new architecture.”

    The game is:
    - Learn the common patterns once: caching, queues, pub-sub, sharding, CDNs, rate limiting, indexing
    - Study a handful of classic problems: news feed, chat, file storage, URL shortener, notification system
    - Practice a simple structure: requirements → high level design → data model → detailed flows → bottlenecks and tradeoffs

    Once you know the building blocks, “Design Instagram” turns into:
    design a feed, media storage, auth, and a few extra features, at a given scale.

    Hard, yes.
    Impossible, no.

  • Feed post number 84

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • If I wanted to land a Sr. Engineer role at Microsoft in the next 4–5 months and I was starting my preparation from 0, this is exactly how I would do it (based on my experience with cracking Google and Flipkart).
  • [1] Phase 1 – Understand the bar and fix fundamentals (Week 0–2)
  • ● Read the actual process for L63–L64
  • - Expect: recruiter screen, online assessment or phone screen, then 2–3 coding rounds, 1 system design, and behavioral signals inside every round.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, do check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    ● Choose your primary language and clean up basics
    - Practice arrays, strings, trees, graphs, recursion, DP, and common patterns.
    - Start from questions that actually show up at Microsoft: LRU Cache, Merge Sorted Array, Spiral Matrix, Meeting Rooms II, Trapping Rain Water.

    ● Train in a plain editor
    - No autocomplete, no linting, no fancy IDE features.
    - Get used to reading your own code and debugging without strong tooling.

    [2] Phase 2 – Online assessment and coding rounds prep (Week 2–8)
    ● Simulate the online test
    - Two problems per session: one medium, one hard.
    - Goal: correct and efficient solutions within time, not brute force that times out.

    ● Build a focused problem list
    - Around 70–90 LeetCode style questions that cover the main data structures.
    - For each problem, practice: restating the problem, identifying pattern, writing clean code, and testing edge cases.

    ● Practice live coding style
    - Explain with a small example first, then code.
    - Say your complexity out loud and call out corner cases before the interviewer asks.

    ● Avoid classic failure modes
    - Do not start typing before clarifying constraints.
    - Do not spend 35 minutes on the first problem and leave the second untouched.
    - Do not hide confusion; use the collaborative nature of the interview and ask clarifying questions.

    [3] Phase 3 – System design at senior level (Week 4–12)

    ● Build a repeatable system design framework

    - Start with requirements and constraints, then data model, APIs, high level architecture, and only then scaling and reliability.

    ● Practice with Microsoft style problems
    - Chat app, distributed cache, logger system, job scheduler, “design LeetCode.”
    - For each, talk about scale, access patterns, read vs write, and failure cases.

    ● Get comfortable making assumptions
    - Seniors are expected to move forward when specs are fuzzy.
    - Say your assumption, design with it, and adjust when the interviewer pushes back.

    ● Learn the tech you name
    - If you say Kafka, Redis, Cosmos DB, CDN, or JWT, be ready to explain why you chose it and
    how it behaves at a deeper level.

    ● Do timed mocks
    - One hour per design, with interruptions and follow up questions, just like a real loop.

    Continued ↓

  • System design gets a lot easier after you learn these 40 major concepts that keep repeating in interviews and design discussions.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, do check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • [1] Request and data flow basics
  • - Client-server model
    - REST APIs
    - gRPC
    - Load balancer
    - Reverse proxy
    - API gateway
    - Service registry
    - Rate limiting

    [2] Storage and data modeling

    - Relational databases
    - NoSQL databases
    - Sharding
    - Indexes
    - Replication
    - Read write splitting
    - Distributed transactions
    - Event sourcing

    [3] Caching and performance

    - In memory cache
    - Cache aside pattern
    - Write through cache
    - Write back cache
    - Content delivery network
    - Connection pooling
    - Pagination
    - Back pressure

    [4] Reliability and fault tolerance

    - Health checks
    - Heartbeats
    - Leader election
    - Replication factor
    - Data partitioning
    - Quorum reads
    - Circuit breaker
    - Retry with exponential backoff

    [5] Distributed systems and messaging

    - Message queues
    - Publish subscribe pattern
    - Idempotency
    - Exactly once semantics
    - Eventual consistency
    - Saga pattern
    - Distributed locks
    - Observability pillars

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • If you interview at Google for mid-to-senior roles, nearly every other round tests your system design skills.
  • At Amazon? More than half of your interviews is about system design.
  • At Microsoft? You can expect up to 60% of your rounds to go deep on architecture and design.
  • At Meta? 70%+ of questions will challenge how you build and scale real systems.
  • At Uber? Same story, system design is the backbone of the process.

    The more senior the role, the more system design dominates the interview, and with AI accelerating everything, even SDE-1s will soon need to face LLD questions.

    Here are the most asked questions, which will train you on the fundamentals that are continuously tested.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, do check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

     1. Social Networks & Messaging

    ↬ Design Twitter Feed
    ↬ Design Instagram Feed
    ↬ Design WhatsApp Messaging
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design Twitter Direct Messages
    ↬ Design Instagram Stories
    ↬ Design Instagram Ads
    ↬ Design Twitter Notifications
    ↬ Design Tinder
    ↬ Design Reddit
    ↬ Design Facebook News Feed
    ↬ Design LinkedIn Connections
    ↬ Design Notification Service
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger Payments

    2. Media Streaming & Recommendations

    ↬ Design Spotify Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Recommendations
    ↬ Design YouTube Streaming
    ↬ Design Spotify Connect
    ↬ Design Spotify Discover Weekly
    ↬ Design Instagram Reels
    ↬ Design TikTok Feed
    ↬ Design YouTube
    ↬ Design Amazon Recommendations

    3. E-commerce & Marketplaces

    ↬ Design Amazon Shopping Cart
    ↬ Design Amazon E-commerce Store
    ↬ Design Shopify Store
    ↬ Design Flight Booking System
    ↬ Design Stock Exchange System
    ↬ Design Payment System
    ↬ Design Uber Eats
    ↬ Design Uber Dispatch System
    ↬ Design Food Delivery App
    ↬ Design Uber Pool
    ↬ Design Uber Freight
    ↬ Design Booking System (BookMyShow)

    4. File Storage, Collaboration & Productivity

    ↬ Design Google Drive
    ↬ Design Google Docs
    ↬ Design Dropbox File Sharing
    ↬ Design Distributed Cloud Storage (S3)
    ↬ Design Online Code Editor
    ↬ Design Code Deployment System
    ↬ Design File Sharing System
    ↬ Design Distributed Web Crawler

    5. Infrastructure & Distributed Systems

    ↬ Design Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)
    ↬ Design Amazon SQS
    ↬ Design Distributed Locking Service
    ↬ Design Amazon EC2
    ↬ Design Amazon DynamoDB
    ↬ Design Amazon Redshift
    ↬ Design AWS S3
    ↬ Design Google Analytics
    ↬ Design Analytics Platform (Metrics & Logging)

    Check comments for more questions ↓

  • Feed post number 87

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Imagine you're sitting in an interview for an L5/L6 Software Engineer role at Google, and you're asked this question: "Design Uber for a metro city."
  • Here's how I would break it down if I were the one being interviewed. There are lots of aspects we can cover here, but I'll only cover the high-level design.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  •  1. Requirements

    The classic pain points in ride-hailing apps:
    - Matching is slow because the system can't efficiently find the nearest available driver from a pool of hundreds of thousands in real time
    - Consistency is fragile because concurrent ride requests can accidentally assign the same driver to multiple riders
    - Location tracking is expensive because millions of driver pings per minute overwhelm traditional databases

    Functional requirements
    - Rider inputs a start location and destination, gets a fare estimate and ETA
    - Rider requests a ride based on that estimate and gets matched with a nearby available driver in real time
    - Driver receives a ride request notification and can accept or deny it
    - Driver navigates to the pickup location, then to the destination, updating status along the way
    - Drivers periodically send their current location so the system knows where they are

    Out of scope (worth mentioning to show product sense)
    - Multiple car types (UberXL, Black, etc.) , we'll assume one tier
    - Ratings and reviews for drivers and riders
    - Scheduled rides in advance
    - Surge pricing algorithms
    - Payment processing

    Non-functional requirements
    - Low-latency matching: under 1 minute from ride request to driver match (or a failure message)
    - Consistency of matching: a ride is matched to exactly one driver, and a driver is never double-booked across rides
    - High availability outside of matching: the rest of the system (fare estimates, location updates, navigation) should be up at all times
    - High throughput in surges: the system must handle hundreds of thousands of requests within a region during festivals or special events

    Rest of the breakdown:     https://lnkd.in/gieR-EqR

  • Feed post number 88

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • He cleared both the Meta and Google interview rounds but still got rejected in the team matching phase and failed to secure an offer. Unbelievable, but this can happen to anyone in 2026.
  • You can do everything “right” and still not have an offer in hand.
  • At that stage, the only rational move is what he is doing now. Keep moving forward.  The good news: ML and AI infra roles are only going to grow.

  • For these domain-specific roles, you will almost always face ML system design and practical ML questions, not just basic DSA.

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Here’s a list of resources to help you:

    1) ML basics - CS229 videos by Stanford
    https://lnkd.in/gH_UfCC7

    2) ML infra and systems
    https://lnkd.in/gNNdGnKa

    3) ML system design case studies
    https://lnkd.in/gGHxAZpm

    After that, do a small practical project in 2 weeks and put it on your CV. Even a focused project gives you something real to talk about in ML design rounds.

    4) Domain fundamentals you should know for ML and AI infra interviews
    1. Difference between online and offline inference
    2. Batch vs real time prediction pipelines
    3. How to design a data labeling workflow
    4. Feature stores: online vs offline features and why they must be consistent
    5. Training serving skew and how to avoid it
    6. How to choose and track prediction targets (labels, windows, leakage)
    7. Common data quality checks before training
    8. Model evaluation metrics: classification, regression, ranking, recommendation
    9. A/B tests for models and what to watch beyond accuracy
    10. How to design a feature pipeline on top of data warehouses or streams
    11. Caching for inference: when memoization or key based caching helps
    12. Model versioning and rollback strategies
    13. Canary release of new models and shadow deployment
    14. Monitoring for ML: drift, data distribution, output stability, business KPIs
    15. Logging what you need to debug wrong predictions later
    16. Basics of distributed training, data parallel vs model parallel ideas
    17. GPU vs CPU tradeoffs and simple resource planning
    18. How to design an embedding service and vector store usage
    19. Rate limiting and scaling an inference API under unpredictable traffic
    20. Handling failures in downstream calls, timeouts, and fallbacks for predictions
    21. Security basics: protecting models, feature data, and PII in pipelines
    22. How to integrate models into existing services without blocking the whole request path
    23. When to call an external model provider vs host your own
    24. Cost awareness for ML systems: storage, compute, network, and how to reduce waste
    25. How you would explain an ML system design to product and infra engineers in simple language

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • DSA gets you shortlisted. But it’s System Design that gets you the big offers, especially in 2026, where the focus will be growing even more on LLD and HLD.
  • Here is a list of 50+ questions on essential LLD, so you can cut down the time and learn fast. These are from various interview experiences I came across during my research.
  • Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  •  1. Resource and booking systems

    Pattern: limited resources, time slots, no double booking, fair allocation.

    - Design a Parking Lot
    - Design a Parking Lot - Multi Threaded
    - Design a Simple Elevator System - Multiple Lifts
    - Design Elevator Management System - Single Lift
    - Design an Elevator System - Request Feasibility (Single Lift)
    - Design a Train Platform Management System
    - Design a Meeting Room Reservation System
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler - List Bookings
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler for Recurrent Meetings
    - Design a Car Rental System
    - Design Locker Management System for Warehouse Packages
    - Design a Movie ticket booking system like BookMyShow

    2. Money, commerce and pricing flows

    Pattern: orders, payments, balances, revenue split, leaderboards around money or points.

    - Design a restaurant food ordering system like Zomato, Swiggy, DoorDash
    - Design expense sharing app like Splitwise
    - Design Stock Trading Platform like Zerodha, Groww
    - Design a Shopping Cart
    - Design a Payment Wallet like PayPal
    - Design an order and inventory management system - Multi Threaded
    - Design Product Plan Cost Explorer
    - Design a customer support agent rating leaderboard
    - Design a Leaderboard for Fantasy Teams
    - Design Last Lap Hero (F1 Race Tracker)

    3. Editors, spreadsheets and office tools

    Pattern: document state, operations on content, formulas, undo or recalculation.

    - Design a Text Editor or Word Processor like Microsoft Word
    - Design a Text Editor with Undo and Redo
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations and Basic Editing
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations
    - Design spreadsheet like Microsoft Excel
    - Design Excel Sum Formula

    4. File systems, search and storage

    Pattern: hierarchical paths, metadata, indexing, queries on files or logs.

    - Design Unix `find` command for file search
    - Design Unix `find` command with boolean predicates
    - Design File System (cd with `-`)
    - Design File System
    - Design In Memory File System
    - Design File Collections Tracker
    - Design Log Storage System

    5. Search, lookup and dictionaries

    Pattern: key based lookup, tries, prefix search.

    - Design Dictionary App to store words and their meanings
    - Design Phone Directory
    - Design Google Search Autocomplete

  • Feed post number 90

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Imagine you're sitting in an Interview for an SDE-III role at Meta, and you're asked this question: "Design a dating application like Tinder or Bumble."
  • Here's how I would break it down if I were the one being interviewed. There are lots of aspects we can cover here, but I'll only cover the high-level design.
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • 1. Requirements

    The classic pain in dating apps:
    - Recommendations are bad because the system doesn't efficiently filter and rank profiles based on user preferences
    - Matching logic is fragile because swipe data is scattered and hard to reconcile in real time
    - Chat feels sluggish because the messaging layer isn't designed for persistent, bidirectional connections

    Functional requirements
    - User registration, profile creation, and profile updates (bio, photos, preferences)
    - Image uploads and retrieval (multiple photos per user)
    - Set preferences: age range, gender orientation, location radius
    - Swipe left (pass) or right (like) on recommended profiles
    - Match when two users mutually right-swipe each other
    - Real-time chat between matched users
    - Recommendation engine that surfaces the most relevant profiles

    Non-functional requirements
    - Low latency (swiping and chatting must feel instant)
    - Horizontal scalability (millions of users, spiky evening traffic)
    - High availability (the app should be usable at all times)
    - Eventual consistency is acceptable (profile updates don't need to propagate instantly)
    - Security and privacy (images, location data, and conversations must be protected)

    If you want numbers to anchor the discussion, I usually assume something like:
    - Active users: tens of millions monthly, millions daily
    - Swipes: hundreds of millions per day
    - Matches: millions per day
    - Messages: tens of millions per day
    - Image storage: multiple TB and growing
    - Availability target: four to five 9s

    Rest of the breakdown in detail is here:     https://lnkd.in/gSi6iN66

  • Feed post number 91

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • My friend's colleague recently joined Google as an L5 - Senior Software Engineer (in the AI/ML domain) in USA last year. I recently connected with him on LinkedIn, Sharing his interview experience from his successful attempt.
  • Background: 6 YOE in ML/AI roles
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • ➦Interview Timeline

    Total Duration: ~3 months from application to offer

    Application Response:
    Within 1 hour of applying (second attempt after 6-month cooldown)

    ➦Round Structure

    ↳Rounds 1 & 2: Coding (2 weeks prep time)

    Format: 45 minutes each, standard Google CoderPad

    Topics Covered:
    - Backtracking with caching optimization
    - Sliding window variation
    - Topological sorting

    Feedback: "Lean hire" on both (still passed to next rounds)

    Prep Strategy:
    - LeetCode Premium - Google top 100 (Medium/Hard only)
    - Focused on: graphs, DP with memoization, sliding window patterns
    - 2-3 weeks of 2-3 problems daily

    ↳Round 3: Coding

    Topic: Topological sorting problem

    Feedback: Strong positive

    ↳Round 4: System Design (ML-focused)
    Duration: 45 minutes

    Topic: ML System Design - NLP-related application

    What they looked for:
    - Model serving architecture
    - Feature pipelines and feature stores
    - A/B testing setup for ML models
    - Monitoring and retraining triggers
    - Trade-offs between batch vs real-time inference

    Feedback: Strong positive

    ↳Round 5: Googlyness & Leadership
    Duration: 45 minutes

    Format: Pure behavioral using STAR method

    Questions covered:
    - Cross-functional collaboration
    - Handling ambiguity
    - Conflict resolution
    - Technical decision-making with business impact
    - Mentorship examples

    Prep: YouTube content + structured stories with ChatGPT for STAR format
    Feedback: Strong positive

    ↳Team Matching
    Duration: 1 month (fast!)

    Process:
    - 2 fitment calls with the same team
    - Discussed previous projects and the team's current challenges
    - Both calls were more conversational, less technical

    The hiring Committee requested one more coding round due to two "lean hire" signals. It was scheduled 2 months after the last interview
    Result: Passed

    Offer Details
    - Level: L5 (not down-leveled)
    - Comp: 20% bump (equity was lower due to recent comp cuts)
    - Domain: ML Infrastructure

    Bottom line: For L5, all four areas matter - DSA, System Design, ML knowledge, and Behavioral. You can't max one and ignore others. Prepare holistically.

  • Feed post number 92

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Learning and grinding 400 DSA problems might get you 20-30 LPA offers...
  • But it's system design that will take you from 30 to 60-80LPA+ as an SWE.
  • Here are the 20% of topics that cover 80% of ML/AI & Backend role system design rounds.
  • Also, check out our mock interview tool on Layrs, it will actually help you practice and understand these concepts in real time. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    1. Load Balancer
    Key Topics:
    - Types of Load Balancers - Application Layer (L7) vs Network Layer (L4)
    - Algorithms - Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hashing, Weighted distribution
    - Health Checks - Monitoring server availability and performance
    - Sticky Sessions - Keeping user sessions tied to specific servers
    - Scaling Strategies - Horizontal vs Vertical scaling with load balancers
    - Global Load Balancers - Handling traffic across multiple regions
    - Reverse Proxy - Serving as a gateway and caching responses

    2. Application Server
    Key Topics:
    - Stateless vs Stateful Servers - When to use which
    - Caching Strategies - In-memory caching (Redis/Memcached) and local caching
    - Session Management - Cookies vs Tokens (JWT)
    - Concurrency Handling - Managing multiple requests with threads or async models
    - Microservices Architecture - Service discovery and inter-service communication
    - Containerization - Docker, Kubernetes, and deployment strategies
    - Rate Limiting & Throttling - Preventing abuse and managing traffic bursts

    3. Database (SQL vs NoSQL)
    Key Topics:
    - SQL vs NoSQL - When to choose which database type
    - Sharding and Partitioning - Horizontal scaling techniques
    - Replication - Primary-Secondary, Multi-Master setups for reliability
    - Consistency Models - Strong vs Eventual Consistency (CAP theorem)
    - Indexing Strategies - Improving query performance
    - Caching Layers - Redis, Memcached for faster reads
    - Backup and Recovery - Disaster recovery planning and failover systems

    4. Message Queue & Pub-Sub Systems
    Key Topics:
    - Messaging Patterns - Pub-Sub vs Queue-based systems
    - Message Brokers - Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS/SNS
    - Idempotency - Avoiding duplicate message processing
    - Durability and Ordering - Ensuring messages aren't lost or misordered
    - Dead Letter Queues - Handling failed messages
    - Scaling Consumers - Parallel processing and worker pools
    - Eventual Consistency - Maintaining consistency with asynchronous systems

    5. Content Delivery Network (CDN)
    Key Topics:
    - How CDNs Work - Edge caching and reducing latency
    - Caching Policies - TTL (Time-to-Live) and Cache Invalidation
    - Geolocation Routing - Serving content from nearest data centers
    - Static vs Dynamic Content Delivery - Optimizing for both
    - SSL/TLS Termination - Secure communication
    - Load Distribution - Managing spikes in traffic
    - DDoS Protection - Preventing attacks and ensuring availability

  • Feed post number 93

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • In 2023, Amazon Prime Video moved its audio/video monitoring service from a microservices architecture to a monolith and saved about 90% in costs.
  • They did this because the new design scaled better for that use case, cost less, and proved more resilient. In system design, Monolith vs Microservices has always been a hot topic.
  • If you are preparing for system design interviews, this will almost surely come up, so let’s break it down and see when you need each and why you might break the monolith or move back to it.

  • Also, check out our mock interview tool on Layrs, it will actually help you practice this in real time. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    [1] Monolith

    Think of a monolith as one big application that does everything.

    When it shines:

    - Early stage product, small team, fast iteration.
    - You want one repo, one deploy pipeline, one logging surface.
    - You want to debug locally without spinning up half a data center.

    Pros you should mention in interviews:

    - Simple to build and deploy.
    - Easier debugging and tracing.
    - No network hops between modules, so less latency.
    - Cheaper and simpler for small and medium scale workloads.

    When it starts to hurt:

    - Codebase becomes huge, onboarding takes weeks.
    - A tiny change needs full regression testing and full app redeploy.
    - One hot feature forces you to scale the entire app, which is expensive.

    [2] Microservices

    Microservices split that big app into many small services, each owning one clear responsibility (billing, search, recommendations, notifications, etc).

    When it shines:

    - Many teams working in parallel on different parts of the system.
    - Some features are much hotter than others and need independent scaling.
    - You want independent deploys, faster release cycles, and fault isolation.

    Pros to call out:

    - Each service is smaller and easier to reason about.
    - A failure in one area does not always take down the whole product.
    - You can scale only the hot services, which saves money at large scale.

    Costs that come with it:

    - Extra latency and failure modes from network calls.
    - Much harder local development story.
    - Complex infra: multiple pipelines, monitoring, service discovery, service mesh.
    - Higher base cost for small workloads, because every service needs infra.

    [3] How to talk about it in interviews?

    Start from the product context: traffic, team size, reliability needs, and how fast requirements change.

    - For a new system with unclear demand, say you would start with a modular monolith, with clear boundaries inside the codebase so it is easy to split later.

    - For a mature system with many teams and uneven load, explain which parts you would extract first into microservices and why (hot paths, different scaling profile, clearer SLAs).

    A good signal is being able to explain what you gain and what you pay with each choice, and when it actually makes sense to move in either direction.

  • Feed post number 94

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Microsoft for an Sr. Engineer role on a cloud team.
  • The interviewer smiles and asks:
  • “GitHub stores tens of millions of repositories. How would you design the storage layer so a single machine dying does not lose data or take the site down?”
  • Here is how you can break it down:

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    1] Start from the product goals

    For a repo host like GitHub the storage layer must give you:

    - Durability: once a push is accepted, those commits must never disappear
    - High availability: clones, pulls and pushes should keep working even if disks, servers or racks fail
    - Consistency: every replica of a repo should see the same history and branch state
    - Operability: easy repair, rebalancing and planned maintenance across thousands of file servers

    So your system answers one key question:
    “How do we keep every repo safe and online when individual machines are unreliable?”

    2] Naive design – one big file server

    You can start with a simple idea:

    - Store each repo as a bare Git repo on a shared NFS server
    - Application servers mount that server and read or write directly

    Why this breaks at GitHub scale:

    - Single point of failure and a huge blast radius
    - Very hard to scale to millions of repos and concurrent operations
    - Any disk corruption or bad RAID controller can corrupt many customers at once

    This gives you a place to start, then you level it up.

    3] Realistic design – sharded file servers plus a replication layer

    Now describe something similar to GitHub’s Spokes system:

    - Repos are sharded across many file servers
    - Each repo lives on at least three independent servers in different racks
    - A “replication controller” knows which servers hold each repo

    Write path:

    - User pushes to a repo
    - Controller locates its three replicas
    - Change is applied on all of them using Git primitives
    - The push is acknowledged only after a majority of replicas commit the same result

    This majority write rule gives:

    - Strong consistency for each repo
    - Durability if one server dies right after a push
    - A clear story for concurrent writes: only one write can hold a majority lock at a time

    4] Detecting failures and routing around them

    Next, talk about availability.

    - Each app server tracks file server health using both heartbeats and real traffic
    - If several real requests to a replica fail in a row, that node is treated as “suspect”
    - For any Git operation the router returns an ordered list of replicas
    - If the first one fails, the app retries on the next server

    This gives fast failover without a central single health checker.
    It also lets you separate “one bad file server” from “one confused app server”.

    --
    Continued ↓

  • Feed post number 95

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3mo •
  • Saying you need microservices in a system design interview sounds cool until the Google interviewer asks why the bill is 50,000 dollars per day and it takes four days to debug a simple bug.
  • 17 things to consider before you say the word “microservices” in an interview:
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • 1. If one team of five can build it, a single service is usually fine.
    2. Every network call you add increases latency and failure points.
    3. Each service needs monitoring, dashboards, and alerts of its own.
    4. You now need proper logging and distributed tracing to debug flows.
    5. Local development gets harder, you cannot just run one process anymore.
    6. Data is now split, so you must handle cross service queries carefully.
    7. You lose simple ACID transactions, so think about sagas and compensation.
    8. Schema changes become versioned releases, not quick edits to one DB.
    9. Deployment is now a pipeline of many services, not one Docker image.
    10. You must plan service discovery and configuration management from day one.
    11. Retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers are mandatory, not optional.
    12. Partial failure is normal, so the system must degrade gracefully.
    13. You need a clear ownership map, one team per service, not everyone touching everything.
    14. Cost goes up, every tiny service still needs CPU, memory, and storage.
    15. Security boundaries multiply, you must secure every internal API and secret.
    16. Testing is harder, you need contract tests and good staging environments.
    17. Refactors are slower, because you coordinate changes across many services.

    If you can speak confidently about these trade-offs and justify your choices, then “microservices” sounds senior.

    If not, you are usually safer starting with a simple modular monolith and evolving from there.

  • Feed post number 97

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • A friend of my colleague from Google, who is based in the US, finished interviews for a SWE Product E5 role at Meta London last year.
  • Sharing his interview experience for everyone preparing right now. Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T
  • 1. Phone screen – pure DSA
  • 45 minutes, classic Meta style

    Q1: 3Sum
    - Solved it cleanly with a clear two-pointer approach
    - Dry ran on examples and called out edge cases
    - Got a follow-up about avoiding duplicates, and he explained the idea, but did not fully implement the follow-up

    Q2: Simplify Path style problem
    - Variant of canonical path simplification, current directory plus `cd`
    - He struggled at the start, asked plenty of clarifying questions
    - With the interviewer’s nudges, he got to the optimal stack based solution and dry ran it

    This was still enough to clear the screen, mainly because of clarity, communication and his willingness to ask questions instead of guessing.

    2. Onsite Coding Round 1

    Q1: Merge Sorted Array
    - Straightforward, did in place merging from the end
    - No hints, clean dry run, good variable naming and explanation

    Q2: Diameter of Binary Tree
    - Used DFS with height calculation and a global max diameter
    - Again, no hints needed, walked through tree examples and explained why the solution is O(n)

    3. Onsite Coding Round 2

    Q1: Sliding window average
    - Given a list of integers, return list of averages for window size k
    - He reached the optimal sliding window solution quickly
    - No hints, careful dry run, spoke through overflow and edge cases

    Q2: Missing Element in Sorted Array

    - Had seen it before but forgot the optimal approach
    - Spent time asking questions, then realised sorted input and that binary search is the right tool
    - While coding, a few bugs popped up during dry run
    - Interviewer gave a hint, he fixed it, but the clock ran out

    4. Product Architecture / System Design

    Prompt: Design Dropbox or Google Drive
    - Interviewer pushed on synchronization between multiple machines
    - He walked through trade offs between
    - Request response
    - Server sent events
    - WebSockets
    - Talked about eventual consistency and conflict resolution at a high level

    5. Behavioral Round 1 and 2

    - Two separate behavioral interviews, 45 minutes each
    - Recruiter hinted that one might be a training round for the interviewer, but did not say which
    - He went in with clear stories mapped to ownership, conflict, failure, and cross team work
    - Both rounds felt like natural conversations
    - They asked fewer follow ups
    - He could tie back every story to impact and learning

    His guess is both rounds landed well from a signal point of view, but he will only know for sure at the end of team match.

    The takeaway is simple.

    At E5 level, you cannot treat DSA, coding, system design, and behavioral as four sliders where you max one and ignore the others.

    All four matter.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • Even in 2026, hiring for SDE-1 roles is purely based on DSA skills. Being good enough with DSA as a fresher can land you at:
  • Big Tech
  • 1. Google as SDE-1
  • 2. Amazon as SDE-1
  • 3. Meta as SDE-1 or (E3, Junior Engineer)
    4. Apple as Software Engineer (Entry Level)
    5. Nvidia as Software Engineer (New Grad)

    Strong Product Companies
    1. Adobe as SDE-1
    2. Intuit as SDE-1
    3. ServiceNow as SDE-1
    4. Oracle as SDE-1
    5. SAP Labs as SDE-1
    6. VMware as SDE-1
    7. Qualcomm as SDE-1
    8. Juniper Networks as SDE-1
    9. MediaTek as SDE-1
    10. Samsung R&D India as SDE-1

    Enterprise Tech
    1. IBM as SDE-1
    2. Dell as SDE-1
    3. HPE as SDE-1
    4. Ericsson as SDE-1
    5. Broadcom as SDE-1

    Banks and Fintech
    1. Morgan Stanley as SDE-1
    2. Barclays as SDE-1
    3. Deutsche Bank as SDE-1
    4. HSBC as SDE-1
    5. American Express as SDE-1

    DSA still runs SDE-1 hiring in 2026 because companies need a fast, standard filter when thousands of people apply for the same role. If you’re genuinely strong at DSA as a fresher, you can get into interview loops at the companies above.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for a coding interview, check out our free Mock Interview tool that tests your skills and helps you perform better:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Just don’t confuse “clearing DSA” with “being a good engineer.”

    DSA gets you the interview. After that, they judge how you operate:
    Can you explain your approach clearly?
    Can you debug when your first solution breaks?
    Can you handle edge cases and tradeoffs without panicking?

    On system design: It’s a non-negotiable skill in 2026 if you want to grow, but as a fresher you can delay deep HLD and LLD for a bit. First, get the job.

    Build strong basics: coding, debugging, clean projects, and communication. Then go all-in on system design with context from work.

    DSA is not easy, but it’s still a real pathway if you commit to it.

  • Feed post number 101

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Amazon for an SDE-3 role and the interviewer has given you the question to Design Netflix. He then asks a follow up to you:
  • How does Netflix knows when to show: Are you still watching? Is it just time-based?
  • If it were time-based, you would see it every time.
  • So what is actually going on under the hood?

    Both look like a simple pop-up. Underneath, it is a mix of product thinking, client logic, and backend events.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    [1] Start from the product goals

    The feature is not only about nagging people.

    - Save bandwidth and CDN cost if the viewer has slept or walked away
    - Avoid autoplaying potentially sensitive content in an empty room
    - Protect kids if parents start a show and leave the TV on
    - Do all this without annoying active binge watchers

    So the design must answer one question: "When is the user probably not here any more"

    [2] Naive design - pure timer

    Simplest idea:
    - If playback has been running for 2 hours, show Are you still watching
    - If user clicks "Yes", reset the timer

    Why this is weak:
    - Someone can binge 5 episodes in a row and gets interrupted in the middle of an intense scene
    - Someone can start a show, walk away after 5 minutes, and the platform will keep streaming for the next 2 hours
    - It ignores how many episodes were auto played, device type, time of day, user habits

    [3] Realistic design - session and engagement signals

    Think in terms of a "watch session" and "engagement events".

    Signals the client can track:

    - Play, pause, seek, volume change
    - Episode finished and next episode auto started
    - Remote or keyboard input, UI navigation
    - Screen on or off events from the device
    - For mobiles: app background or locked state

    A common heuristic could be:

    - Count how many episodes have auto played without any user interaction
    - Track how long it has been since the last button press or navigation
    - Only trigger the prompt when both are high enough

    Example rule:

    - If 3 episodes in a row have auto played
    - And there has been no interaction for 45 minutes
    - Then, before starting episode 4, show Are you still watching

    This feels much smarter:

    - Active viewers who pause, skip intro, change volume keep resetting the "engagement clock"
    - Sleeping viewers do not touch anything, so the next episode is blocked by the prompt

    [4] Client heavy vs server heavy design

    You can talk about two design choices.

    Client first:

    - All logic runs inside the app on TV, mobile, or web
    - Client keeps an in memory session model and decides when to show the popup
    - It still sends telemetry events to backend for analytics and future tuning

    Pros:

    - Works even with flaky network
    - Highly responsive, no extra server round trip

    Cons:

    - Logic must be implemented and updated across many platforms
    - Harder to quickly roll out rule changes

    Continued ↓

  • Feed post number 102

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • A Senior Engineer at Amazon in India (L6) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • A Senior Engineer at Microsoft in India (L64) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • A Senior Engineer at Google in India (L5) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • Tech salaries at the higher levels are absolutely crazy, but so is the standards and hiring bar for these roles. (Salary numbers source: Levels(dot)fyi app)

  • if you're preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    If you're beginning to learn system design, I would say the best way to do it is learn by doing and keep it practical. Here are 40 Fundamentals of system design framed as question that will force you think and help you learn.

    [1] Caching and streaming experience

    - Why does Spotify keep playing in low network zones while YouTube buffers
    - How does Netflix resume exactly where you left off
    - How does Instagram preload the next few Reels so they play instantly when you swipe
    - Why do some video apps let you scrub back instantly but jumping far ahead takes longer

    [2] Data modeling and identity

    - How does WhatsApp guarantee a message goes to the right chat even if you change your phone
    - Why can you log into the same Netflix account on multiple devices with consistent profiles
    - How does Amazon track one order through cart, payment, shipping and delivery events
    - Why do ride hailing apps treat driver, rider and trip as separate entities instead of one big record

    [3] Scalable storage

    - How does Instagram store billions of images without one database table exploding
    - Why does a URL shortener not run out of unique short links at massive scale
    - How does Google Photos let you search across years of uploads without long delays
    - Why do apps split big tables into shards instead of just buying a bigger machine

    [4] Read vs write paths and consistency

    - How can you see your own new tweet immediately while others see it a bit later
    - Why does your bank show a pending transaction before it becomes final
    - How does a food delivery app avoid showing a restaurant as open when it just went offline
    - Why do some dashboards show last updated 2 minutes ago instead of real time numbers

    [5] Indexing and search

    - How does LinkedIn suggest profiles as soon as you type two or three characters
    - Why can Gmail search across years of emails in a second
    - How does Swiggy or Zomato filter restaurants by cuisine, rating and delivery time together
    - Why do some apps need a separate search service instead of querying the main database

    [6] Replication and geo distribution

    - How does Netflix keep your watch history in sync when you travel between countries
    - Why can you still read older WhatsApp messages when the service is facing an outage
    - How does a global game server keep players in different continents in the same match
    - Why do many systems talk about leader and follower replicas for data

    Continued ↓

  • Feed post number 103

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • If I had an E4 role System Design interview at Meta next week, and only 2 or 3 good days to revise, here’s exactly what I would go over.
  • People love to say out fancy jargon.
  • But simple almost always scales better than complex.
  • And the first rule of distributed systems is to never build one if you can solve the problem without it. They’re messy, expensive, slow to debug, and the source of most outages.

    The best way to learn system design is to learn it by doing, and that’s exactly what     layrs.me helps you to do. Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    This is my short revision plan..

    – Networking Essentials

    (You need these to survive half the follow-up questions)

    • How the network stack actually works
    • TCP vs UDP vs QUIC
    • DNS resolution, TLS handshakes, HTTP lifecycle
    • REST vs RPC vs gRPC
    • Idempotency, retries, backoff, timeouts, and rate limiting
    • ETags, conditional requests, and how caching works
    • Load balancer basics (L4 vs L7, health checks, stickiness)

    – Data Storage Foundations

    (If this is shaky, everything you design collapses)

    • SQL vs NoSQL and when to choose which
    • B+ Trees, LSM Trees, WAL, MVCC
    • Isolation levels and common anomalies
    • Multi-tenant data layouts in SaaS apps

    – Distributed Systems

    (Treat this section like a weapons locker)

    • Sharding strategies
    • Replication models
    • Strong vs eventual consistency
    • Leader, follower, quorum
    • Time, clocks, drift, and ordering events in distributed systems

    If these concepts are clear, any big design, URL shortener, messaging queue, social feed, notifications, payment pipeline. becomes far easier to reason about.

    If they’re weak, even a tiny follow-up question can blow up your entire architecture.

    Keep it simple. Pick the right components. Explain tradeoffs. And don’t jump into distributed systems unless you absolutely must.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • Amazon puts over 50 LPA+ on the table for the SDE II role in India. Imagine you are interviewing for SDE-II role @ Amazon and in your system design round, the interviewer asks:
  • "When I pause a movie on my phone and later open it on my TV, Netflix/Prime Video starts from the exact second I left. How would you design a system so a user can always resume from where they stopped, across all their devices?"
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • [1] What a naive design would do

    Idea: Store progress only on the device.

    Flow
    - The player keeps the current timestamp in local storage.
    - When you reopen the app on the same device, it reads that value and seeks to that point.

    Why this breaks down?
    - You switch from mobile to TV and the TV has no idea where you stopped.
    - If you uninstall the app or clear data, progress is gone.
    - There is no single source of truth when you have many devices.

    Just local storage is not enough for a company like Amazon or Netflix.

    [2] What real systems do: server-side progress service

    Idea: Keep playback state on the server, and let every device sync to it.

    Core pieces
    - A "watch progress" service with an API like
    POST /progress and GET /progress.
    - A table or key value store keyed by user_id + profile_id + title_id (+ episode_id).
    - Fields like position_seconds, duration_seconds, device_id, updated_at, status (in_progress, finished).

    What happens while you watch
    - The video player sends progress updates every N seconds or on key events
    (play, pause, seek, stop, app background).
    - These are small API calls that update the row for that title.
    - If position crosses a threshold like 90 percent, the backend can mark the episode as "watched" and maybe reset resume point to 0.

    What happens when you open the app on another device?

    - The app calls GET /progress for the current title when you land on the details page or hit play.
    - The server returns the last known position.
    - The player seeks to that timestamp and starts from there, often with a "Resume from 37:12" prompt.

    Extra details you can add
    - Store a small history, not only one row, in case of bugs or rolling back state.
    - Use write behind or batching so heartbeats do not overload the backend.
    - Use TTL or cleanup jobs to remove very old partial watches.

    [3] Handling tricky cases

    Multiple devices at once
    - Two devices might send updates at the same time.
    - The service should keep the latest updated_at, or prefer the active device id.

    Offline viewing
    - For downloads, the device tracks progress locally while offline.
    - When it reconnects, it pushes a final progress event that updates the server.

    Privacy and size
    - Progress data is small but grows with millions of users.
    - A key value store or a simple sharded relational table is usually enough, since the access pattern is "get by key, update by key".

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 4mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Meta (which owns WhatsApp) for the E4 Software Engineer role, and the interviewer leans in and asks:
  • "Why can Slack search across a workspace with 100,000 messages in under a second, while searching in a big WhatsApp group often feels slow and needs you to scroll up to load more?"
  • Here is how you break it down. Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • We’ve added new features as well:
    -Company-specific interviews
    -In-built interview scheduler
    -performance insights & trends

    [1] Slack: server-side indexed search

    Idea: Treat every message like a searchable document and index it on the server.

    What happens in Slack

    - Client sends plaintext message to the server.
    - Server stores it and feeds it into a search index (inverted index per workspace).
    - When you search, the query hits that index, not the raw messages.
    - Index lookup returns matching message IDs in milliseconds, even for huge workspaces.

    Why it feels fast

    - Heavy work runs on powerful servers across many shards.
    - All history is already indexed, so search is just a lookup plus ranking.

    In an answer, name

    - Inverted index per workspace or per channel.
    - Background workers that keep the index fresh.
    - Sharding and access control at query time.

    [2] WhatsApp: end-to-end encrypted, client-side search

    Idea: Privacy first. The server must not read your messages, so it cannot build a global search index.

    What happens in WhatsApp

    - Message is encrypted on device and sent as ciphertext.
    - Server stores and forwards encrypted blobs only.
    - Each phone keeps its own encrypted local database of messages.

    What happens when you search

    - Phone has to scan its local store, decrypt messages, and compare text.
    - Older history might not be on device, so the app fetches more as you scroll up.
    - All of this runs on a single mobile CPU, with tight limits on storage and battery.

    Why it feels slow

    - No server side index, because the server never sees plaintext.
    - Full text indexing on device for years of group history would be expensive.

    [3] The tradeoff you call out

    To the user, both apps are "type, send, search."
    To a designer, the key question is: where is content visible and where is search allowed to run?

    Slack style

    - Optimized for rich, fast search over all workspace history.
    - Server can read and index content.
    - Speed and features win, privacy is weaker than strict E2EE.

    WhatsApp style

    - Optimized for strong privacy with end to end encryption.
    - Server never sees message content, so search is local and heavier.
    - User pays with slower, more incremental search on big chats.

    If you add one more line in an interview, you can propose a middle path: local inverted index for recent messages, background indexing when charging, and user controls for how much history should be searchable on device.

  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Google for the L5 Senior Engineer role, and the interviewer leans in and asks:
  • “Why does Spotify keep playing when I drive into a tunnel with no signal, but YouTube Music often stops or buffers? If you were designing a music streaming system, what different design choices would lead to these two behaviors?”
  • Here is how you break it down. Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • We’ve added new features as well:
    -Company-specific interviews
    -In-built interview scheduler
    -performance insights & trends

    —
    Both apps look like simple music players.
    Under the hood, they are optimized for very different priorities.

    [1] Spotify style – Cache first, stream second

    Idea: The client behaves like a smart offline player that happens to stream.
    The backend is built to support aggressive prefetching.

    What happens when you hit play

    - Client requests the track from a CDN or edge node.
    - Instead of tiny chunks, it downloads a big buffer ahead of the playhead.
    - In parallel, it starts pre downloading the next few tracks in the queue.
    - Data is written to an encrypted cache on disk, not just memory.
    - Playback reads from that local cache, not directly from the network socket.

    What this means for tunnels and bad networks

    - When the network drops, the player already has tens of seconds or entire tracks cached.
    - Because the next one or two songs are already downloaded, you can be offline for a while and never notice.
    - Cold start cost is a bit higher. First play might take slightly longer, but then everything feels smooth.
    - It burns more local storage and possibly more data, because not every prefetched song will be listened to fully.

    In a design answer, you can mention

    - Local disk cache with eviction policies (LRU per user, per device).
    - Background prefetch of N upcoming tracks based on queue.
    - Download manager that adapts how aggressively it prefetches based on network quality and user settings.
    - CDN tuned for larger object delivery and range requests.
    - Explicit offline mode that pins playlists into cache.

    [2] YouTube Music style – Stream first, cache is minimal

    Idea: Treat audio like video streams.
    Cost and bandwidth are optimized first.

    When you hit play
    - Player requests audio (and sometimes video) via HLS or DASH-style chunks.
    - Each chunk is only a few seconds long.
    - Client keeps a small rolling buffer in memory, not a large queue on disk.
    - Prefetch of future songs is limited, because video tracks are large and expensive to fetch speculatively.

    What this means for tunnels and bad networks

    - If your connection dies, the player only has a few seconds of buffered data.
    - As soon as those chunks are consumed, playback stalls.
    - Startup can feel snappy and data usage is controlled, especially for casual listeners.
    - Works very well on stable networks, feels fragile in spotty coverage.

  • Feed post number 107

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • A Sr. SWE used Kafka during a system design round at Amazon.
  • Another Sr. SWE used Redis during a system design round at Meta.
  • One other Sr. SWE used Elasticsearch during a system design round at Google.
  • But in every round, they designed custom APIs.

  • Because without clear APIs, you do not have contracts.
    And without clear contracts, you do not have a system that teams can safely build on.

    That’s why you should focus on API design fundamentals. Here's a list to get you started:

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, check out our mock interview tool:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    We’ve added new features as well:
    -Company-specific interviews
    -In-built interview scheduler
    -performance insights & trends

    [1] API Goals and Contracts
    • Who is the consumer, human or machine
    • What exact problem the API solves
    • Inputs, outputs, and non goals
    • SLAs and expectations around latency, availability, and limits

    [2] Resource Modeling
    • Identifying core resources and relationships
    • Nouns vs actions, when to use each
    • Mapping business concepts to URLs
    • Handling one to many and many to many relations

    [3] Endpoints and HTTP Semantics
    • Choosing correct verbs (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE)
    • Idempotent vs non idempotent operations
    • Path vs query params vs headers
    • When to create a new endpoint vs extend an existing one

    [4] Request and Response Design
    • Consistent JSON structure and field naming
    • Required vs optional fields
    • Standard metadata (request ids, timestamps, cursors)
    • Handling large payloads and file uploads

    [5] Errors and Status Codes
    • Clear mapping between failure types and HTTP status codes
    • Error bodies with machine readable codes and human messages
    • Validation errors vs business rule errors vs system failures
    • How clients should retry or correct the request

    [6] Idempotency, Retries and Ordering
    • Idempotency keys for payments and critical writes
    • Safe retry policies for clients and gateways
    • Handling at least once delivery from queues
    • When to support exactly once or ordered semantics

    [7] Versioning and Backward Compatibility
    • URI versioning vs header based versioning
    • Adding fields without breaking old clients
    • Soft deprecations and sunset timelines
    • Feature flags and rollout strategies for new behavior

    [8] Security and Access Control
    • Auth choices, OAuth, JWT, API keys, mTLS
    • Scopes and permissions per endpoint
    • Protecting sensitive fields in logs and responses
    • Rate limits and abuse protection per user, app, IP

    [9] Performance, Pagination and Caching
    • Offset vs cursor pagination and trade offs
    • Partial responses and filtering
    • Cache control headers, ETags, conditional requests
    • Timeouts, payload limits and batching patterns

    [10] Observability and Documentation
    • Standard logging fields for each request
    • Traces that follow a request across services
    • Metrics per endpoint, latency, error rate, saturation
    • Clear docs, examples, and SDKs so others integrate without meetings

  • The Avg. Comp for Meta E5 Level in India is over 75 Lakhs+, including bonuses and stocks, it goes over 1Cr+, and this is just at the Sr. Software Engineer level.
  • That is what Meta puts on the table if you can clear the process and it’s not too complicated:
  • - There are 2 DSA Rounds
  • - There is 1 System Design Round
  • - There is 1 Managerial / Behavioural Round

    Note: They’re also testing a new AI-based round, where you’re free to use AI but have to deliver a full functioning app in limited time.

    But they will grill you beyond what you can imagine, and that’s why you need bulletproof fundamentals, especially with DSA and System Design.

    If I were targeting Meta E5, these are the resources I would use to study up on my fundamentals:

  • Feed post number 109

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • I’ve interviewed at Google.
  • I’ve interviewed at Flipkart.
  • I’ve talked to people who interviewed at Uber, Adobe, Meta, and Atlassian.
  • No matter where you go, the first round is always DSA, no matter the title. Even the most experienced engineers can struggle with passing interviews because it’s challenging to adapt to the format after you’ve building day to day.

  • In my community at Layrs, there are also a lot of folks struggling with coding interviews and DSA. So, let me break down insights from my journey on how I passed coding interviews.

    Btw, if you’re preparing for system design/coding interviews, our mock interview tool. You can use it for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    [1] Think in three pillars.

    For a coding round, I treat everything as three parallel checks:

    - Planning and design
    - Coding
    - Communication

    You cannot bomb any one of them.
    Most engineers do fine in one or two and fail the third.

    [2] Start every problem the same way
    Before I touch the keyboard, run this script in your head:

    - Clarify the question
    “Can the array be empty? Are values negative? Is there always a valid answer?”

    - Clarify input and output types
    “Do you want the index, the value, or a boolean?”

    - Ask for at least one example test case
    You show depth and you also remove edge cases you do not need to handle.

    [3] Visualise and build test cases first
    If the problem has a grid, graph or linked list, draw it.

    Then:
    - Walk through the sample test case out loud
    - Create one extra test of my own
    - Include an edge case: empty input, one element, duplicates, extremes

    By the time you finish this, you know what the code must do step by step.

    [4] Design the algorithm before you code

    Always state in plain English first:
    - Start with a simple brute force approach
    - Say why it is slow
    - Then improve it and talk through the pattern I am using

    Only when you can explain the full plan in words, in order, you should start writing code.
    If you jump into code without this, you almost always get stuck.

    [5] Code like you do this for a living

    This part should look smooth. That means:
    - No long silence without context
    - Say what you will write next, then write it
    - Use clear variable and function names
    - Break logic into small helpers instead of one large block

    The interviewer should be able to read your code like simple text.

    [6] Always analyse your solution

    Before the interviewer asks, you say:
    - “Time complexity is O(...), space is O(...). It is suboptimal because…”
    - “With more time, I would try X to bring it to linear time.”

    Even if the code is not perfect, this shows control over your tradeoffs.

    [7] Talk like you are pairing with a teammate

    The interviewer cannot read your mind.

    So you should:
    - Keep speaking about what I am thinking
    - Ask small questions instead of sitting stuck
    - Treat it like a pair programming session, not an exam

    If they know where your head is, they can nudge you instead of writing “no hire”.

  • Feed post number 110

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • API design in system design interviews sounds easy: draw a box, write /users, move on. Then the interviewer asks what happens when a client retries your payment API five times and you charge them $50,000 instead of $10,000...
  • Here are 15 things to think through before you talk about your APIs in a system design round:
  • Btw, if you’re preparing for system design interviews, I’ve got good news for you. Last year we added our mock interview tool for system design. Try it out here: https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

  • We’ve just added some new features:
    -Company-specific interviews
    -In-built interview scheduler
    -performance insights & trends

    1. Start from flows.
    Sign up, login, pay, refund, search. Design APIs that match user journeys, not raw CRUD on every database table.

    2. Define clear resources
    Decide your core nouns: user, order, payment, session. Show how they relate. If your resources are fuzzy, your APIs will be too.

    3. Be explicit about contracts
    For each endpoint, say what comes in, what goes out, which fields are required, which are optional, and which are stable for years.

    4. Handle idempotency for writes
    Especially for payments, bookings and order creation. Talk about idempotency keys, how you store them and how you ensure one logical action per key.

    5. Choose a versioning story
    Explain how you evolve APIs without breaking old clients. v1 vs v2 paths, header based versioning, or additive changes with strict deprecation rules.

    6. Design consistent error responses
    Show a structured error body with code, message and hints so clients know what to do next.

    7. Think about authentication and authz
    Who calls the API. Which token. Which scopes or roles. Where you validate them in the request flow.

    8. Rate limits and abuse protection
    Say what happens when a client spams your endpoint. Mention per user or per token limits and how you communicate limits back.

    9. Pagination and filters
    Explain how clients fetch large lists. Offset vs cursor. How you keep results stable when data is changing.

    10. Consistency rules
    Call out which operations must be strongly consistent and which can be eventually consistent. Tie this to specific APIs like balances or notifications.

    11. Sync vs async operations
    Long running work should not block a request forever. Show where you would use job creation plus status endpoints or callbacks.

    12. Observability baked in
    Mention trace IDs, structured logs and metrics per endpoint. Explain how you would debug a slow or flaky API call in production.

    13. Backward compatibility discipline
    New field is fine. Removing or renaming fields is not. Explain how you deprecate and how you track who still calls old versions.

    14. Ownership boundaries
    Show how APIs map to team boundaries. Who owns auth, who owns billing, who owns search. Avoid everyone touching everything.

    15. Security by design
    Input validation, output encoding, rate limits, audit logs. Mention how you protect sensitive fields and secrets in transit and at rest.

  • Feed post number 111

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • A Senior Engineer at Amazon in India (L6) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • A Senior Engineer at Microsoft in India (L64) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • A Senior Engineer at Google in India (L5) makes 1Cr+ a year
  • Tech salaries at the higher levels are absolutely crazy, but so is the standards and hiring bar for these roles. (Salary numbers source: Levels(dot)fyi app)

  • Last year, I spoke to Senior, Principal and Staff engineers at Google, Microsoft, Uber, Oracle and a few other companies to get feedback and shape Layrs.

    if you're preparing for system design/coding interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     https://lnkd.in/gpCn7t2T

    Here is what kept coming up when we spoke about interviews in 2025–2026 and what separates strong senior candidates from the rest.

    • Coding is more practical now
    - Less focus on tricky puzzles, more on strings, maps, heaps, real world logic
    - You may still get LeetCode style questions, but not as the only signal
    - They watch how you structure code, name things, handle edge cases and errors

    • System design is the real filter
    - Fewer design YouTube type questions, more design this feature or service we care about
    - They want you to talk through data models, throughput, latency, cost, and failure recovery
    - Good seniors keep tradeoffs on the table instead of pushing one perfect design

    • Concurrency and low level design show up a lot
    - Many loops include something concurrent or multi threaded, or at least async flows
    - You need to reason about race conditions, locking, ordering and correctness under load
    - If you can only talk high level and avoid these details, it shows very fast

    • AI usage is no longer a nice to have
    - Many companies want to see that you have used LLMs or AI tools in a serious way
    - You do not need to be an ML engineer, but you should know how to own a system that calls models
    - Talking about RAG, batching, caching, streaming, and latency versus cost gives you an edge

    • Project deep dives separate strong from average
    - Panels often ask you to bring one or two projects you are proud of
    - They then drill into why you made certain choices, what broke, and what you would change
    - If your stories are shallow or full of buzzwords, you lose ground fast

    • Behavioural rounds are tougher at senior levels
    - They test how you handle conflict, vague direction and production failures
    - They look for ownership, not my manager said or the team decided on every answer
    - Saying here is what I pushed for and why lands much better than hiding behind process

    • Strong seniors show depth in at least one area
    - It can be backend, infra, data, AI integration or something else
    - You do not need to know everything, but you must own something end to end
    - Interviewers feel it when you can answer follow up questions for ten to fifteen minutes without hand waving

  • Feed post number 113

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • In 2014: If you could draw boxes, add a database, and solve URL Shortener, you passed the system design rounds.
  • In 2026: Those days are gone because the systems you design now are solving for entirely different problems. System design interviews have adjusted to that reality.
  • Today, you have to know more than just patterns and pretty diagrams.
  • Bookish Knowledge ≠ Practical Knowledge.

    That’s why you should focus on:

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     http://Layrs.me/interviews

    1. Problems and not patterns in isolation
    - Do not only memorise “CQRS”, “event sourcing”, “SAGA” from a blog.
    - Pick a problem like “notifications with retries” or “payments with idempotency” and ask: what pattern solves this and why.

    2. Data flows and contracts
    - Be very clear about what flows where: requests, events, metrics, logs.
    - Define what each service guarantees to the next one: at most once, at least once, exactly once.

    3. Failure, backpressure, and recovery
    - Talk about timeouts, retries with backoff, poison messages, dead letter queues.
    - Explain how you would detect the issue in production using metrics, logs and tracing.

    4. Storage and consistency trade-offs

    - For each component, answer: what needs strong consistency and what can be eventual.
    - Payments and balances are not the same as likes and view counts.
    - Mention how you would use caches, queues and databases together without corrupting data.

    5. Real-time and batch together

    - Analytics, recommendations and pricing rarely live in pure batch or pure real time.
    -  Show you understand how to mix both: stream for fresh data, batch for heavy crunching.
    - Explain how you would keep the two views consistent enough for the product to behave.

    6. Operability and cost

    - A design that “works” but costs 10x the budget will not survive.
    -  Talk about what you would store cold vs hot, what you would shard, where you would compress.
    - Mention how you would start simple and evolve as traffic grows.

    7. Depth on a few core systems

    - Instead of skimming 40 random problems, pick 10 to 15 and go deep.
    - For example: metrics, logging, auth, notifications, search, payments, ride matching, file storage.
    - When you can explain one of these across architecture, data model, failure modes and trade offs, it shows.

    Learn the theory, but train by solving problems.

  • Feed post number 114

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • A candidate interviewing for a Senior Engineer @ Meta was asked to design a Distributed Stream Processing System like Kafka. Another candidate at Google's L5 loop got hit with the same question.
  • If this question shows up in your system design interview, you are not being tested on config flags. You are being tested on whether you understand when to use it and what tradeoffs you are making.
  • Btw, If you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:http://layrs.me/interviews

  • Here is how I would structure a clear answer. (Note: I am not going into nuances, this is a very high-level explanation)

    1. What problem are we solving

    At a high level, Kafka is my go-to when I need:

    - A central backbone for events or logs coming from many services
    - High throughput writes with ordering per key
    - Consumers that can read at their own pace and be added or removed independently
    - The ability to replay history using offsets

    Typical use cases I call out:

    - Asynchronous workflows
    - Central logging and metrics
    - Ad click or analytics streams
    - Pub sub for notifications or chat

    That already tells the interviewer I know -why- Kafka is in the picture.

    2. Requirements

    Functional

    - Accept events from many producers in different services and regions
    - Preserve order for events that share the same key
    - Allow many independent consumers
    +some doing real time processing
    +some doing batch jobs or analytics
    - Support replay from an offset for backfills and reprocessing

    Non functional
    - Handle very high write rates
    - Scale horizontally by adding machines
    - Survive broker failures without losing committed data
    - Let us tune retention for cost vs replay needs

    Rest of the breakdown:     https://lnkd.in/gmg7u-ku

  • Feed post number 116

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • Do Google/Amazon really ask Two Sum in their Coding interviews? You'd think that's a dumb question, but yep, it happens, and not in the way you think.
  • More than one engineer has walked into a FAANG interview, been given Two Sum, solved it with a hash map, and then heard: "Cool. Now solve it without extra memory. Then compare both approaches."
  • That is the real test. Not whether you can type for (int i = 0; i < n; i++), but whether you understand what you just wrote.

  • By the way, if you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:    http://layrs.me/interviews

    A simple-looking question gives the interviewer room to check things that actually matter on the job:

    - Can you generate more than one approach, for example, brute force, hash map, sorting plus two pointers?
    -  Do you know how time and space change across these versions and when each tradeoff is acceptable?
    - Do you understand what "no extra memory" really means for arrays, recursion, in place sorting and constant space?
    - Can you talk through integer limits, stability, cache friendliness, large inputs, not just pass on the platform?
    - Do you stay calm when the question is easy on the surface and you know there is a catch coming?

    That is what separates someone who has memorized LeetCode discussions from someone who can think like an engineer.

    The classics keep showing up for a reason. Two Sum, Trapping Rain Water, monotonic stack problems, basic graph traversal. They are short to explain, hard to fake, and rich in follow up questions.

    You can probe communication, depth of understanding, and design instincts without spending ten minutes reading a huge prompt.

    So if you see Two Sum or any "easy" tag in an interview, do not roll your eyes. Use it to show your range.

    Solve it.  Then offer another approach yourself.
    Compare them in terms of time, space, and practical use cases. Explain where you would pick one over the other in a product that handles millions of users.

    That is the level that gets you hired.

    In the end, the question label does not matter. What matters is whether, in those 45 minutes, you looked like someone who can reason, trade off, and communicate under pressure.
