  • Feed post number 117

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • Saying “this will easily scale to 100 million users” in a system design interview sounds cool, until the interviewer asks exactly which component dies first when traffic spikes 10x in ten minutes, and you have no answer.
  • Here are 17 things to think about before you claim your system scales:
  • By the way, if you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free: http://layrs.me/interviews

  • 1. What does traffic really look like, peak vs average, reads vs writes?

    2. Where is the first bottleneck today, CPU, memory, disk, database, or network?

    3. How will you shard data so that one hot user or tenant does not melt a single shard?

    4. Which services are stateless so they can auto scale, and which hold sticky state?

    5. How will you handle connection limits to databases, caches, and message brokers?

    6. What is your caching story, keys, TTLs, invalidation, and cache stampede control?

    7. How do you apply rate limits so a few bad clients cannot starve everyone else?

    8. Where do you use queues or logs to smooth bursty traffic and protect slow workers?

    9. What is your backpressure strategy when downstream systems are overloaded?

    10. How do you store and query data so indexes still work at billions of rows?

    11. What is your replication model, sync or async, and what consistency tradeoffs you accept?

    12. How does your design change when you go multi region with failover and data locality?

    13. What are your failure domains, and what happens when one zone or shard disappears?

    14. How will you observe the system, metrics, traces, logs, and clear SLOs?

    15. What is your plan for safe rollouts, canaries, and instant rollback at scale?

    16. How do you keep cloud costs under control when traffic doubles overnight?

    17. How will you test all this, load tests, chaos tests, and realistic traffic replay?

  • Feed post number 118

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • This software engineer applied for an L60 (SDE-1) role at Microsoft but got interviewed like he was applying for L62, one step below Senior.
  • Usually entry entry-level roles lean heavily on DSA only.
  • Here is what actually happened in his loop for Azure CoreAI:
  • Round 1 – DSA, but high difficulty.
    - Streaming array problem that needed a custom data structure for time efficiency.
    - Variant of the same problem with tighter space constraints. Crypto style encoder / decoder using public and private keys.

    Round 2 – LLD
    Design a multi-threaded rate limiter library that
    – supports multiple algorithms
    – works as a Java library
    –  plugs into API gateways in real systems

    Round 3 – HLD
    Scale that rate limiter in a distributed system. Talk about Redis internals, rate limiting headers, partitions, hot keys, and real production tradeoffs.

    Round 4 – Hiring Manager
    Ninety minutes of discussion around one legacy migration project, decisions taken, failures, recovery, and how he thinks about AI improving developer productivity.

    The offer he got was L60, but he cleared an interview loop that many people in comments called “easily L61 or L62 level.”

    What you should take from this:
    – DSA gets you into the room.
    –  LLD and HLD decide whether you look like a long-term hire.
    – Even for SDE-1 level roles, companies are testing how you think about systems, not just how fast you can code.
    –  If you want to stand out in 2026, having solid system design fundamentals is not optional anymore.

    If you are preparing for FAANG plus interviews and want to build this muscle, I have already put together resources for you:

    LLD problems list: [    https://lnkd.in/g7uAjhRx]

    HLD problems list: [    https://lnkd.in/gqJP-k9M]

    –
    Btw, you can also check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     http://Layrs.me/interviews

  • Feed post number 120

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • DSA gets you shortlisted. But it’s System Design that gets you the big offers, especially in 2026, where the focus will be growing even more on LLD and HLD.
  • I invested 2+ hours to collect the best questions on essential LLD, so you can cut down the time and learn fast. These are from various interview experiences I came across during my research.
  • Here is the list, grouped by question patterns so you know exactly what to practice.

  • Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     http://Layrs.me/interviews

     1. Resource and booking systems

    Pattern: limited resources, time slots, no double booking, fair allocation.

    - Design a Parking Lot
    - Design a Parking Lot - Multi Threaded
    - Design a Simple Elevator System - Multiple Lifts
    - Design Elevator Management System - Single Lift
    - Design an Elevator System - Request Feasibility (Single Lift)
    - Design a Train Platform Management System
    - Design a Meeting Room Reservation System
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler - List Bookings
    - Design a Meeting Room Scheduler for Recurrent Meetings
    - Design a Car Rental System
    - Design Locker Management System for Warehouse Packages
    - Design a Movie ticket booking system like BookMyShow

    2. Money, commerce and pricing flows

    Pattern: orders, payments, balances, revenue split, leaderboards around money or points.

    - Design a restaurant food ordering system like Zomato, Swiggy, DoorDash
    - Design expense sharing app like Splitwise
    - Design Stock Trading Platform like Zerodha, Groww
    - Design a Shopping Cart
    - Design a Payment Wallet like PayPal
    - Design an order and inventory management system - Multi Threaded
    - Design Product Plan Cost Explorer
    - Design a customer support agent rating leaderboard
    - Design a Leaderboard for Fantasy Teams
    - Design Last Lap Hero (F1 Race Tracker)

    3. Editors, spreadsheets and office tools

    Pattern: document state, operations on content, formulas, undo or recalculation.

    - Design a Text Editor or Word Processor like Microsoft Word
    - Design a Text Editor with Undo and Redo
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations and Basic Editing
    - Design a Text Editor with cursor operations
    - Design spreadsheet like Microsoft Excel
    - Design Excel Sum Formula

    4. File systems, search and storage

    Pattern: hierarchical paths, metadata, indexing, queries on files or logs.

    - Design Unix `find` command for file search
    - Design Unix `find` command with boolean predicates
    - Design File System (cd with `-`)
    - Design File System
    - Design In Memory File System
    - Design File Collections Tracker
    - Design Log Storage System

    5. Search, lookup and dictionaries

    Pattern: key based lookup, tries, prefix search.

    - Design Dictionary App to store words and their meanings
    - Design Phone Directory
    - Design Google Search Autocomplete

  • Feed post number 121

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 4mo •
  • If I go back to my interview days, I will study these 40 questions to get ready for concept-based system design questions that are often asked in the telephonic screens.
  • I myself have impressed interviewers with answers because I was prepared, hope this helps you, too!
  • Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here: http://Layrs.me/interviews

  • 1. What is difference between API Gateway and Load Balancer?
    2. What is difference between Reverse Proxy and Forward Proxy?
    3. What is difference between Horizontal scaling and vertical scaling?
    4. What is difference between Microservices and Monolithic architecture?
    5. What is difference between vertical and horizontal partitioning?
    6. What is Rate Limiter? How does it work?
    7. How does Single Sign On (SSO) works?
    8. How does Apache Kafka works? Why is it so fast?
    9. Difference between Kafka, ActiveMQ, and RabbitMQ?
    10. Difference between JWT, OAuth, and SAML?
    11. What is difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
    12. How does Content Delivery Network (CDN) work?
    13. What is difference between Synchronous and Asynchronous communication?
    14. How does Database Sharding work?
    15. What is difference between REST and GraphQL?
    16. How does Caching work? What are caching strategies?
    17. What is difference between Strong and Eventual Consistency?
    18. How does Message Queue work?
    19. What is difference between TCP and UDP?
    20. How does Database Replication work?
    21. What is difference between Stateful and Stateless architecture?
    22. How does Circuit Breaker pattern work?
    23. What is difference between Authentication and Authorization?
    24. How does Distributed Tracing work?
    25. What is difference between ACID and BASE properties?
    26. How does Service Discovery work?
    27. What is difference between Push and Pull architecture?
    28. How does Database Indexing work?
    29. What is difference between Batch and Stream processing?
    30. How does Consistent Hashing work?
    31. What is difference between RPC and REST?
    32. How does Leader Election work in distributed systems?
    33. What is difference between Optimistic and Pessimistic locking?
    34. How does Two Phase Commit work?
    35. What is difference between CAP theorem components?
    36. How does Bloom Filter work?
    37. What is difference between WebSocket and HTTP?
    38. How does Service Mesh work?
    39. What is difference between Blue Green and Canary deployment?
    40. How does Distributed Cache work?

    Bookmark this.
    Review before your next system design interview.

    What other questions would you add to this list?

  • Feed post number 122

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Meta, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Google, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Amazon, they’ll again go deep into your system design
  • System design is that one skill that levels up your:

  • – pay scale
    – responsibilities
    – impact on the company

    I asked 5+ L5 and equivalent engineers at Uber, Google, Meta, and Microsoft which problems they would ask in a mock interview to candidates if they had to test how well they knew system design.

    This is the list I came up with, grouped into 8 themes, 5 problems each.

    Btw, if you're preparing for system design interviews, check out our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA. You can use them for free here:     http://Layrs.me/interviews

    1. Social feeds and content platforms
    ↬ Design Twitter
    ↬ Design Instagram
    ↬ Design Facebook’s Newsfeed
    ↬ Design a Platform Like Reddit
    ↬ Design Youtube or Netflix

    2. Messaging and real time collaboration
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design a Live Comment System
    ↬ Design a Video Conferencing system
    ↬ Design a Collaboration Tool for Team Communication
    ↬ Design Google Doc

    3. E commerce, payments, and logistics
    ↬ Design an E-commerce Service
    ↬ Design Shopify
    ↬ Design an Online Payment Service
    ↬ Design a Digital Wallet
    ↬ Design a Food Delivery Service

    4. Core infra and storage primitives
    ↬ Design a Key Value Store
    ↬ Design a Web Cache
    ↬ Design a Distributed File System
    ↬ Design a Global Content Distribution Network
    ↬ Design a Load Balancer

    5. Events, queues, and rate limiting
    ↬ Design an API Rate Limiter
    ↬ Design a Distributed Messaging System
    ↬ Design a Distributed Counter
    ↬ Design a Task Scheduler
    ↬ Design a Webhook Notification Service

    6. Data, analytics, and logging
    ↬ Design a Web Analytics Tool
    ↬ Design a Metrics Monitoring and Alerting System
    ↬ Design a Log Collection and Analysis System
    ↬ Design an Ad Click Aggregation System
    ↬ Design a Top-K Request Analysis System

    7. Ids, consistency, and coordination
    ↬ Design a Distributed Unique Id Generator
    ↬ Design a Distributed Locking System
    ↬ Design a Resource Allocation Service
    ↬ Design a Distributed Tracing System
    ↬ Design a Database Batch Auditing Service

    8. Boss fight level problems xD
    ↬ Design Uber Backend
    ↬ Design Ticketmaster
    ↬ Design Google Search
    ↬ Design a Real Time Stock Trading Platform
    ↬ Design a Container Orchestration System

    Pick any one from each category and you already have a solid system design interview loop to practice.

    Image Source: Levels(dot)fyi

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • Five 9s of availability usually costs five divorces on the engineering team. Yes, the uptime is great but the therapy to recover from the outage they cause is $1,000/hour.
  • Okay, joke aside:
  • – 99% uptime = ~3 days, 15 hours of downtime per year
  • – 99.9% uptime = ~8 hours, 45 minutes per year
    – 99.99% uptime = ~52 minutes per year
    – 99.999% uptime (“Five 9s”) = just 5 minutes, 15 seconds per year
    (~26 seconds/month, ~6 seconds/week)

    Every ‘9’ you add is another layer.  Failover, redundancy, instant detection, zero-downtime migrations, and global disaster recovery, all of these elements will be added to it.

    So, why can’t every company just aim for Five 9s?

    Because at that level, everything gets brutal:
    – You need to split data across the globe and still keep it consistent.
    – You have to spot failures and reroute in milliseconds.

    Enter the CAP theorem:

    – Consistency: Users see the same data, everywhere, all the time.
    – Availability: The system never, ever goes down.
    – Partition tolerance: It survives network splits and chaos.

    You don’t get all three. Push for that extra ‘9’ in uptime, and you WILL have to give up consistency somewhere, or spend millions building cross-region failover that barely holds together under chaos.

    Every ‘9’ you chase is a multiplier of cost, engineering stress, and sleepless nights.

    That extra '9' is a trophy. As the meme shows, look at the face of the guy who won it.

    That is the face of a man who just wants to turn off his pager and sleep for a week. That’s why companies choose their battles. Not every system needs to be immortal, those who do, have tons of teams like most FAANG+ companies.

    —
    By the way, if you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:    http://layrs.me/interviews

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • The Avg. Comp for Meta E5 Level in India is over 75 Lakhs+, including bonuses and stocks, it goes over 1Cr+, and this is just at the Sr. Software Engineer level (5 YOE+)
  • The Avg. Comp for Google E5 Level in India is over 60 lakhs+, including bonuses and stocks, it also goes over 1Cr+, this is the Sr. Software Engineer level (5 YOE+)
  • If I were targeting Sr. Roles are Meta, Google, and Microsoft with my 5+ YOE, this is 1 cheat I would 100% use, created from my own experience.

  • How to use this cheatsheet in an interview?

    Think in three quick steps:

    1. Listen to the problem and label the pattern
    Example in your head:
    This is mostly read-heavy with some writes or
    This is real-time fanout, lots of live updates

    2. Pick the closest pattern from the list below

    3. Use the lines like this when you speak

    – First arrow → what kind of systems does this pattern fit
    – Second arrow → what really matters here
    – Third arrow → which tools or building blocks to mention
    – Fourth arrow → how you might summarise it to the interviewer

    This will help to structure your thinking.

    By the way, if you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:    http://layrs.me/interviews

    1. Read heavy systems

    → Fits: feeds, product pages, profiles, dashboards
    → Focus: fast reads, acceptable staleness, low DB pressure
    → Tools: cache in front of DB, read replicas, search index, background jobs
    → How to say it:
    "This is read heavy. I would put a cache and maybe a search index in front of the main DB, and refresh it with background jobs so most requests never hit the core database."

    2. Write heavy, event-driven systems

    → Fits: logs, clickstream, payments events, audit trails
    → Focus: durable writes, ordering, handling spikes
    → Tools: queues or streams, consumers, batching, idempotent handlers, dead letter queue
    → How to say it:
    "Here writes are the main thing. I would land everything on a queue or stream first, then process with consumers that are idempotent and can batch work to the database."

    3. Real-time fanout systems

    → Fits: chat, live scores, delivery tracking, notifications
    → Focus: low latency push, many consumers, avoid constant polling
    → Tools: websockets or SSE, pub or sub layer, in memory cache, fanout service
    → How to say it:
    "This needs real time fanout. I would keep a websocket layer or similar, push updates through a pub or sub channel, and avoid going to the database for every small update."

    4. Batch and analytics systems

    → Fits: reports, BI dashboards, recommendation training pipelines
    → Focus: huge volume, freshness in minutes or hours, cheap scans
    → Tools: data lake or warehouse, ETL or ELT pipeline, columnar formats, pre aggregated tables
    → How to say it:
    "This is batch and analytics. I would separate the OLTP database from the analytics store, load data in batches, and build pre-aggregated tables for the dashboards."

    Check the comments as well, please…

  • Feed post number 125

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • The word “microservices” can be a trap for you, especially at companies like Google, Amazon, Uber...and others that operate at a large scale.
  • Most candidates use it to sound smart. But many interviewers will grill you on network latency, data consistency, and distributed sagas. If you can't defend the "Why," you've already lost.
  • Remember, Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication in System Design.

  • By the way, if you are preparing for DSA or system design, try our mock interview tool on Layrs for free:    http://layrs.me/interviews

    Here are 17 things to think about before you drop the word “microservices” in an interview:

    1. If one team of five can build and own this, why is a single well structured service not enough?

    2. What real problem am I solving by splitting this into services, apart from sounding senior in the room?

    3. How much extra latency and failure am I adding with every network hop between services?

    4. Who will own dashboards, alerts and oncall for each service I am proposing?

    5. How will developers run and test this locally when it is no longer a single process?

    6. Where does the data live and how will I handle cross service queries without hacks in every call?

    7. If I lose simple ACID transactions, what is my plan for sagas, retries and compensation flows?

    8. When the schema changes, how will I roll out versions without breaking older services?

    9. How complex does my deployment pipeline become when I ship ten services instead of one?

    10. What is my plan for service discovery, configuration and secrets for all of these services?

    11. How will I tune retries, timeouts and circuit breakers so services do not accidentally DDoS each other?

    12. When one dependency is slow, how will my system degrade gracefully instead of just falling over?

    13. Is there a clear map of one team per service or am I designing a free for all architecture?

    14. How much extra cloud cost am I accepting so every tiny service can have its own CPU and memory?

    15. How will I secure internal APIs, tokens and keys now that everything talks to everything?

    16. What is my testing strategy, from unit to integration to contract tests, for flows that cross
    many services?

    17. When the business changes, how painful will it be to refactor this across multiple services instead of one codebase?

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 5mo •
  • A friend of my colleague from Google, who’s based in the US, has just interviewed for the E4 role at Meta. The total package on the table was up to ($260-270k ~ 2.7 Cr)
  • Sharing his interview experience for everyone preparing for interviews right now:
  • 1. Technical screen – pure DSA

  • - 45 minutes
    - 2 problems straight from Meta’s “Top 25 in last 3 months” on LeetCode
    - Code could run, but there were almost no test cases
    - He first explained the approach out loud, wrote it as comments, then coded and walked through examples

    He cleared this, then got the final loop.

    2. Final loop DSA round

    - Another 45-minute DSA interview
    - Difficulty very similar to the screen
    - This time code could not be executed, so clarity of reasoning mattered more than speed
    - He still did 2 problems, 10 to 15 minutes each, and spent the rest of the time explaining trade offs

    3. AI coding round
    - Given a small codebase with functions to fix or implement
    - 4 tasks, he fully solved 3 and explained the approach for the last one
    - AI could help read the code and give context, but not solve details, so fundamentals still mattered

    4. System design

    - 45 minutes
    - One of the most common Meta questions from HelloInterview
    - They used Excalidraw, he followed a strict delivery framework and kept asking for feedback as he went
    - Clear trade offs, scaling discussion, and follow ups on high traffic cases

    Before we move forward, I also want to tell you about our latest addition to Layrs. The AI based interview tools for system design and DSA are live and free to try:     layrs.me/interviews

    You can run DSA mocks that pick real interview-style questions, get follow-up questions step by step, and get instant feedback on structure and coding.
    For system design, you can practice delivery on real prompts, draw designs, and get nudges on coverage gaps before you sit in front of an interviewer.

    5. Behavioral and the surprise twist

    - 45 minute behavioral
    - He under prepared, thought “tech is what really matters”
    - Feedback after debrief: very strong technical signals, but too many missing signals on behavioral
    - Meta asked for a behavioral follow up
    - He then spent a week building strong STAR plus learning stories, did a focused follow up round, and only then cleared into team matching

    The point is simple.

    For roles at this level, you cannot treat DSA, system design, AI coding and behavioral as optional sliders. All four show up, and any one of them can decide the outcome.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • You’re in a system design interview at Microsoft. The interviewer asks you a very tricky problem:
  • “You’ve just designed a global product catalog service for millions of users. To handle traffic, you added Redis caching around your product lookup API.
  • A week later, you start getting weird complaints:

  • – users see stale prices during flash sales.
    – debugging takes twice as long, can’t tell if a bug is from the DB or cache.
    – memory usage keeps spiking, and it’s not clear which keys even matter anymore.

    The interviewer asks:
    “Caching made your system faster at first, but now it’s causing headaches.

    How would you decide what to cache, when to cache, and how to keep your cache healthy and correct? What questions should you ask before wrapping everything in Redis?

    This is how I would tackle it with proper reasoning:

    Before caching anything, I’d walk through these 7 critical questions:

    [1] Is the data accessed frequently?

    Caching makes sense only for data that’s hit often, like product pages requested by thousands every minute. Rarely used data just wastes memory and doesn’t deliver ROI.

    [2] Is it expensive to retrieve?

    Caching slow or resource-intensive queries saves real work (think heavy joins, aggregations, external API calls). But for cheap, indexed lookups, the overhead of cache might not be worth it.

    [3] Is the data stable or volatile?

    Stable data (like product categories or supported countries) is ideal for caching, safe to keep around.

    Volatile data (like prices in flash sales or fast-changing inventory) risks staleness; caching here demands strict TTLs or invalidation hooks.

    [4] Is the data small and simple?

    Small, flat objects are quick to cache and fetch; big, nested blobs slow everything down and eat up RAM.

    Don’t cache huge objects if you can break them up or just cache what’s actually used.

    [5] Does it directly impact user experience?

    Prioritize caching on the critical path: anything that speeds up what a user sees or interacts with.

    Background jobs or rare admin tasks? Latency here doesn’t matter much, so skip caching.

    [6] Is it safe to cache?

    Caching sensitive or user-specific data without scoping keys risks leaks, never cache what can’t go in a log file.

    Always use per-user/session keys, encrypt when needed, and set short TTLs for anything risky.

    [7] Will this scale?
    Your caching strategy for 1K users can break at 1M — unbounded keys, no eviction, and churn kill performance.
    Normalize inputs, cap cardinality, and monitor hit/miss/eviction stats as you grow.

    —

    P.S. Follow me for more system design insights and check out Layrs :     layrs.me/practice

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 5mo •
  • This software engineer got a downgraded offer as an SDE 1 during his Amazon interviews, even though he applied for an SDE 2 role. That is a difference of over 20 Lakhs in his CTC.
  • SDE 1 at Amazon starts at 36 LPA+
  • SDE 2 at Amazon starts at 56 LPA+
  • He admits it himself.
    The main reason was a weak system design round.

    Most people still treat system design as a “senior-level” topic.
    Amazon does not. Neither do most top product companies.

    You can clear OAs, crush LeetCode, and have great LP stories
    but if you cannot do this in a one hour conversation

    – take a vague product problem
    – ask sharp clarifying questions
    – propose a sensible high level design
    – talk through scaling, failures, and trade offs

    you will often get leveled down.

    System design is not about drawing the most complex diagram in the room.

    Things interviewers look for:
    – Do you know basic building blocks like cache, DB, queues, object storage
    – Can you decide when to read heavy vs write heavy, sync vs async
    – Do you think about failure, retries, data loss, and monitoring
    – Can you explain your choices in simple language that a PM or junior dev can follow

    If you ignore this and keep saying “I will do system design later”, you are not just risking rejection.

    You are also risking walking away with the wrong level and the wrong paycheck for the same effort.

    PS: If you want to stop guessing and actually practice system design with real time feedback, start here:     layrs.me/practice

  • Feed post number 129

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • Give a system design interview at Google, and you will probably be asked to design something for 100M+ users.
  • Sit in a system design round at AWS, and you will see the same scale in almost every question.
  • System design interviews often look nothing like your real job.
  • In the interview, you might design “Twitter at 100M QPS” but your day-to-day work might be:

    • Owning a couple of internal services that serve a few thousand requests per minute
    • Making one database and one cache behave nicely for a single region
    • Cleaning up flaky cron jobs, queues, and timeouts so customers stop seeing random failures

    So why is the interview so different from reality?

    • Big tech needs one bar for candidates from hundreds of teams, so they pick generic, large-scale prompts.
    • Extreme numbers force you to talk about trade-offs, bottlenecks, and failure modes clearly.
    • In 45 minutes, they cannot watch you work for months, so they simulate pressure, ambiguity, and growth.
    • Future proofing. You might join a “small” system today, but they want to know you can grow into bigger, more critical ones.

    So, how should you prepare smartly, without getting lost in the fantasy scale?

    This is exactly the problem Layrs solves, practice system design and scale your learning:     layrs.me/practice

    When you’re learning by solving system design problem, keep this in mind:

    1. Learn the primitives, not the brands
    Caching, databases, queues, load balancers, sharding, replication, rate limiting. Tools change, ideas stay.

    2. Start at a realistic scale, then dial it up
    First design for 1M users, then let the interviewer increase it and explain what breaks and what you would change.

    3. Use a simple structure every time
    Clarify requirements → pick data model → basic API → high level diagram → scaling → reliability and failure modes → trade-offs and extensions.

    4. Talk through trade-offs.
    Ex: “I am choosing X because it keeps writes simple, even though reads are slightly slower. For this product, write safety matters more.”

    5. Practice with small projects
    Build a tiny URL shortener, feed system, or job queue yourself. You remember designs much better when you have actually fought their bugs.

  • System design interviews are already hard and crazed interviewers like this make it even more harder.
  • I had the opportunity to learn from extremely talented seniors during my time at Google and system design was a topic I explored a lot about.
  • Yes, It’s true that deep fundamentals are crucial, but deriving a consensus protocol from scratch is almost never the right business decision, also you can’t do that in 1 hour.

  • Knowing when to "shop" for AWS S3 or Kafka is exactly what makes an engineer valuable, especially over Senior+ levels.

    Because no tech company pays you to reinvent the wheel. They pay you to use the wheel to build the car faster than the competition.

    System design has never been about unnecessary complexity. It's about being someone who understands the available tools well enough to pick the right one instantly.

    This is what it means to be a pragmatic engineer, and it's the core signal companies use to find a builder who gets things done.

    PS : Practice system design with real time feedback here :     layrs.me/practice

  • 5mo • Edited •
  • If you're an SDE-II, stop grinding for 10% raises. System Design is that one skill that can 2X your CTC in a single interview.
  • At SDE 2, nobody is impressed that you can ship tickets fast. That is the entry fee. If you want to go beyond that, system design is non-negotiable.
  • Let’s say you’re 1 of the 100 candidates for a L5 or equivalent Sr. role. All of you can code.
  • Most of you can solve medium-hard DSA.

    Only a handful can sit in front of a senior engineer and calmly walk through how to design a notification system for 50 million users, handle retries, failures, rate limits, and data growth.

    Guess who walks out with the 2X offer?

    AI has already eaten the easy part of your job
    – CRUD services
    – Basic REST APIs
    – Reference architectures from blog posts

    What it still struggles with is judgment

    + When is a simple relational database better than Kafka plus five extra services
    + How to design for failure modes, not just the happy flow
    + How to evolve a system from v1 to v3 without breaking everything
    + How to trade off consistency, latency, and cost for a very specific use case

    That judgment is what separates a plateaued SDE 2 from someone who is ready for senior level money and scope.

    That’s why I keep saying, don’t sleep on system design, it will change your life.

    Btw, If you are practising system design, give me a follow and check out     Layrs.me/practice
    – It’s an interactive platform where you learn by doing
    – 60+ problems available, and you can add your own
    – AI assisted feedback that pushes you to think from first principles

  • Feed post number 132

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • Saying you need microservices in a system design interview sounds cool until the Google interviewer asks why the bill is 50,000 dollars per day and it takes four days to debug a simple bug.
  • 17 things to consider before you say the word “microservices” in an interview:
  • 1. If one team of five can build it, a single service is usually fine.
  • 2. Every network call you add increases latency and failure points.
    3. Each service needs monitoring, dashboards, and alerts of its own.
    4. You now need proper logging and distributed tracing to debug flows.
    5. Local development gets harder, you cannot just run one process anymore.
    6. Data is now split, so you must handle cross service queries carefully.
    7. You lose simple ACID transactions, so think about sagas and compensation.
    8. Schema changes become versioned releases, not quick edits to one DB.
    9. Deployment is now a pipeline of many services, not one Docker image.
    10. You must plan service discovery and configuration management from day one.
    11. Retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers are mandatory, not optional.
    12. Partial failure is normal, so the system must degrade gracefully.
    13. You need a clear ownership map, one team per service, not everyone touching everything.
    14. Cost goes up, every tiny service still needs CPU, memory, and storage.
    15. Security boundaries multiply, you must secure every internal API and secret.
    16. Testing is harder, you need contract tests and good staging environments.
    17. Refactors are slower, because you coordinate changes across many services.

    If you can speak confidently about these trade-offs and justify your choices, then “microservices” sounds senior.

    If not, you are usually safer starting with a simple modular monolith and evolving from there.

    —
    Btw, if you’re preparing for dsa or system design, check out our Mock interview tool for system design on Layrs for free:     http://layrs.me/interviews

  • Feed post number 133

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • You are in a system design interview at Meta (which owns WhatsApp) for the IC4 role The interviewer leans in and asks:
  • "Why can I log into Discord or Slack on a new laptop and instantly see my old chats, but WhatsApp needs a backup or a long 'restoring history' step? I asked you to design a chat application. How would these two approaches change your system?"
  • Here is how you should break it down 👇

  • Both WhatsApp and Discord look like simple chat apps.
    Under the hood, they are built on two very different philosophies.

    Btw, we’ve recently launched our system design mock interview tool at Layrs. If you’re preparing for a switch or SD interviews, it’s free to use:     http://layrs.me/interviews

     1. WhatsApp – Phone is the source of truth

    Idea: Your device is the real database.
    The server is mostly a courier.

    What happens when you send a message:

    – You type the message.
    – It is encrypted on your phone with keys stored only on devices in that chat.
    – WhatsApp server receives the encrypted blob, stores it briefly, and forwards it to the receiver.
    – Once all devices in that chat acknowledge, the server is allowed to drop the message content.
    – Your long-term history lives in a local database on phone (SQLite style), plus whatever you back up to iCloud or Google Drive.

    What this means:

    – New phone, no backup = almost no history.
    – Multi device is hard, because every device needs keys and a copy of messages.
    – Server cost is lower, because they are not keeping infinite message history for every user.
    – Privacy is strong. Even WhatsApp cannot easily scan your chat content, because they do not keep decrypted data.

    In your design answer, you can mention:
    + Local storage on device
    + End to end encryption
    + Store and forward model
    + Optional cloud backup handled as a separate flow

    2. Discord or Slack – Server is the source of truth

    Idea: Servers hold a permanent log of messages. Your device is just a window.

    What happens when you send a message:

    – You hit send. The message goes to a backend service.
    – Backend writes it into a big distributed database or log store (for example Cassandra or ScyllaDB).
    – Indexes and partitions are based on channel, user, time, workspace, so reads scale horizontally.
    – Any device that logs in simply fetches messages from the server and renders them.

    What this means:

    – New laptop, new browser, office desktop, phone, all show the same history instantly.
    – Multi device is easy, because all devices just read from the same central store.
    – Server cost is higher. You are paying to store years of chat history and to replicate it.
    – Privacy tradeoff. Company can run search, analytics, and moderation on server side data.

    In your design answer, you can mention:

    + Distributed database for messages
    + Append only style message log
    + Search indexes, pagination, and caching
    + Stronger focus on availability and horizontal scalability

    Check my 1st comment as well:

  • Feed post number 134

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • One simple assumption just cost this engineer a position at Uber (SDE-1). By the time he realized the mistake, it was too late.
  • He saw the word “minimize” and did what most of us would do.
  • He jumped straight to Dynamic Programming.
  • With a few minutes left, the interviewer stopped him and asked:
    “Look at the range of possible answers. It is sorted in terms of sums, right”

    Every time you see “minimize” or “maximize”, it is not automatically DP.
    You have to train your brain to check other patterns first.

    Here is a 30-problem LeetCode list, split by patterns, that forces you to stop auto-selecting DP that is based on my learning from my DSA journey that landed me in Google.

    Btw, such mistakes don’t happen to anyone ever is a big part of the reason we created the DSA mock interview tool at Layrs. Check it out here for free:     http://layrs.me/interviews

     A. Binary search on answer problems
    Minimize or maximize some value, with a monotonic “possible or not” check.

    1. 410. Split Array Largest Sum
    Exact same idea as the Uber question, train the “try a max sum X and greedily check splits” reflex.

    2. 875. Koko Eating Bananas
    Practice checking “is speed X enough” instead of doing DP on bananas and hours.

    3. 1011. Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
    Very similar to 410, helps you get comfortable designing a simulation based check.

    4. 1482. Minimum Number of Days to Make m Bouquets
    Trains time based binary search, “in D days can I finish the task”.

    5. 1552. Magnetic Force Between Two Balls
    Maximize minimum distance, teaches “place items greedily and test if distance X works”.

    6. 774. Minimize Max Distance to Gas Station
    Continuous answer, but same idea, strengthens comfort with “answer is a real number but still monotonic”.

    7. 1283. Find the Smallest Divisor Given a Threshold
    Teaches you to turn “sum of ceil divisions ≤ threshold” into a monotonic predicate.

    8. 1760. Minimum Limit of Balls in a Bag
    Convert “operations to split items” into a check while searching on max bag size.

    9. 1891. Cutting Ribbons
    Maximize length of pieces, forces you to think “how many pieces if each is X” instead of knapsack.

    10. 2226. Maximum Candies Allocated to K Children
    Same style as 1891, more practice so pattern becomes second nature.

    11. 2187. Minimum Time to Complete Trips
    Rate based. Helps you recognise when answer is time, not a DP state.

    12. 1231. Divide Chocolate
    Very close cousin of Split Array, makes the “maximise minimum sweetness via binary search” instinct stronger.

    B. Greedy plus sorting problems
    Here “minimize” looks like DP again, but the right answer is sorted intervals or sorted costs.

    13. 435. Non overlapping intervals
    Sort by end time, keep maximal non intersecting set. Trains the classic interval greedy.

    14. 452. Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
    Same pattern as 435, helps you see that many interval questions share one greedy backbone.

  • Feed post number 135

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • Sit in for a Dev role at Google, they grill you on DSA for three or more rounds.
  • Sit in for a Dev role at Amazon, Uber, Microsoft same story.
  • Then you join the job and nobody is asking you to invert a binary tree to save the quarter. The interviews and the actual work often feel like two different worlds.
  • You can call it a broken process, but right now it is still the process.
    So the game is simple: learn how to perform on DSA without losing your mind or cheating your way through it.

    If I had to start over from zero, this is how I would train for DSA.

    Before we dive in - here’s a free DSA mock interview tool we built at Layrs. If you want structured practice with real feedback, go to     layrs.me/interviews.

    1. Win the first 30 minutes

    Most coding rounds are decided early.
    Practice solving the first 2 to 3 problems of a set in under 30 minutes.

    You want:
    - One easy problem in under 5 to 7 minutes
    - One more easy or easy medium in the next 10 minutes
    - One solid medium before the clock hits 30

    You are training speed and remaining calm.

    2. Train debugging as a real skill

    Guard your brain from “submit and pray.”
    For every problem you solve, spend a few minutes asking:

    - Which edge cases can break this
    - How I would find a failing test if the judge says wrong answer
    - What pattern of mistake I made

    People who climb rankings are not guessing more. They are fixing faster.

    3. Build a focused toolbox.

    Pick a small set of topics and get very good at them:

    - Arrays, hashes, two pointers, binary search
    - Classic DP and a bit of bitmask DP
    - Trees and graphs with BFS and DFS
    - Basic greedy and prefix sums

    You can add fancy stuff later.
    First make sure you can crush the common 80 percent.

    4. Use templates and a repeatable system

    Have ready skeletons for:
    - DFS and BFS
    - Common DP layouts
    - Binary search on answer
    - Graph adjacency lists

    The less time you spend fighting syntax,
    the more time you have to think.

    5. Measure progress.

    Instead of chasing green days, track:

    - Problems solved under 20 or 30 minutes
    - Problems you could fully explain to a friend
    - Topics where you still panic

    Interviews are not going to change tomorrow.
    Your approach can change tonight.

  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Meta, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Google, you’ll be grilled on system design and DSA
  • If you aim for a 1Cr+ role at Amazon, they’ll again go deep into your system design
  • System design is that one skill that levels up your:
  • – pay scale
    – responsibilities
    – impact on the company

    I asked 5+ L5 and equivalent engineers at Uber, Google, Meta and Microsoft which problems they would focus on if they had to prepare again. Here is a list of 40 system design questions I came up with:

    Before I go into the problems, I want to tell you about Layrs. The best way to learn system design is by doing, and that’s exactly what Layrs is all about. It’s the Leetcode of system design:

    We’ve just added our AI-assisted mock interview tools for both system design and DSA, you can use them for free here:     http://Layrs.me/interviews

    ➤ Tickets, travel, consumer apps
    1. Design an online movie ticket platform like BookMyShow.
    2. Design a hotel search and booking engine similar to     Booking.com.
    3. Design a ride booking platform for cabs.
    4. Design a fresh grocery delivery service.
    5. Design a food delivery platform.

    ➤ Social networks and feeds
    6. Design a microblogging platform like Twitter.
    7. Design a social news feed similar to Facebook.
    8. Design a community discussion site like Reddit.
    9. Design a nested comments system for posts.
    10. Design a voting system for posts and comments.

    ➤ Media, streaming and content
    11. Design a video streaming platform like YouTube or Netflix.
    12. Design a live video streaming service for events.
    13. Design a podcast hosting and listening platform.
    14. Design an image sharing and hosting system like Instagram.
    15. Design a movie reviews aggregation service.

    ➤ Messaging, notifications and collaboration
    16. Design a WhatsApp style chat and group messaging system.
    17. Design a push notification service for mobile and web.
    18. Design a webhook based notification delivery service.
    19. Design a tagging or hashtag service for user content.
    20. Design a collaborative online document or spreadsheet editor.

    ➤ Payments, wallets and fintech
    21. Design an online payment processing service.
    22. Design a digital wallet for peer payments and merchants.
    23. Design an ATM and bank account management backend.
    24. Design a scheduled digital transaction system for recurring payments.
    25. Design an online auction and bidding platform.

    ➤ Caching, rate limiting and infra
    26. Design a web cache service for frequently accessed content.
    27. Design an API rate limiting service to protect backend APIs.
    28. Design a job or task scheduling system.
    29. Design a migration strategy for a large scale caching layer with close to zero downtime.
    30. Design a distributed key value store.

  • Feed post number 137

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • An L5 Senior Engineer at Google makes ~90L to 1Cr.
  • An SDE III at Amazon makes ~90L to 1.1Cr+.
  • But the bar is very high, and system design depth is what gets you there.
  • If you're aiming for these roles in 2026, here is the checklist-style roadmap that has worked for strong seniors:

    1. Understand what system design really tests

    It is not about drawing boxes.
    It is about scale, tradeoffs, and thinking in 10x to 100x user growth.

    If you want to see how real interview questions look, try this:     layrs.me/practice

    2. Master the 10 core concepts

    Storage basics • Partitioning and sharding • Replication • Load balancing • Caching • CDNs
    Rate limiting • Async processing • CAP • PACELC
    If you cannot explain each in 3 minutes, it is not locked in.

    3. Learn with real product examples

    Tie every concept to a real feature:
    Instagram feed, Amazon cart, YouTube CDN, password change flow.
    Theory alone makes you sound junior.

    4. Practice with the RESHADED framework

    Requirements -> Estimations -> Storage -> High level -> APIs -> Detailed -> Evaluation -> Distinctive
    Use this for every mock. Build muscle memory.

    If you want guided mock interviews that follow this exact structure:     https://lnkd.in/gj7g4wnR

    5. Follow a 4 to 8 week plan

    Weeks 1 to 2: Foundations and examples
    Weeks 3 to 4: Classic designs (Feed, Media service, Chat, Ride sharing)
    Weeks 5 to 6: Bottlenecks, caching, sharding, rate limiting
    Weeks 7 to 8: 2 to 3 serious mocks per week

    6. Do not skip security and auth

    Where auth happens, token flow, revocation, background jobs.
    This is what separates mid level from senior.

    7. Treat system design like a 1 Cr skill

    Checklist for readiness:

    Know the 10 concepts well enough to teach them
    Can do RESHADED without notes
    Built 5 full designs
    Done 6 to 8 solid mocks
    Can talk caching, sharding, CAP and PACELC confidently

    If any box is empty, that is your starting point.

  • Learning and grinding 400 DSA problems might get you 20-30 LPA offers...
  • But it's system design that will take you from 30 to 60-80LPA+ as an SWE.
  • Here are some great resources that will help you master system design and get ready for this market:
  • Before diving into these resources, I want to tell you about the latest release on Layrs.. We’ve just added our system design mock interview too, along with our DSA mock interview tool as well.

    Both are available to try for free at:     https://lnkd.in/gj7g4wnR

    Why do mock interviews? Because:
    Learning without feedback is like guessing in the dark.
    Learning without real-world constraints leaves you unprepared for reality.
    Learning system design without building is like just flipping through slides.

    With this tool, you get everything you need to learn system design, right at Layrs:
    - 60+ problems
    - AI-assisted feedback and Interviews
    - An active community that shows up for each other

    So, use all these resources you want but don’t forget to apply them and learn at Layrs.

    Coming back to the resources:

    ➤ Great GitHub Repos to learn basics:
    - System Design Primer -     https://lnkd.in/gmTP7kwc
     - System Design 101 - https://lnkd.in/gyDYHhpF
     - Low-Level Design Primer - https://lnkd.in/g4aVVDue
     - Awesome System Design Resources - https://lnkd.in/gEX9FCaU
     - System Design Questions- https://lnkd.in/gzDhrk-J

     ➤ Some Must-Read Engineering Articles
    – How Discord stores trillions of messages:    https://lnkd.in/ezDPQzq6
    – Building In-Video Search at Netflix:https://lnkd.in/euENKEkS
    – How Canva scaled Media uploads from Zero to 50 Million per Day:https://lnkd.in/eDyAugu9
    – How Airbnb avoids double payments in a Distributed Payments System:https://lnkd.in/ekuAzhPh
    – Stripe’s payments APIs - The first 10 years: https://lnkd.in/eyXHiGXv
    – Real-time messaging at Slack: https://lnkd.in/e48uxfB7

    ➤ Some Must-Read Distributed Systems Papers
    – Paxos: The Part-Time Parliament:     https://lnkd.in/ew-z83GY
    – MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters: https://lnkd.in/e2FDfikq
    – The Google File System:https://lnkd.in/eTtjq3Hx
    – Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store:https://lnkd.in/eva6n2Ej

    – Spanner: Google’s Globally-Distributed Database:https://lnkd.in/eRM6crw8
    – Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data:
    https://lnkd.in/eqyS2aU4
    – ZooKeeper: Wait-free coordination for Internet-scale systems:https://lnkd.in/eADJaST3
    – The Log-Structured Merge-Tree (LSM-Tree):https://lnkd.in/eZ7iaXD8
    – The Chubby lock service for loosely-coupled distributed systems:https://lnkd.in/ehmtKEWJ

  • Feed post number 139

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • Don't think people who earn 6 LPA are failures...
  • Don't think people who earn 30 LPA were lucky...
  • Because sometimes… The only difference between the two
  • is just 6-12 months of intentional effort.

  • 6-12 months of deep focus
    6-12 months of saying no to distractions
    6-12 months of not giving up on yourself

    is enough to change your life.

    And over the years, the only thing I have seen that actually makes DSA prep easier is not motivation. It is a clear and repeatable system. Let me give you one:

    If you want to check your current level before starting, here’s a free mock interview tool:     layrs.me/interviews

    1. Start with a fixed problem list
    Most people fail because they solve random questions every day.
    One day arrays. Next day trees. Then they jump to dynamic programming.

    There is no continuity.

    Pick one defined list (you’ll find one at Layrs) and finish it end to end with full focus.
    Do not rush through it.
    Solve each problem properly.
    Understand why the solution works.

    2. Focus on patterns, not question counts
    Interviewers are not testing how many problems you solved.
    They are testing whether you recognize patterns.

    You should clearly know when to use:
    • Two pointers
    • Sliding window
    • Binary search
    • Recursion and backtracking
    • Stack and monotonic stack
    • BFS and DFS
    • Basic dynamic programming

    When patterns become natural, new problems stop feeling scary.

    3. Practice speaking while you solve
    Many people can solve on paper.
    They fail when they have to explain their thinking.

    So while practicing:
    • Say what you observe in the question
    • Say which pattern you think applies
    • Say what you will try first
    • Say why something failed
    • Say why the next approach worked

    This builds interview confidence.

    4. Put yourself in real interview pressure early
    DSA is not tested in silence. It is tested with a human watching, judging, and asking questions.

    That is why mock interviews matter more than people realize.

    You can use our free DSA mock interview tool here:     http://layrs.me/interviews

    It simulates real conditions:
    • You get a real problem
    • You work within time
    • You explain your thinking
    • You learn where you freeze

    5. Keep your pace boring and consistent
    Two problems a day for three months will beat:
    • Weekend marathon prep
    • Sudden bursts of motivation
    • One month of overwork followed by burnout

    DSA rewards boring consistency.
    And a clear system will carry you to the offer letter.

  • Feed post number 140

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • System design preparation:
  • You've watched 50 hours of system design videos.
  • You've memorized every diagram from Grokking.
  • You've bookmarked 30 articles on microservices
  • Results:
    Still no offer, and you're losing confidence in yourself.

    System design interviews aren't harder because companies have raised the bar.

    You're just preparing like it's still 2019.

    5-6 years ago, you could sketch a load balancer, add a database, call it "microservices" and pass.

    Now interviewers expect you to explain workflow engines, geospatial indexing, and real-time data pipelines.

    The problem isn't that there's more to learn.
    It's that most people are learning it wrong.

    Stop memorizing theory
    Start solving real problems.

    Pick a system.
    Design it from scratch.
    Like you're building it tomorrow.

    Try these 10 problems:

    1. URL Shortener:     https://lnkd.in/gtK5edXe
    2. Drive: https://lnkd.in/gy_qSfMS
    3. News Feed: https://lnkd.in/g9P3EXwY
    4. WhatsApp: https://lnkd.in/gmbHDDuT
    5. Uber: https://lnkd.in/gZ3ANnkg
    6. Instagram Stories: https://lnkd.in/gtVn_CAy
    7. YouTube: https://lnkd.in/grvU3Ens
    8. Calender: https://lnkd.in/gf2eCiYc
    9. E-commerce Checkout: https://lnkd.in/gbpxaqQs
    10. Google Docs: https://lnkd.in/gtPzfBrm

    After 5-6 problems, you'll notice patterns: caching, queues, rate limiting, and partitioning.

    That's when concepts click.

    This is exactly why we built Layrs (    layrs.me). Interactive canvas, real problems, instant feedback on your designs.

    No one expects perfection.

    They want to see you think through real constraints and explain your choices clearly.

  • Reading through feedback on our DSA mock interviewer this week, and honestly… it made my day.
  • A few people said it was the first time they felt "heard" by an AI interviewer. Someone else wrote that the conversational back-and-forth helped them realize why they were making certain mistakes - something they never got from grinding LeetCode alone.
  • We built it to recreate that feeling of having someone genuinely walk through your thought process with you - the way a good mentor or peer would. And based on the response, I think we nailed it.

  • That gave us the confidence to start building something new: a system design mock interviewer. Early tests are really promising - especially the way it guides you through tradeoff discussions without just handing you the answer.

    If you want to try the DSA interviewer, here's the link:     layrs.me/interviews

    Thank you all again for the positive response!

  • If I had an L4 or SDE-2 equivalent role schedule for Google, Amazon, or Uber 5-6 weeks from now, this is the exact list of questions I’d use to revise, study up, and test my DSA skills.
  • This is based on my earlier prep that landed me offers from Google, Flipkart, and many other startups. (Includes tagged questions from Microsoft, Meta, Flipkart, Uber, Google, Amazon, and many other companies)
  • 1. Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
  • 2. Course Schedule II
    3. Clone Graph
    4. Reorder List
    5. Add Two Numbers
    6. Subtree of Another Tree
    7. Non-Overlapping Intervals
    8. Binary Tree Right Side View
    9. Gas Station
    10. Partition Equal Subset Sum
    11. House Robber
    12. Word Break
    13. Kth Smallest Element in BST
    14. LCA of BST
    15. Insert Interval
    16. Counting Bits
    17. Majority Element
    18. Jump Game
    19. Coin Change
    20. Find Peak Element
    21. Validate BST
    22. Word Search
    23. Merge Intervals
    24. Search in Rotated Sorted Array
    25. Find Min in Rotated Sorted Array
    26. Course Schedule
    27. Number of Islands
    28. Flood Fill
    29. Single Number
    30. Missing Number
    31. Move Zeroes
    32. Next Greater Element
    33. Longest Increasing Subsequence
    34. Middle of Linked List
    35. Linked List Cycle
    36. Reverse Linked List
    37. Merge Two Sorted Lists
    38. Valid Parentheses
    39. Container With Most Water
    40. 3Sum

    The rest of this list is available at:     https://lnkd.in/euswhYdY

    –
    P.S: And while we’re on this topic, I also want to tell you about our latest addition to Layrs. After many months of effort, our very AI-Based DSA Mock Interview is live for everyone to use.

    Check it out:     layrs.me/interviews

    Combine this lest and this tool, you’ve got both a resource for learning DSA and then testing your skills. Constant testing will expose your gaps faster and help you grow.

    Good luck!

  • Feed post number 143

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • In 2025 alone, we watched:
  • – Amazon lay off 30,000 employees
  • – Accenture lay off 11,000 employees
  • – Salesforce lay off 4,000 employees
  • – Oracle lay off 3,000 employees

  • And thousands more across Big Tech and Fortune 500 companies.

    At this point, we need to be honest with ourselves.
    Job security as a concept is broken.

    Only one thing travels with you across layoffs, hiring freezes, and market cycles:

    Your skills.

    Today, for backend and full-stack engineers, security is built on two pillars:

    DSA.
    System Design.

    If you are waiting for layoffs to hit your team before sharpening your interview readiness, you are already late.

    If I could go back to my interview years with what I know now, I would not study system design from random blogs, scattered YouTube videos, and recycled frameworks.

    I would learn system design by practicing it end-to-end.
    That’s exactly why, inside Layrs, we added BYOP (Bring Your Own Problem).
    You can take any system design question - from an interview, a friend, or something you made up - and instantly generate a full practice environment for it: interactive canvas + constraints + assessment.

    You can access it here:     layrs.me/byop

    Because real mastery comes from solving real problems, not memorizing patterns.
    Here are 45 problems that cover almost every architecture pattern companies test today.
    If you can work through these properly, you won’t just “prepare for interviews” - you’ll understand how large systems behave in the real world.

    The market is not getting safer.
    But you can get stronger.

    Here are the problems:

    1️⃣ Messaging, Streaming and Event Systems

    Design a Distributed Stream Processing System like Kafka
    Design an API Rate Limiter
    Design a Distributed Queue like RabbitMQ
    Design a Surge Pricing System like Uber or Lyft
    Design a Distributed Tracing System
    Design an On-Call Escalation System
    Design a Live Comments System like Facebook Live
    Design Live Likes and Reactions

    2️⃣ Distributed Storage, Sync and Caching

    Design a Distributed Metrics Aggregation System
    Design a Key Value Store
    Design a Large Scale Sorting System
    Design Dropbox or Google Drive
    Design a Distributed Job Scheduler
    Design a Control Plane for a Distributed Database
    Design a File Sync and Download System
    Design a Large Cloud Data Migration System
    Design a P2P File Transfer System like BitTorrent

    3️⃣ Search, Ranking and Analytics

    Design Top K Most Shared Articles
    Design a User Analytics Pipeline like Google Analytics
    Design a Top K Rankings System like App Store or Amazon Bestsellers
    Design a Global Notification System
    Design an A B Testing and Experimentation Platform
    Design a Price Alert System
    Design Like Count for High Profile Users
    Design a Real Time Global Stock Price Viewer

    Continued in Comments:

    This is not “interview prep”.
    This is career insurance.

    Because when layoffs hit, the only people who move fast are the ones who stayed sharp when things looked stable.

  • Feed post number 144

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 5mo •
  • I cleared interviews at Flipkart, Google, and multiple startups between leaving Flipkart and joining Google. With my experience, I can tell you for a fact:
  • You don’t need to solve 500 DSA problems to crack good companies.
  • I have seen people solve 500 and still fail.
  • I have seen people cross 700 and still fail.
    I have also seen people solve 200 to 300 and convert strong offers.

    So what actually matters?

    A common and reliable benchmark is this: You should feel comfortable solving any medium-level problem in under 20-30 minutes without external help.

    Some people hit that level in 180 problems.
    Some need 250.
    Some need 350.

    The exact number does not matter.
    The outcome matters.

    If you still panic at mediums after 400 problems, your issue is not volume.
    It is how you practiced.

    Your brain works like a cache. What matters is which patterns are currently cached, not how many total problems you once solved.

    That is why this happens to so many working engineers coming back to DSA:

    * Problems you solved before suddenly feel unfamiliar
    * Confidence drops even after hundreds of solved questions
    * You waste hours on random lists, thinking you are progressing
    * Syntax feels rusty
    * Memory feels unreliable

    This is normal.
    DSA is not a once-and-done skill.
    It decays without focused retrieval and pattern reinforcement.

    And while we’re on this topic, I also want to tell you about our latest addition to Layrs. While discussing system design in the community, many told me how they would appreciate a DSA track as well.
    And after many months of effort, our very AI-Based DSA Mock Interview is live for everyone to use.

    Check it out:     layrs.me/interviews

    If you are confused about how many problems you should solve, use this simple filter:

    Do not ask,
    “How many problems have I solved?”

    Ask,
    “Can I solve unfamiliar medium problems calmly within 20 to 25 minutes?”

    If the answer is no, keep practicing.
    If the answer is yes, you are interview-ready.

    Volume without pattern control creates false confidence.
    Pattern control creates real offers.

    If you are restarting DSA prep after years in a full-time job, you are not behind.
    You just need a better system than brute force repetition.

    And yes, your brain really is just an LRU cache.
    The goal is to keep the right patterns hot.

  • The Avg. Comp for Meta E5 Level in India is over 75 Lakhs+, including bonuses and stocks, it goes over 1Cr+, and this is just at the Sr. Software Engineer level.
  • That is what Meta puts on the table if you can clear the process and it’s not too complicated:
  • - There are 2 DSA Rounds
  • - There is 1 System Design Round
  • - There is 1 Managerial / Behavioural Round

    Note: They’re also testing a new AI-based round, where you’re free to use AI but have to deliver a full functioning app in limited time.

    But they will grill you beyond what you can imagine, and that’s why you need bulletproof fundamentals, especially with DSA and System Design.

  • Feed post number 146

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 6mo •
  • He cleared all three DSA questions in a Google L4 onsite interview but still got rejected.
  • And then his application was put on a hiring freeze.
  • Now pause for a second and really think about that. About 95 percent of candidates never even make it past the first DSA round for L3 and L4 roles at Google.
  • This person crossed that barrier.
    He solved all the problems.
    And still did not make it through.

    And the reason for that is very clear.

    Even though he prepared well for DSA, he did not take the rest of the interview process into account.

    You see,
    – Amazon will not care how many years you spent learning DSA.
    – Google will not ask if you are a LeetCode knight or a guardian.
    – Microsoft will not even check how many contest badges you have collected.

    They already assume that if you reached this stage, you can solve problems.

    What they are actually judging is how you are coding, not just whether your final answer is correct.

    In his feedback, the interviewer clearly mentioned that even though his logic was correct and his variable names were fine, he had written the entire solution inside a single function.

    The code lacked readability. It was not modular enough. It was hard to follow. And that became a common concern across rounds.

    What can we learn from his experience?

    First, readability matters just as much as correctness.
    If your interviewer has to mentally fight your code to understand what is happening, your chances drop immediately. Clear structure always beats clever logic.

    Second, modularity is a signal of engineering maturity.
    Breaking your solution into two or three well-named helper functions tells the interviewer that you know how to build maintainable systems, not just throw together a working answer.

    Third, FAANG interviews are not competitive programming contests.
    They are judging how you would write production-level code inside a team, where other engineers must read, debug, and extend your work.

    Fourth, communication is part of the evaluation.
    Explaining what you are doing and why you are doing it while you are coding matters. Silence plus fast typing does not create confidence at L4 and above.

    So, you might prepare for solving problems.
    But also have to prepare for how engineers are actually evaluated.

    DSA gets you into the room.
    Your code quality, structure, and communication decide whether you stay.

    –
    P.S: While we’re on the subject of DSA, I am proud to announce, Layrs has finally launched our very own DSA mock interview tool for everyone to use.

    Go try it out, test your skills and give us your feedback:     layrs.me/interviews

  • Feed post number 147

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 6mo •
  • 20% of the fundamental system design topics that cover 80% of the interview questions in interviews at Google, Uber, Microsoft, and other top PBCs…
  • [O] Load Balancer
  • Types of Load Balancers (L7 vs L4)
  • Algorithms (Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hashing)
    Health Checks
    Sticky Sessions
    Scaling Strategies (Horizontal vs Vertical)
    Global Load Balancers
    Reverse Proxy

    [O] Application Server

    Stateless vs Stateful Servers
    Caching Strategies (In-memory, Local)
    Session Management (Cookies, Tokens)
    Concurrency Handling (Threads, Async)
    Microservices Architecture
    Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)
    Rate Limiting & Throttling

    [O] Database (SQL vs NoSQL)

    SQL vs NoSQL
    Sharding and Partitioning
    Replication (Primary-Secondary, Multi-Master)
    Consistency Models (CAP Theorem)
    Indexing Strategies
    Caching Layers
    Backup and Recovery

    [O] Pub-Sub / Producer-Consumer

    Messaging Patterns (Pub-Sub, Queue)
    Message Brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS/SNS)
    Idempotency
    Durability and Ordering
    Dead Letter Queues
    Scaling Consumers
    Eventual Consistency

    [O] Content Delivery Network (CDN)

    How CDNs Work (Edge Caching)
    Caching Policies (TTL, Invalidation)
    Geolocation Routing
    Static vs Dynamic Content Delivery
    SSL/TLS Termination
    Load Distribution
    DDoS Protection

    This list is a strong starting point - but system design is something you only really learn by seeing it in action.
    That’s exactly what     Layrs.me does.

    Want to go deeper into every topic above, with practice and real-time feedback?
    👉     Layrs.me Black Friday Sale is live. Grab your plan now.

  • Feed post number 148

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 6mo •
  • I invested 2+ hours and built this System Design cheat sheet for revision and interviews, based on my interactions with people who have cleared interviews at Google, Salesforce, Flipkart, Uber, Microsoft, and Amazon.
  • Btw, before we get into it, give me a follow if you like system design insights like this and if you’re studying system design for the first time, I’ve got a platform to help you reach your goals.
  • The best way to learn system design is to learn it by doing, and that’s exactly what Layrs.me helps you to do.

  • You get:
    – 60+ interactive problems
    – Option to add your own problem
    – Real-time, first-principles feedback
    – A community that shows up for each other

    ○ Networking

    DNS
    ↳ Resolves domain names to IP addresses using recursive resolvers and authoritative name servers while relying heavily on caching and TTLs.

    Load Balancers
    ↳ Distributes traffic across instances using Layer 4 for raw TCP routing and Layer 7 for intelligent routing using headers, paths, and cookies.

    CDN
    ↳ Delivers cached content from edge servers close to users to reduce latency and offload origin traffic.

    Proxies
    ↳ Forward proxies protect clients while reverse proxies protect servers, handle TLS termination, caching, and rate limiting.

    VPNs
    ↳ Creates encrypted tunnels over public internet for secure access to private networks.

    Firewalls
    ↳ Filters network traffic using packet rules, connection state, and application level inspection.

    NAT
    ↳ Translates private internal IPs to public IPs so multiple devices can access the internet using one address.

    Gateways
    ↳ Bridges different protocols and networks such as HTTP to gRPC and internal to external traffic.

    ---

    ○ Storage

    SQL Databases
    ↳ Structured relational databases with strong consistency and transactions like PostgreSQL and MySQL.

    NoSQL Databases
    ↳ Schema flexible databases optimized for scale like MongoDB, DynamoDB, and Cassandra.

    Object Storage
    ↳ Highly durable storage for unstructured data like images, videos, and backups.

    Block Storage
    ↳ High performance disk attached to VMs for databases and critical workloads.

    Distributed File Systems
    ↳ Throughput optimized file storage used in big data systems like HDFS and Ceph.

    Caching Systems
    ↳ In memory data stores like Redis and Memcached used to reduce latency and database load.

    Sharding
    ↳ Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple machines to scale write and storage capacity.

    ○ Compute

    Bare Metal Servers
    ↳ Physical machines offering full control and performance without virtualization overhead.

    Virtual Machines
    ↳ Software based machines that provide isolation and fast provisioning.

    Containers
    ↳ Lightweight runtime environments using Docker and Kubernetes for efficient deployment.

    Serverless Computing
    ↳ Event driven code execution without managing servers using AWS Lambda and similar platforms.

    PaaS
    ↳ Managed platforms that handle deployment, scaling, and infrastructure automatically.

  • Feed post number 149

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 6mo •
  • “I became a Senior Engineer at Google by memorizing the top 50 system design questions.”
  • Said no real engineer ever.
  • If memorization alone was enough, everyone who crammed YouTube playlists would be clearing L5 and L6 level interviews but there’s a reason they don’t.
  • Yes, repetition is a part of learning.
    Yes, patterns will definitely repeat.
    Yes, some facts and numbers naturally stick.

    But system design interviews are not memory tests.
    They test how you think when the problem is unfamiliar.

    1. You might memorize “Design Twitter,” but the interviewer will twist it with:
    – different traffic
    – different latency goals
    – different reliability expectations

    The moment the constraints change, memorized answers will fail you.

    2. System design is about tradeoffs, not templates

    Databases, caches, queues, sharding, replication. Every choice has a cost.
    If you cannot explain why you picked one over the other, memorization is exposed instantly.

    3. There is no single correct answer.

    They want to see:
    – how you ask clarifying questions
    – how you reason with partial information
    – how you adapt when assumptions break

    You cannot memorize judgment.

    4. Execution reveals understanding and you won’t be hired without it

    Anyone can say “use Kafka.”
    Very few can explain:
    – why Kafka over SQS
    – how partitions affect ordering
    – how consumer lag affects reliability

    That gap is not filled by rote learning.

    So yes, patterns help.
    Experience helps more.
    But first-principles thinking beats both.

    So, when it comes to system design, always start with thinking, not cramming.

    Also, if you are looking to practice your system design skills, check out     Layrs.me

    – A complete interactive system design platform
    – 60+ real world problems
    – You can add your own problems
    – AI-assisted feedback that forces first-principles thinking

  • Feed post number 150

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 6mo •
  • If I had an L5 System Design interview at Google next week, and only 2 or 3 good days to revise, here’s exactly what I would go over.
  • Because trust me you don’t want to design a sharded, geo-replicated, tier-cached monster on the whiteboard and then explode when the interviewer asks a simple question like
  • “So what happens when the user forgets their password?”

  • People love to spit out fancy jargon.
    But simple almost always scales better than complex.

    And the first rule of distributed systems
    is to never build one if you can solve the problem without it.
    They’re messy, expensive, slow to debug, and the source of most outages.

    Btw, before we get into it, give me a follow if you like system design insights like this and if you’re studying system design for the first time, I’ve got a platform to help you reach your goals.

    The best way to learn system design is to learn it by doing, and that’s exactly what     layrs.me helps you to do.

    You get:
    – 60+ interactive problems
    – Option to add your own problem
    – Real-time, first-principles feedback
    – A community that shows up for each other

    This is my short revision plan..

    – Networking Essentials

    (You need these to survive half the follow-up questions)

    • How the network stack actually works
    • TCP vs UDP vs QUIC
    • DNS resolution, TLS handshakes, HTTP lifecycle
    • REST vs RPC vs gRPC
    • Idempotency, retries, backoff, timeouts, and rate limiting
    • ETags, conditional requests, and how caching works
    • Load balancer basics (L4 vs L7, health checks, stickiness)

    – Data Storage Foundations

    (If this is shaky, everything you design collapses)

    • SQL vs NoSQL and when to choose which
    • B+ Trees, LSM Trees, WAL, MVCC
    • Isolation levels and common anomalies
    • Multi-tenant data layouts in SaaS apps

    – Distributed Systems

    (Treat this section like a weapons locker)

    • Sharding strategies
    • Replication models
    • Strong vs eventual consistency
    • Leader, follower, quorum
    • Time, clocks, drift, and ordering events in distributed systems

    If these concepts are clear, any big design, URL shortener, messaging queue, social feed, notifications, payment pipeline. becomes far easier to reason about.

    If they’re weak, even a tiny follow-up question can blow up your entire architecture.

    Keep it simple. Pick the right components.
    Explain tradeoffs. And don’t jump into distributed systems unless you absolutely must.
