  • Visit my website
  • 8mo • Edited •
  • A Junior of mine has just made the switch from Samsung Electronics (12 LPA) to Google (35+ LPA) in the current job market. This is his entire interview experience with Google that he shared with me.
  • ➱ Background
  • – Education: B.Tech, CSE, Tier-3 College
  • – Experience: 1.5+ years at Samsung R&D
    – Previous CTC: 12.5 LPA

    ➱ Google Offer Details (Bengaluru | L3)

    – Base: INR 23.1 LPA
    – Joining Bonus: 1.47 L
    – Relocation Bonus: 4 L
    – Variable Bonus: ~3.3 L (15% of base)
    – Stock Grant: USD 57K (vesting: 38% / 32% / 20% / 10% over 4 years)
    – Total Comp (Year 1): ~50.7 LPA

    ➱ Interview Process

    1. Resume Shortlist

    – Initial recruiter outreach (LinkedIn + phone)
    – Basic profile screening: role, coding profiles, past projects, timeline

    2. Phone Screen (DSA, 45 min)

    – Questions similar to:
    + Longest Consecutive Sequence
    + Painter's Partition Problem (variation)

    3. Onsite Rounds (4 Technical, 1 Googleyness, 45 min each)

    ○ Round 1: Dynamic Programming / Tree-based Problems

    + Coding problems similar to:
    • House Robber III (Stickler Thief)
    • Dungeon Game
    + Focus:
    • Designing optimal DP/state transitions
    • Handling tree recursion and edge cases
    • Discussing alternative solutions and time/space trade-offs

    ○ Round 2: Subsets, Partitions & Array Manipulation

    + Coding problems similar to:
    • Partition Equal Subset Sum
    • Partition Array into Two Arrays to Minimize Sum Difference
    + Focus:
    • Breaking down problems into subproblems
    • Backtracking, bitmasking, or advanced DP as needed
    • Walking through test cases and edge scenarios

    ○  Round 3: Advanced Scenarios / Graph & Optimization

    + Coding problems similar to:
    • Maximum Employees to Be Invited to a Meeting
    • Loss Interval Optimization
    + Focus:
    • Building custom algorithms from scratch
    • Working with graphs, cycles, and optimal selection
    • Dealing with constraints, handling tricky inputs, and thinking out loud

    ○Round 4: Complex Data Structures & Performance

    + Coding problem similar to:
    • Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
    + Focus:
    • Efficient use of segment trees/BSTs/binary indexed trees
    • Writing code that scales for large inputs
    • Explaining logic, handling follow-ups, and discussing optimization

    Round 5 (Googleyness): Behavioral/Culture Fit

    +Standard behavioral questions (teamwork, conflict resolution, prioritization, motivation for Google, etc.)

    Result:
    Offer received: Google SWE II (L3), Bengaluru | 1st year comp: ~50.7 LPA

    —
    P.S: I am sharing this because I took mock interviews to help him prepare.

    Also, I am proud to tell you that 2 of our community members who were users of     Layrs got placed at:
    – Salesforce SDE 2
    – DP-World SDE 2

    It’s been a few months after I’ve quit my 9-5 at     Google, and seeing results like this just gives me more power.

    Btw, if you haven’t tried     Layrs yet, so sign up, it’s Leetcode but for system design: layrs.me

  • Feed post number 194

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • I’ve conducted 30+ interviews at Google, Flipkart, and honestly, some of my best hires have been engineers who came from places with no support, tier-3/4 cities, or non-prestigious colleges, but had undeniable skill and hunger.
  • No two stories were ever the same.
  • It’s true that life is unfair and nothing is handed to you.
  • It’s true that some people will always have more privilege than you.

    But the only way to break through those barriers is to go all in on your goals and back it up with relentless consistency.

    I’ve seen people come from backgrounds where nobody even expected them to get into tech.
    But what they had was hunger, ownership, and a drive to keep learning.

    I’ve watched folks join with zero experience in teamwork.
    People who started off lost, but never lost their curiosity.
    They asked questions, made mistakes, and delivered anyway.

    Within months, they were:
    – Shipping features
    – Fixing broken things nobody else would touch
    – Communicating with stakeholders like pros
    – Taking on challenges well above their level

    They just showed up, figured it out, and grew with every step.

    That’s why I believe you don’t need a perfect background to break into tech.

    You need ownership. You need curiosity.
    You need the willingness to keep learning, even when nobody’s watching.

    Most people count themself out before they even start because of some reason in their head, I say, that’s a stupid way of holding yourself back.

    If I thought that way, I wouldn’t be here writing this post.

    You might not have head start.
    But just refuse to give up. Things will fall in place.

  • Feed post number 195

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • 80% of software development candidates appearing for system design interviews make these 13 mistakes and trap themselves in the wrong approach, ultimately leading to rejections.
  • Avoid them to become a better candidate (these are hard learned lessons from my journey):
  • 1. Believing that adding more caching layers always boosts performance.
  • It often adds unnecessary complexity and increases the risk of serving stale data.

    →  Always weigh the trade-offs, sometimes a simple cache strategy with well-defined TTL is better than stacking layers.

    2. Thinking that availability and reliability are the same thing.
    A system can be “up” and serving responses, but those responses might be completely wrong or useless.

    →  Measure both availability and correctness, build checks for data integrity, not just uptime.

    3. Assuming that scaling out always means better performance.
    Network overhead, coordination cost, or eventual consistency can actually slow you down at scale.

    →  Benchmark, measure, and plan for coordination and network limits before scaling out mindlessly.

    4. Expecting that databases will scale linearly as you add partitions.
    Hot partitions or uneven data distribution will still bottleneck your entire system.

    →  Always design for uniform data distribution and have plans for dealing with hot partitions from day one.

    5. Assuming queues never fill up.
    They do, and when they do, you’ll suddenly hit backpressure and the entire pipeline can choke.

    →  Monitor queue lengths and implement dead-letter queues or graceful degradation strategies.

    6. Treating CAP theorem as just theory.
    In production, you’ll be forced to pick two, your system can’t magically have all three.
    →  Understand the trade-offs and make explicit design decisions, document them, don’t just “hope for the best.”

    7. Believing load balancers always distribute traffic evenly.
    Session affinity, sticky sessions, or unevenly hashed keys often create hotspots and server overloads.

    →  Regularly audit your distribution, and use techniques like consistent hashing and dynamic rebalancing.

    8. Assuming sharding once solves scaling forever.
    Resharding, migrating data, or handling rapid growth is a nightmare most teams are unprepared for.

    →  Build your data model with re-sharding and migrations in mind; automate wherever possible.

    9. Thinking retries always solve packet loss.
    Retries can amplify network storms and make things worse during outages (the “thundering herd” problem).

    →  Use exponential backoff and circuit breakers instead of blind retries.

    Continued in Comments ↓

    —
    P.S: Check out our platform:     layrs.me.

    It’s like Leetcode but for system design, a path to learn in a structured and thorough manner which no theory, book or YT tutorial teaches you.

    Join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • Feed post number 196

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • Everyone loves getting deals and discounts during Big Billion Days, but for the engineering team at Flipkart, it can be pure hell.
  • I know because I’ve seen it up close.  Every year when Big Billion Days arrives, it takes me straight back to my time at Flipkart.
  • This time of the year, every team at Flipkart knew a big fight was coming, so the whole office had another vibe, it was almost electric.

  • Each team operated like its own startup, moving fast, owning its piece of the puzzle, and rallying together for one mission: to keep India shopping.

    For me and my team, this fight meant:
    - long nights
    - back-to-back shifts
    - continuously looking for alerts

    You have to understand, our monitoring systems were pushed to their limits, the traffic would be 2x more than usual, so the strain was very powerful, and if something broke, it would mean a lot of dissatisfied users.

    Every time those graphs shot up, I’d get that sinking feeling in my gut, fully aware that things could go sideways at any second.

    But we prepared for those moments all year. When something did break (and it always did), you just had to fix it, own it, and move on.

    When I moved on to Google, I took those lessons with me: How to design for scale, how to build for failure, and how to operate platforms that never sleep.

    And today, as we build     Layrs, those same lessons drive everything I do. Because no simulation can truly capture what it feels like to hold up a platform while the country is watching, clicking, and buying in real time.

    That experience at Flipkart taught me what it really means to keep India shopping 24/7. Now, I want the next generation of engineers to learn how to create tools and platforms that can run 24/7, serving millions, scaling gracefully, and never missing a beat.

    —
    P.S: Check out our platform:     layrs.me.

    It’s like Leetcode but for system design, a path to learn in a structured and thorough manner which no theory, book or YT tutorial teaches you.

    Join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • DSA gets you shortlisted. But it’s System Design that gets you the offer, especially in 2025. Basic system design knowledge is required for an SDE role, especially if you’re going for top tech companies.
  • I collected the 16 most complex system design problems so you can put your skills to the test. These are from (Facebook, Google, Expedia, Bloomberg, Amazon, Atlassian, Uber, Patreon & LinkedIn)
  • 1. Design Typeahead Suggestion/Autocomplete

  • - Companies: Facebook, Google, Expedia, Bloomberg,
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Build a fast, scalable autocomplete system that predicts user input in real time.

    2. Design an API Rate Limiter

    - Companies: Amazon, Atlassian, Uber, Patreon,
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Create a reliable system to limit API usage per user or IP while scaling globally.

    3. Identify the K Most Shared Articles in Various Time Windows

    - Companies: LinkedIn, Facebook, Twitter
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Track and retrieve the top-K trending articles within multiple rolling time frames.

    4. System to Collect Performance Metrics from Thousands of Servers

    - Companies: Google, Datadog, Amazon, eBay,
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Design a distributed system to collect, store, and analyze server performance metrics at scale.

    5. Design a Distributed Queue like RabbitMQ

    - Companies: Amazon, Apple, Instacart
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Architect a robust, distributed message queue system for asynchronous processing.

    6. Design Google Analytics - User Analytics Dashboard and Pipeline

    - Companies: Microsoft, Facebook, Qualtrics, Google
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Build a platform to track, process, and visualize user analytics events at massive scale.

    7. Design a Notification Service at Scale

    - Companies: Google, Pinterest, OCI, Stubhub,
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Create a system to deliver millions of timely notifications to users reliably.

    8. Netflix: Limit the Number of Screens Each User Can Watch

    - Companies: Some FAANG
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Enforce screen limits for user accounts in a global video streaming platform.

    9. Design an A/B Testing System (like Optimizely)

    - Companies: Affirm, Some FAANG
    - Complexity: Hard
    - Build a scalable system to run, track, and analyze online experiments across products.

    Continued in Comments ↓

    P.S: You can learn all of these concepts and how they play out in real-life scenarios by using our system design learning platform:     layrs.me.

  • Feed post number 198

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • >>  System Design - Ultimate Cheat Sheet For Revisions & Interviews <<
  • [1] Networking
  • ◦ DNS -- Resolves domain names to IP addresses for routing
  • ◦ Load Balancer -- Distributes incoming traffic evenly across servers
  • ◦ CDN -- Delivers cached content from edge servers near users
    ◦ Proxy -- Forwards and filters requests for security/performance
    ◦ VPN -- Encrypts and tunnels traffic for private access
    ◦ Firewall -- Blocks or allows traffic based on rules
    ◦ NAT -- Maps internal private IPs to public IPs
    ◦ Gateway -- Connects different network types or protocols

    [2] Storage
    ◦ SQL Database -- Structured, relational storage with ACID properties
    ◦ NoSQL Database -- Flexible, scalable storage for unstructured data
    ◦ Object Storage -- Stores large unstructured files as objects (e.g., S3)
    ◦ Block Storage -- Low-latency data blocks for VMs/DBs
    ◦ File System -- Shared access to files across network (e.g., NFS, HDFS)
    ◦ Caching -- Speeds up reads with in-memory data (e.g., Redis)
    ◦ Sharding -- Partitions data horizontally for scalability

    [3] Compute
    ◦ Bare Metal -- Dedicated hardware servers for raw performance
    ◦ VM -- Virtualized servers running isolated environments
    ◦ Container -- Lightweight, portable app environments (Docker/K8s)
    ◦ Serverless -- Runs code in response to events, no server management
    ◦ PaaS -- Platforms to deploy code without infra setup
    ◦ Edge Computing -- Processes data close to user for low latency

    [4] Communication
    ◦ API -- Interfaces for system-to-system communication (REST/gRPC)
    ◦ Message Queue -- Buffers and delivers async messages between services
    ◦ WebSocket -- Enables two-way real-time communication
    ◦ RPC -- Remotely invoke procedures between services
    ◦ Pub/Sub -- Scalable broadcast/subscribe for events
    ◦ Service Mesh -- Manages traffic and observability in microservices

    [5] Architecture Patterns
    ◦ Monolith -- Single deployable app with all components
    ◦ Microservices -- Independent, API-driven services
    ◦ Event-Driven -- Reacts to and processes events in real time
    ◦ Serverless -- Stateless, event-triggered functions
    ◦ Distributed Systems -- Components spread across multiple nodes

    Continued in comments

    —
    P.S: You can learn all of these concepts and how they play out in real-life scenarios by using our system design learning platform:     layrs.me.

    I quit my job at Google to change how everyone studies system design, on     Layrs, you can see the architecture, components, and how everyone works, and simulate a real-life situation, whether it is for 100 people or 1M.

    Go check it out:     layrs.me

  • Feed post number 199

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • If you copy Netflix’s system design to build YouTube, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy WhatsApp’s backend to build Slack, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Flipkart’s database architecture to build Amazon Web Services, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Twitter’s real-time feed design to build LinkedIn, it will 100% fail.
  • If you copy Zomato’s scaling approach to build Swiggy’s delivery engine, it will 100% fail.

  • Why?

    Because two products can look similar from the outside, but beneath the UI, their workloads, scaling challenges, and priorities are totally different.

    Let’s break it down with an example:

    Netflix vs. YouTube might look similar. But they’re not even close.

    Netflix

    ↳ Long-form, pre-produced content (think 30–120 min episodes)
    ↳ Content is published at known intervals, rarely “bursts”
    ↳ 99% of the time, everyone is watching the same files (“Stranger Things” S3E1 is the same everywhere)
    ↳ Heavy pre-caching via global CDNs can predict demand days in advance
    ↳ Personalization drives what you see, but not what you watch, the catalog is mostly static

    YouTube

    ↳ Wild mix: 30-second shorts to 12-hour live streams
    ↳ Anyone, anywhere, can upload anytime, real “unpredictable” traffic
    ↳ Viral spikes: one new video can draw 50M views in hours, worldwide
    ↳ UGC (User Generated Content) = constant writes, real-time metadata, comments, notifications
    ↳ CDN can help, but can’t pre-cache a trending video that didn’t exist an hour ago
    ↳ Personalization, search, and real-time engagement are mission-critical

    So, even though both platforms stream video at scale:
    Netflix can “cheat” by pre-caching and optimizing for a mostly fixed catalog.

    YouTube must handle unpredictable uploads, real-time spikes, and millions of tiny metadata writes per second. Copy Netflix’s approach, and YouTube collapses the next time a viral video drops.

    System design is not “CTRL+C, CTRL+V.”


    It’s a deep analysis:
    – What are my real bottlenecks?
    – What’s predictable vs. spiky?
    – Is my traffic read-heavy or write-heavy?
    – What must be consistent vs. eventually consistent?
    – Where do my users’ needs change week to week?

    If you build for someone else’s scale, problems, and team,
    You’ll inherit their tradeoffs, and your system will break in ways you never anticipated.

    Never build by imitation. Build by understanding.

    –
    This is exactly why we are building     layrs.me, a new way of learning system design. Just like Leetcode helps you learn problem-solving, Layrs helps you learn system design.

    1. Sign up on     Layrs.me
    2. Pick any problem, go to our interactive canvas
    3. Use the components to plan out a flow, put up constraints
    4. Get proper feedback on your design with our AI assistant
    5. Join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • Feed post number 200

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • If I were having system design interviews at Uber, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta in the next 2 months...this is exactly how I would prepare (based on my experience of 5+ years in the Industry):
  • 1. Get Clear on What System Design Actually Tests
  • ⤻What you’re really being judged on:
  • → How you break down an ambiguous problem
    → How you weigh trade-offs (performance, scalability, cost, maintainability)
    → How you communicate complexity simply
    → How well you handle constraints, edge cases, and real-world failures
    There’s no “perfect answer.” The goal is to show how you think, not just what you know.

    ⤻The main interview focus:
    • Product Design: (e.g. “Design Uber’s backend”)
    • Infrastructure Design: (e.g. “Design a rate-limiter”)
    • Object-Oriented: (e.g. “Parking lot system,” less common for SDE2+)
    • Frontend/App: (skip for most backend interviews)

    In almost every company, you’re judged on:
    → Problem Solving
    → Solution Design
    → Technical Depth
    → Communication Clarity

    2. Refresh The Fundamentals (Don’t Skip This)

    Before you solve big problems, nail the basics that every interviewer expects. Focus on depth over “checking boxes.”

    (Check the 1st Comment, I’ve put all the main fundamentals there)

    3. Master The Building Blocks

    You don’t need to memorize every tech out there. Just deeply understand these 7 core components (know pros, cons, and failure modes):

    –Server/Compute: Monolith, microservice, serverless
    –Database: SQL, NoSQL (document, key-value, column)
    –Cache: Redis, Memcached (cache-aside, read/write-through)
    –Message Queue: Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS (async, decouple, backpressure)
    –Load Balancer: Distributes traffic, handles failover
    –Blob Storage: S3, GCS (for unstructured, large objects)
    –CDN: Edge caching for speed, offloading origin, DDoS protection

    Bonus: Know how you’d monitor/log/alert for each piece.

    4. Work Backwards From Common Problems (PRACTICE > THEORY)

    Passive learning is a trap.

    Pick up the most asked system design questions. Don’t only watch YouTube breakdowns - actually practice sketching, whiteboarding, and thinking aloud, and you can do it all on our platform -     layrs.me.

    Join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

    It’s like Leetcode for system design, which helps you give a proper, structured plan with problems so you understand what you need to learn to stand out.

    1. Design a URL Shortener (Bitly)
    2. Design Dropbox
    3. Design Ticketmaster
    4. Design a News Feed
    5. Design WhatsApp

    5. Mock Interviews & Feedback Loops

    Collect feedback, act on it. Find your blind spots (did you skip failure scenarios, did you not justify a choice, did you fumble API design?). You’ll see patterns repeat after 3–5 interviews.

    You’ll never “know it all.” But you can get dramatically better, fast, if you build on the fundamentals, master your building blocks, and drill through those 10 classic problems with practice and feedback.

    You got this.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 8mo • Edited •
  • Redis is open source.
  • Nginx is open source.
  • PostgreSQL, Docker, Kubernetes, RabbitMQ, MongoDB, Prometheus, Grafana - all open and accessible.
  • GitHub Actions even has a free tier.
  • You don’t need a certificate to design real-world systems.
    The docs are public. The code is one git clone away.
    – Set up a load balancer with Nginx or HAProxy.
    – Build a pub/sub queue with RabbitMQ or Kafka.
    – Store logs and metrics with Prometheus, Grafana, or ELK.
    – Architect a fault-tolerant API with PostgreSQL, Redis, and stateless containers.
    – Automate CI/CD with GitHub Actions.
    – Simulate failures, recover, and deploy again.

    It doesn’t matter if you learned from YouTube, a paid bootcamp, or trial and error.

    What matters is whether you can break a system, find the root cause, and ship the fix.

    Don’t wait for the “perfect” course or the “right” time.

    Pick a system design pattern, open the docs, and start building.

    That’s how you actually learn.

    Everything else is just delay.

    P.S. I quit my job at     Google 2 months ago to build Layrs (layrs.me)
    - a platform where you can practice system design hands-on.

    Since then, 1000s of engineers have already started learning and improving.
    I’d love for you to give it a try.

  • Feed post number 203

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • In 2023, Amazon Prime Video made a big change by changing its live stream monitoring service. They moved a critical component from a microservices architecture to a monolith.
  • This targeted change saved 90% of operational costs and significantly improved scalability. Here’s why they made the move, and what we can actually learn from it
  • [0] The Problem

  • ➥The live stream quality monitoring service for Prime Video was designed as a distributed, serverless system: multiple Lambda functions, AWS Step Functions for orchestration, and a mesh of fine-grained microservices.

    ➥Each user device could upload segments of their watched stream back to Amazon for quality analysis, frames/images, and audio snippets were then passed between microservices.

    But two main bottlenecks emerged:

    ➥ Orchestration Overhead: AWS Step Functions added latency and cost, with state transitions and hand-offs between services.

    ➥ Data Transfer Costs: Large amounts of media data (frames/audio) were constantly written to and read from S3 buckets between microservices, leading to both high latency and high AWS bills.

    As they tried to scale to monitor more streams, both cost and technical complexity became unmanageable. They hit hard scaling limits at just 5% of the expected load.

    [0] The Solution

    ➥ Collapsed the service into a single-process “monolith” (but just for this one monitoring component, not the whole Prime Video stack).

    ➥ All steps, media conversion, frame extraction, detection, result aggregation were now run in-memory within the same process (or at least within a tightly coupled ECS cluster), removing the need to shuttle data between distributed services.

    ➥ Reduced S3 writes/reads and internal network overhead. Orchestration became simple function calls inside the same process, not distributed workflows.

    ➥ For horizontal scaling, they just spun up multiple replicas (ECS tasks), each handling a subset of detectors, instead of sharding every microservice independently.

    ➥ The “monolith” is still containerized and deployable, but much tighter than dozens of scattered serverless functions.

    [0] Key Lessons in the comments, do check them out ↓

    —
    P.S:  Reading posts like this or sketching diagrams in a notebook alone won’t cut it for real system design interviews.

    You need to see the system bottlenecks, test your designs, and get feedback, just like you’d face in a high-stakes interview.

    That’s exactly why we built     Layrs (layrs.me)

    If you’re serious about levelling up with system design, check out the platform. Also, join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • Feed post number 204

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • I broke my team’s entire data pipeline at Flipkart by a simple batch job, delayed about 3,00,000+ shipments, and still reported to work the next day. When it comes to learning from failures, I’ve learned a great many lessons from my experiences.
  • This was when I was an SDE-1, back then, we were rolling out a new feature to include delivery distances in payouts. The initial design was straightforward:
  • 1. Fetch a batch of shipments.
  • 2. Call an external API for each shipment’s lat/long.
    3. Calculate routes and distances.

    For a while, it felt like a smart solution. But code that works on some 10,000 records can crumble at millions.

    The day it failed, it failed hard.

    Minutes became hours, queues ballooned, dashboards turned red, and everyone wanted answers.

    My “elegant” batch job had spun out millions of API calls, grinding the whole pipeline to a halt. There’s a special feeling when you realize you are the reason a major e-commerce operation is delayed.

    You learn to sit with that discomfort. But you also learn to fix it.

    We tore the process down and rebuilt it from first principles. Instead of waiting for a batch to accumulate, we started reacting in real time.

    Every new shipment triggered an event, fetched the lat/long instantly, and persisted the results. By the time the batch job ran, all the heavy lifting was already done.

    So now, batch processing wasn’t a bottleneck, it was just a final pass, operating on pre-enriched data.

    What used to take all night now ran in a fraction of the time. The pipeline was not just faster, but finally resilient and predictable.

    The lesson I learned was this: Batching is useful, but it can hide dangerous inefficiencies at scale.

    If a step is slow, costly, or critical, don’t push it off till later. Do it upfront, in real time, and let your batches focus on what they do best: moving lots of simple, clean data.

    Every engineer remembers their first big failure.
    If you’re lucky, you also remember the day you fixed it, too.

    —
    P.S: I believe in failing fast and failing forward, and it’s a principle I am also using in     Layrs (layrs.me), it’s Leetcode but for system design.

    I’ve quit my job at     Google, it’s been 2 months and I’m full-time all in on this platform, you can learn the lessons here with real-time scale and constraints, so you never mess up in interviews and get the practical insights needed to move to better roles.

    Check it out, it’s free to use:     layrs.me
    Join our discord: https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • Feed post number 205

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • 20% of the fundamental system design topics that cover 80% of the interview questions I’ve seen in interviews at Google, Uber, Microsoft, and other top PBCs…
  • [O] Load Balancer
  • Types of Load Balancers (L7 vs L4)
  • Algorithms (Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hashing)
    Health Checks
    Sticky Sessions
    Scaling Strategies (Horizontal vs Vertical)
    Global Load Balancers
    Reverse Proxy

    [O] Application Server

    Stateless vs Stateful Servers
    Caching Strategies (In-memory, Local)
    Session Management (Cookies, Tokens)
    Concurrency Handling (Threads, Async)
    Microservices Architecture
    Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)
    Rate Limiting & Throttling

    [O] Database (SQL vs NoSQL)

    SQL vs NoSQL
    Sharding and Partitioning
    Replication (Primary-Secondary, Multi-Master)
    Consistency Models (CAP Theorem)
    Indexing Strategies
    Caching Layers
    Backup and Recovery

    [O] Pub-Sub / Producer-Consumer

    Messaging Patterns (Pub-Sub, Queue)
    Message Brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS/SNS)
    Idempotency
    Durability and Ordering
    Dead Letter Queues
    Scaling Consumers
    Eventual Consistency

    [O] Content Delivery Network (CDN)

    How CDNs Work (Edge Caching)
    Caching Policies (TTL, Invalidation)
    Geolocation Routing
    Static vs Dynamic Content Delivery
    SSL/TLS Termination
    Load Distribution
    DDoS Protection

    This list alone is not enough but a great starting point and you know where you can learn each of these topics and see them play out in real time? Go to     Layrs ( layrs.me)

    I quit my job at Google to build this platform and change the way we learn system design for every engineer out there.

    Also join our Discord Server, we would love to hear from you:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 8mo •
  • If you interview at Google for mid-to-senior roles, nearly every other round tests your system design skills.
  • At Amazon? More than half of your interviews is about system design.
  • At Microsoft? You can expect up to 60% of your rounds to go deep on architecture and design.
  • At Meta? 70%+ of questions will challenge how you build and scale real systems.
  • At Uber? Same story, system design is the backbone of the process.

    The more senior the role, the more system design dominates the interview, and with AI accelerating everything, even SDE-1s will soon need to master these fundamentals.

    Here are the most asked questions, so you can start preparing today, which will train you on the fundamentals that are continuously tested.

    1. Social Networks & Messaging

    ↬ Design Twitter Feed
    ↬ Design Instagram Feed
    ↬ Design WhatsApp Messaging
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design Twitter Direct Messages
    ↬ Design Instagram Stories
    ↬ Design Instagram Ads
    ↬ Design Twitter Notifications
    ↬ Design Tinder
    ↬ Design Reddit
    ↬ Design Facebook News Feed
    ↬ Design LinkedIn Connections
    ↬ Design Notification Service
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger Payments

    2. Media Streaming & Recommendations

    ↬ Design Spotify Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Recommendations
    ↬ Design YouTube Streaming
    ↬ Design Spotify Connect
    ↬ Design Spotify Discover Weekly
    ↬ Design Instagram Reels
    ↬ Design TikTok Feed
    ↬ Design YouTube
    ↬ Design Amazon Recommendations

    3. E-commerce & Marketplaces

    ↬ Design Amazon Shopping Cart
    ↬ Design Amazon E-commerce Store
    ↬ Design Shopify Store
    ↬ Design Flight Booking System
    ↬ Design Stock Exchange System
    ↬ Design Payment System
    ↬ Design Uber Eats
    ↬ Design Uber Dispatch System
    ↬ Design Food Delivery App
    ↬ Design Uber Pool
    ↬ Design Uber Freight
    ↬ Design Booking System (BookMyShow)

    4. File Storage, Collaboration & Productivity

    ↬ Design Google Drive
    ↬ Design Google Docs
    ↬ Design Dropbox File Sharing
    ↬ Design Distributed Cloud Storage (S3)
    ↬ Design Online Code Editor
    ↬ Design Code Deployment System
    ↬ Design File Sharing System
    ↬ Design Distributed Web Crawler

    5. Infrastructure & Distributed Systems

    ↬ Design Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)
    ↬ Design Amazon SQS
    ↬ Design Distributed Locking Service
    ↬ Design Amazon EC2
    ↬ Design Amazon DynamoDB
    ↬ Design Amazon Redshift
    ↬ Design AWS S3
    ↬ Design Google Analytics
    ↬ Design Analytics Platform (Metrics & Logging)

    Check comments for more questions ↓

    –

  • Feed post number 207

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 8mo •
  • Top tech companies like Microsoft, Google, and Amazon pay 20-30 LPA for SDE-1 roles and 40-50 LPA for SDE-2 roles.
  • But they’ll never give you a direct, textbook problem in a real system design interview. Instead, here’s what you might get: The Celebrity Problem
  • Imagine you’ve built a social media app like Instagram. Most users have a few hundred followers, and everything works fine with your NoSQL database, partitioned by user ID.

  • Now, a celebrity joins. Suddenly, millions want to follow him and like his posts in real time. That one database partition built for average users now handles 95% of all traffic.

    Your “evenly distributed” backend is collapsing.

    So what would you do to solve this?

    ● First, introduce caching.
    Add a distributed cache (like Redis or Memcached) before your database. Now, when people view his posts, the cache handles most of the reads, taking the pressure off that hot partition.

    ● Next, scale out your writes.
    Reads are easier, but what about millions of likes in a few seconds?

    You need to split up his posts or even the likes themselves across multiple database partitions (“sharding”), so you’re not limited by a single partition’s write capacity.

    ● Update your business logic.
    Now your application needs to know where data is stored, across multiple partitions or buckets. When you show total likes, your service aggregates numbers from all shards.

    ● Plan for real-world problems.
    With more shards and caching, you might have to live with eventual consistency in like counts and handle more complex code for aggregation and data routing.

    ● Expect follow-up questions.
    – What if your cache goes down?
    – How do you protect your backend from massive write spikes?
    – How do you keep things consistent for users?

    This is why companies ask these types of problems in interviews. They want to know if you can spot hidden bottlenecks, scale beyond the basics, and make tough calls under pressure.

    And this is why just memorizing blog posts, drawing boxes, or using buzzwords never works for real interviews.

    That’s why you need to see all the components in real time and then think about these solutions.

    Thanks to     Layrs.me, the platform I am building, you can solve problems like this on an interactive canvas, get feedback on your decisions, and actually build the skills that interviews demand.

    If you want to move past textbook prep and think like a real SDE, try     Layrs (layrs.me) and see what difference real practice makes. It is 100% free to try, no paywall, just sign up and get started.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • 8mo •
  • I asked this question to 50+ engineers during system design mock interviews, and only 30% could give me the right answer. I asked: “What can you use Redis for other than caching?”
  • Redis is an open-source, in-memory data structure store that can function as a database, cache, and message broker.
  • Most engineers think of Redis just for caching, but it’s far more versatile. Here are 20 powerful use cases you might not know:

  • 1. Cache database queries and web page responses for a limited time with TTL
    2. Message queue for asynchronous processing
    3. Distributed locking to orchestrate access for a shared resource
    4. Throttle requests for load smoothening
    5. Session store for fast retrieval and update of user data
    6. Rate limit the web server requests
    7. Real-time leaderboard using sorted sets
    8. Approximate the cardinality of a large set using HyperLogLog
    9. Real-time messaging using Pub-Sub
    10. Find nearby points using the Geospatial index
    11. Data analytics using Time series data type
    12. Social network timeline using the List data type
    13. Full-text search using RedisSearch
    14. Storing nested JSON without re-serialization costs using RedisJSON
    15. Use as a GraphDB with the RedisGraph module
    16. Job scheduler using sorted sets
    17. Social feed recommendation without duplicates using the bloom filter
    18. Distributed counter using atomic operations such as INCR
    19. E-commerce shopping cart using Redis hashes
    20. Avoid duplicate requests by storing idempotency keys with an expiry

    P.S.: How many of these use cases did you already know?

    P.P.S: You can learn all of these use cases and how they play out in real-life scenarios for by using our system design learning platform:     layrs.me.

    I quit my job at     Google to change how everyone studies system design, on Layrs, you can see the architecture, components, and how everyone works, and simulate a real-life situation, whether it is for 100 people or 1M.

    Go check it out:     layrs.me.

  • Feed post number 210

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • If you want to perform better in system design interviews, spend time understanding these trade-offs, and your interviews will go 10x better.
  • Because when you're building systems, every decision is a trade-off. This is where most candidates fail, they can’t explain why they made a choice. Don’t let that be you.
  • 1. Core Concepts
  • The fundamentals you can’t skip.

    – Scalability vs Performance: Handle more work or make the system faster — rarely both at the same time.

    – Latency vs Throughput: Quick responses vs high volume processing; real-time vs batch-focused needs.

    – SQL vs NoSQL Databases: Structured, reliable queries vs flexible, scalable, schema-less data storage.

    – Stateful vs Stateless Architecture: Systems that remember session context vs fully independent requests.


    2. Foundational Trade-offs
    Principles that shape every architecture decision.

    – Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling: Upgrade one server or add more servers for distributed growth.

    – Consistency vs Availability (CAP Theorem): Always accurate data vs systems that never go down.

    – Strong vs Eventual Consistency: Immediate accuracy vs short delays for better performance and uptime.

    – Normalization vs Denormalization: Clean, duplicate-free storage vs faster reads with some redundancy.


    3. System Optimization
    Patterns for performance and reliability.

    – Read-Through vs Write-Through Cache: Cache when requested vs write to cache and database simultaneously.

    – Synchronous vs Asynchronous Processing: Wait for each task vs process tasks in the background.

    – Batch vs Stream Processing: Process data in bulk vs handle it in real time as it arrives.

    – Long Polling vs WebSockets: Periodically request updates vs keep a live, two-way connection.


    4. Advanced Architecture
    High-level decisions that define modern systems.

    – Monolithic vs Microservices Architecture: One unified codebase vs many smaller, independent services.

    – TCP vs UDP: Reliable, ordered delivery vs faster, less reliable transmission.

    – REST vs GraphQL: Fixed endpoints with standard responses vs flexible, efficient queries for exactly what you need.


    If you can talk through these trade-offs with clarity, you’ll stand out in system design interviews.
    It’s not just about what you build, t’s about why you built it that way.
    Which of these trade-offs do you still struggle with?

    —
    P.S: If you’re trying to master system design, check out my platform,     layrs.me. I quit my job at Google to change the way we learn system design.

     Layrs will help you build, understand, and learn how system design looks in real life. From understanding these trade-offs, you can see how constraints, components, and architecture all come together to power the biggest systems in the world.

  • Feed post number 211

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • I recently had a chance to connect with a Sr. Software Engineer at Meta who started his journey from TCS and in the last 12 years cracked Flipkart, Google, Amazon, and Meta.
  • He came from a Tier-4 college, started in 2013, and is now at Meta. I asked him: If you had to give a Jr. Engineer a simple strategy to learn system design in 2025, what would it look like?
  • Here’s what he gave me:

  • 1. Get Your Basics Right

    – If you don’t know how a request flows from browser to backend, or why databases are used, start there.
    – Basics first. Know the building blocks: API, DB, cache, load balancer, queues.

    2. Pick One Simple Approach and Stick With It

    – Don’t try to follow 10 YouTubers and 15 books. Pick one mentor, one guide, and actually follow through.
    – Too much theory is a trap. Consistency with 1 resource > more information.

    3. Practice With System Design Questions

    – Start with the problem statement, not the solution.
    – Use resources that give clear requirements (not vague one-liners). Good problems have context and constraints.
    – For this you can use the very platform that we are building,     Layrs (layrs.me, it’s Leetcode but for system design and it’s free to use)

    4. Simulate The Interview Every Time

    – Open a whiteboard
    – Set a timer, don’t take 2 hours. Give yourself 35–45 minutes, no breaks.
    – Speak out loud and sketch as if someone’s in front of you.
    – If you get stuck, write the question mark and move on. Don’t quit, don’t Google.

    5. Fill Your Knowledge Gaps, Right After

    – When you’re done, look at all the places you stumbled or had zero clue.
    – Google, ask ChatGPT, or check your notes to learn those points.
    – Fix the leaks in your understanding before you forget.
    – This is also where you can use     Layrs, and see which components you have no clue about.

    (Continued in Comments ↓)

    System Design prep is not a reading exercise. It’s about struggling, stumbling, and fixing your weak spots until thinking about big systems feels normal.

    Every single candidate who cracked big offers did this, not something fancier.

    Show up, do the work, and don’t get distracted by noise.
    You got this.
    –
    P.S: You can also catch us on our official     Layrs Discord: (https://lnkd.in/gCsRee8T)

  • Feed post number 212

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • 39 Software Engineers answered this question in mock interview discussions, only 13 got it right.
  • Over the last 6 weeks, I've been having fun system design discussions with many users. One of the questions I asked is this:
  • "Each second, Uber handles 1 Million+ user requests to find nearby drivers with almost zero crashes. How does it match you with a driver in seconds when you request a ride?"

  • Let me break it down:

    1. Uber doesn’t look everywhere, just where you actually are

    – Every driver’s live location is stored in a smart data structure that divides the city into grids (think “Google Maps for algorithms”).
    –Instead of scanning the whole city, Uber narrows the search to only drivers inside your grid cell (or a few adjacent cells).
    – This means less data to process and ultra-fast lookups, latency stays low, even if there are a million users.

    Btw, when I explained this, I was using the BYOP feature on     Layrs.me. I basically quit my job at Google to build Layrs (Leetcode for system design). It’s free, fun, and helps you learn system design by building in real time.

    If you haven’t already, do check it out. It’s pretty simple to use it:
    1. Sign up on     Layrs.me (it’s free, I’m the founder )
    2. Pick any problem, go to our interactive canvas
    3. Use the components to plan out a flow, put up constraints
    4. Get proper feedback on your design with our AI assistant

    2. Your request is basically spammed to drivers, instantly

    – Your ride request isn’t broadcast to every driver, it’s sent only to those nearby, using pub/sub messaging.

    – Technologies like WebSockets keep an always-on, two-way channel open between your phone and Uber’s backend.

    – The moment you tap, your request is “published” and instantly “subscribed to” by drivers’ apps in your area.

    3. It’s not just ‘who’s closest’, it’s a mini contest every time

    – The dispatch system considers:
    +Who’s truly closest by driving time (not just straight-line distance)
    +Which drivers have a good history (fewer cancellations, high ratings)
    +Surge pricing zones, maybe some drivers are in a busy area, others are not
    +Probability of driver cancelling (based on behavior, ratings, etc.)

    - It runs a mini-auction: multiple drivers can accept, but the system picks the best fit based on many factors.

    4. If a driver ghosts you, you’ll never know

    – Sometimes, a driver accepts your ride but suddenly goes offline (maybe network drop, maybe app crash).
    – Uber’s system is built to retry instantly, the request goes to the next best driver without you even knowing.
    – This automatic failover keeps your experience smooth, even if things break under the hood.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • Solving some 200-300 good LeetCode problems will only get you past the 1st coding round. You still have to master system design if you’re thinking of 40-50+ Packages at top PBCs..
  • Let me help you start with the very fundamentals you should cover:
  • - Types of Load Balancers - Application Layer (L7) vs Network Layer (L4).
  • - Algorithms - Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hashing.
    - Health Checks - Monitoring server availability and performance.
    - Sticky Sessions - Keeping user sessions tied to specific servers.
    - Scaling Strategies - Horizontal vs Vertical scaling with load balancers.
    - Global Load Balancers - Handling traffic across multiple regions.
    - Reverse Proxy - Serving as a gateway and caching responses.

    Before you take a look at the rest of the resources, let me tell you that:
    One thing that will make all the difference is building and getting feedback.

    Learning system design without building is like just flipping through slides.
    Learning without feedback is like guessing in the dark.
    Learning without real-world constraints leaves you unprepared for reality.

    So, use all these resources, but then use all of that knowledge to test yourself on our free platform,     Layrs (Layrs.me)

    1. Sign up on     Layrs.me (it’s free, I’m the founder )
    2. Pick any problem, go to our interactive canvas
    3. Use the components to plan out a flow, put up constraints
    4. Get proper feedback on your design with our AI assistant

    Join the Discord:     https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq

    Continued with the topics ↓

    - Stateless vs Stateful Servers - When to use which.
    - Caching Strategies - In-memory caching (Redis/Memcached) and local caching.
    - Session Management - Cookies vs Tokens (JWT).
    - Concurrency Handling - Managing multiple requests with threads or async models.
    - Microservices Architecture - Service discovery and inter-service communication.
    - Containerization - Docker, Kubernetes, and deployment strategies.
    - Rate Limiting & Throttling - Preventing abuse and managing traffic bursts.

    - SQL vs NoSQL - When to choose which database type.
    - Sharding and Partitioning - Horizontal scaling techniques.
    - Replication - Primary-Secondary, Multi-Master setups for reliability.
    - Consistency Models - Strong vs Eventual Consistency (CAP theorem).
    - Indexing Strategies - Improving query performance.
    - Caching Layers - Redis, Memcached for faster reads.
    - Backup and Recovery - Disaster recovery planning and failover systems.

    - Messaging Patterns - Pub-Sub vs Queue-based systems.
    - Message Brokers - Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS/SNS.
    - Idempotency - Avoiding duplicate message processing.
    - Durability and Ordering - Ensuring messages aren’t lost or misordered.
    - Dead Letter Queues - Handling failed messages.
    - Scaling Consumers - Parallel processing and worker pools.
    - Eventual Consistency - Maintaining consistency with asynchronous systems.

  • Feed post number 214

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • The day I resigned from Google I was 80% expecting the whole thing would be my manager telling me how I’m making a mistake.
  • When I thought about how the conversation would go with my parents, I was expecting them basically to call me an idiot and say, “You will quit your 9-5 to work 24X7 and earn nothing?” Make that make sense..
  • But I couldn’t have been more wrong.

  • Even though my manager at Google was recently assigned to me and it was my first 1-1 meeting with him, the conversation couldn’t have been smoother. I was so surprised at how supportive my manager was.

    Similarly, I was preparing my parents, but I was expecting a huge dramatic moment, my parents surprised me, too, they were supportive as well.

    Now, it has been almost 2 months since I put in my papers at Google and started building     Layrs (layrs.me) full-time.

    My whole resignation process taught me that yes, even though most of our decisions in life will affect the people around us, if you deliver what you say, earn their trust, and give your best, they will trust you.

    There’s nothing more confidence-building than your whole tribe cheering for you, from your colleagues to family. I feel blessed.

    And I can tell you that at least 50+ told me that:
    - take more time, do it when you’re ready
    - Don’t quit your job yet, there’s too much risk
    - there’s layoffs all around. Why are you quitting now?

    The startup world is an uncharted territory for me, but that makes it equal parts thrilling and terrifying. This is exactly what I signed up for when I quit; I had nothing else in mind.

    So here we are,     Gaurav Pal and I, both pushing each other to go beyond anything we’ve ever done in our lives.

    —
    P.S: If you’re hearing about     Layrs for the first time, imagine Leetcode, but it teaches you system design, that’s exactly what Layrs is. Go check out the platform, it’s free. I’m the founder xD.

  • Feed post number 215

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • “I've studied over 100 system design case studies. I've memorized every pattern I could find on the internet. Can I crack Google's system design round?”
  • My honest answer: Probably not, and here’s exactly why.
  • Let me tell you what really happens in a high-stakes interview. A candidate walks in, full of confidence. They think they know every pattern and all the buzzwords, such as sharding, load balancing, CQRS, and so on.

  • Now, say, I give them a problem:

    "Design a URL Shortener like     Bit.ly."

    Immediately, they start diagramming. They mention DNS, load balancers, consistent hashing, distributed databases, cache layers, and even rate limiting. Their answer looks perfect on paper.

    But then I shift the problem just a little:

    "Let’s say 80% of your traffic is suddenly coming from India, but your only database is in the U.S. Users are complaining about high latency. What’s your solution?"

    That’s where most candidates freeze because they haven’t worked with real-world constraints; we do not live in an ideal world.

    That’s why system design is not about how many patterns you can memorize. It’s about how well you can adapt your solution to real, evolving constraints, scale, geography, latency, cost, and failure recovery.

    A great engineer doesn’t just throw patterns at the problem.
    They ask the right questions:

    - Where exactly are users facing delays?
    - Should we set up a read replica in India, or use edge caching?
    - How do we deal with cache consistency and failover?
    - What if our Mumbai cache cluster fails at peak traffic?

    It’s not enough to recite theory. You need to demonstrate that you can reason through trade-offs and design for real business needs, not just textbook examples.

    If you want to bridge the gap between theory and real-world engineering, stop memorizing and start practicing with actual problems.

    And that’s the whole point of why I quit my job at     Google and started building layrs.me, it’s LeetCode but for system design.

    Here’s how you can use it to start learning system design and never be tricked by the interviewer:     https://lnkd.in/gEVzCSDu

    You can also catch us on our Discord: (https://lnkd.in/g9wGRadq)

  • Feed post number 216

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • Cracking a Senior SDE role at Google?
  • → 100% impossible if you can’t design a real system with scale, trade-offs, and failure in mind.
  • Getting through a backend round at Amazon for a Senior Role?
  • → 100% impossible if your system design answer is just a bunch of boxes and buzzwords.

  • Landing an L4/L5 offer at     Microsoft or Meta?
    → 100% impossible if you can’t explain why your design works and what breaks under pressure.

    You don’t get rejected in system design rounds because you “don’t know enough.”
    You get rejected because you never practiced building, defending, and fixing real systems.

    Most engineers only ever:

    – Memorize a few diagrams,
    – Read a dozen blog posts,
    – Copy other people’s notes…

    But in the actual interview. You’re asked to map out a system, justify your trade-offs, predict failures, and handle scale live.

    If you haven’t built those muscles, you freeze, you bluff, or you get exposed.

    The result? Down-level offers, endless rejections, and “come back in 6 months.”

    Here’s what you should do instead:

    1. Work on real, open-ended system design problems
    — not just reading blog posts or memorizing slides.

    2. Sketch out the full data flow, not just high-level boxes
    — think through how data moves, where it bottlenecks, and what breaks at scale.

    3. Practice breaking down requirements, choosing the right components,
    and justifying every architectural decision.

    4. Simulate failures and edge cases
    — walk through what happens when a service crashes, when data spikes, or when latency jumps.

    5. Time yourself
    — practice explaining your design, trade-offs, and reasoning in 45–60 minutes, just like the real interview.

    6. Get instant, actionable feedback
    — don’t wait for a one-time mock; get feedback as you build, so you learn what actually works.

    7.Fix and improve your designs after review
    — go back, iterate, and make your solution better with each attempt.

    8. Create your own custom problems if you want to stretch further
    — test yourself on scenarios you haven’t seen before.

    9. Discuss your solutions with real engineers
    — join a community, get challenged, and learn how others approach the same problem.

    And you know where you can all of this and more, at     layrs.me.
    In fact, that’s why we built     Layrs, it’s Leetcode for system design.

    – Practice on an interactive canvas
    – Drag, drop, and explain your architecture
    – Get feedback after every problem
    – Try BYOP (Bring Your Own Problem) if you want
    – Join our Discord, discuss with real engineers

    Sign up today, DM me if you would like to know more, the platform is free to use!

  • Feed post number 217

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo • Edited •
  • After grinding for 2+ years, an engineer from Infosys finally managed to get an interview from Amazon and Google.
  • He wanted to go for the SWE-3 role, and he was qualified enough, but there was just one problem...
  • Google offered him SWE-2 because he couldn't do well in his system design rounds, even though he was a solid engineer. Amazon? Flat-out rejection…

  • And if you ask me, the real problem is how we learn system design.

    You learn coding and problem-solving by grinding LeetCode, building projects, and writing code that runs.

    But when it comes to system design?
    People just read slides. Draw a few boxes.
    Memorize buzzwords. Then walk into interviews and hope it’s enough.
    That’s not how it works.
    You wouldn’t call yourself a good coder if all you did was read syntax and look at someone else’s GitHub repo.

    So why do we treat system design like it’s just a theoretical subject?
    Staying surface-level is just fooling yourself.
    If you want to crack L4/L5 roles, the game is different in 2025.

    This is why we built     Layrs (Layrs.me), Leetcode for system design.

    Here’s how we’re helping 100s of engineers learn system design, the way top engineers do:
    1. Sign up at     Layrs.me (it’s free, I’m the founder).
    2. Pick any real-world problem, then plan it out on our interactive canvas.
    3. Drag and drop components, add constraints, and map out your solution, just like you would in a real interview.
    4. Get detailed feedback from our AI assistant, so you know exactly what you’re missing.
    5. By your 5th or 6th problem, you’ll know what each component does, how it scales, and when to use it.
    6. By the 10th problem, you will have a much better perspective on SD problems.
    And if you don’t see the problem you want?
    Create your own with our BYOP (Bring Your Own Problem) feature.

    Layrs.me is the only platform that helps you put your theory into action.

    Try     Layrs.me today, also join our Discord: (https://lnkd.in/gHt-n5Sy)

    Build. Get feedback. Repeat.
    Don’t let traditional prep hold you back from the offer you deserve.

  • Learning and grinding 400 DSA problems might get you 20-30 LPA offers...
  • But it's system design that will take you from 30 to 60-80LPA+ as an SWE.
  • Here are some great resources that will help you master system design and get ready for this market:
  • Before diving into these resources, there’s one thing that makes all the difference: building and getting feedback.

    Learning system design without building is like just flipping through slides.
    Learning without feedback is like guessing in the dark.
    Learning without real-world constraints leaves you unprepared for reality.

    So, use all these resources, but then use all of that knowledge to test yourself on out free platform,     Layrs (It’s Leetcode but for system design)

    1. Sign up on     layrs.me (it’s free, I’m the founder )
    2. Pick any problem, go to our interactive canvas
    3. Use the components to plan out a flow, put up constraints
    4. Get proper feedback on your design with our AI assistant

    Join the discord:     https://lnkd.in/gnjkdNqS

    Coming back to the resources:

    ➤ Great GitHub Repos to learn basics:
    - System Design Primer -     https://lnkd.in/gmTP7kwc
     - System Design 101 - https://lnkd.in/gyDYHhpF
     - Low-Level Design Primer - https://lnkd.in/g4aVVDue
     - Awesome System Design Resources - https://lnkd.in/gEX9FCaU
     - System Design Questions- https://lnkd.in/gzDhrk-J

     ➤ Some Must-Read Engineering Articles
    – How Discord stores trillions of messages:    https://lnkd.in/ezDPQzq6
    – Building In-Video Search at Netflix:https://lnkd.in/euENKEkS
    – How Canva scaled Media uploads from Zero to 50 Million per Day:https://lnkd.in/eDyAugu9
    – How Airbnb avoids double payments in a Distributed Payments System:https://lnkd.in/ekuAzhPh
    – Stripe’s payments APIs - The first 10 years: https://lnkd.in/eyXHiGXv
    – Real-time messaging at Slack: https://lnkd.in/e48uxfB7

    ➤ Some Must-Read Distributed Systems Papers
    – Paxos: The Part-Time Parliament:     https://lnkd.in/ew-z83GY
    – MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters: https://lnkd.in/e2FDfikq
    – The Google File System:https://lnkd.in/eTtjq3Hx
    – Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store:https://lnkd.in/eva6n2Ej
    – Kafka: a Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing:https://lnkd.in/eTPA4ey9
    – Spanner: Google’s Globally-Distributed Database:https://lnkd.in/eRM6crw8
    – Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data:
    https://lnkd.in/eqyS2aU4
    – ZooKeeper: Wait-free coordination for Internet-scale systems:https://lnkd.in/eADJaST3
    – The Log-Structured Merge-Tree (LSM-Tree):https://lnkd.in/eZ7iaXD8
    – The Chubby lock service for loosely-coupled distributed systems:https://lnkd.in/ehmtKEWJ

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Thursday was one of the best days of my founder journey since leaving Google and starting up with layrs.me.
  • Gaurav Pal and I got to sit down with Gaurav Sen (GKCS).
  • If you’re a software engineer in India, you probably know him as the OG of system design content on YouTube, and founder of InterviewReady. For almost a decade, he’s been shaping how engineers think about systems.

  • We spoke about     Layrs, how it went from a risky idea to a real product, and the challenges of building in this space. In return, Gaurav shared hard-earned lessons from his own journey.

    A few things that stuck with me:
    – Fundamentals compound over time
    – Long-term thinking beats short-term wins
    – Teaching is as much about iteration as building products

    I left with a notepad full of ideas - and a reminder that the most valuable conversations happen when builders share openly.

    Would recommend to any founder/engineer:
    – Meet the people you respect in your field
    – Exchange notes, not just stories
    – Take away insights you can apply immediately

    Grateful for the exchange, GKCS - one of those talks that stays with you

    👉 If you’re building or learning system design, check out     layrs.me - we’re building the platform I wish I had when I was starting out.

    Join our discord:     https://discord.gg/

  • Feed post number 220

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 9mo • Edited •
  • Amazon pays 37 Lakhs+/ year to SDE-2 Engineers and will 100% ask you to give system design interviews while hiring.
  • Microsoft pays 40 Lakhs+/year to SDE-2 Engineers and will 100% ask you to give system design interviews while hiring.
  • Right now in this market, I’m even hearing that SDE-1 positions also have system design rounds because of AI becoming the norm.

  • So DSA might get you in the interview loop, but it’s system design that will give you the offer letter.

    90% of the System Design interviews I’ve given in the last 5 years…
    …have revolved around just these of the most repeated, practical problems.

    If you’re preparing for FAANG+ interviews or just trying to learn System Design the right way, this is the MUST-SOLVE LIST.

    I've categorized them based on themes:

    1. Social Networks & Messaging

    ↬ Design Twitter Feed
    ↬ Design Instagram Feed
    ↬ Design WhatsApp Messaging
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger
    ↬ Design Twitter Direct Messages
    ↬ Design Instagram Stories
    ↬ Design Instagram Ads
    ↬ Design Twitter Notifications
    ↬ Design Tinder
    ↬ Design Reddit
    ↬ Design Facebook News Feed
    ↬ Design LinkedIn Connections
    ↬ Design Notification Service
    ↬ Design Facebook Messenger Payments

    2. Media Streaming & Recommendations

    ↬ Design Spotify Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Playlist
    ↬ Design Netflix Recommendations
    ↬ Design YouTube Streaming
    ↬ Design Spotify Connect
    ↬ Design Spotify Discover Weekly
    ↬ Design Instagram Reels
    ↬ Design TikTok Feed
    ↬ Design YouTube
    ↬ Design Amazon Recommendations

    3. E-commerce & Marketplaces

    ↬ Design Amazon Shopping Cart
    ↬ Design Amazon E-commerce Store
    ↬ Design Shopify Store
    ↬ Design Flight Booking System
    ↬ Design Stock Exchange System
    ↬ Design Payment System
    ↬ Design Uber Eats
    ↬ Design Uber Dispatch System
    ↬ Design Food Delivery App
    ↬ Design Uber Pool
    ↬ Design Uber Freight
    ↬ Design Booking System (BookMyShow)

    4. File Storage, Collaboration & Productivity

    ↬ Design Google Drive
    ↬ Design Google Docs
    ↬ Design Dropbox File Sharing
    ↬ Design Distributed Cloud Storage (S3)
    ↬ Design Online Code Editor
    ↬ Design Code Deployment System
    ↬ Design File Sharing System
    ↬ Design Distributed Web Crawler

    5. Infrastructure & Distributed Systems

    ↬ Design Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)
    ↬ Design Amazon SQS
    ↬ Design Distributed Locking Service
    ↬ Design Amazon EC2
    ↬ Design Amazon DynamoDB
    ↬ Design Amazon Redshift
    ↬ Design AWS S3
    ↬ Design Google Analytics
    ↬ Design Analytics Platform (Metrics & Logging)

    Check comments for more questions ↓

    –
    P.S: You can find all of these problems on     layrs.me, It’s LeetCode but for learning System Design, we’re constantly adding more to our list.

    I quit my job at     Google to build this platform and change the way we learn system design for every engineer out there.

    Also join our Discord Server, we’re quite active and would love to hear from you:     https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie

  • Feed post number 221

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • 3 years ago, my manager at Flipkart looked me in the eye and said, “Only 30% of people I’ve ever worked with have gotten projects like this so early in their career. Make the most of this project with the payouts team.”
  • This was almost a year after the week I thought I’d be fired, because I had pushed a change that affected nearly a million product listings. It wasn’t malicious, but it was careless as I missed an edge case. And it hurt.
  • So how does someone who thought they were getting fired end up in the 30% who get high-trust projects?

  • I made one decision: to be a reliable engineer.
    What that looked like, day to day:
    → I owned my mistake in writing: postmortem, fix, guardrails.
    → I shipped small PRs with tests and rollback notes. Every change could be undone in minutes.
    → I wrote runbooks for the boring corners: batch jobs, retries, edge cases. Anyone could use them at 3 AM.
    → I watched the numbers: p95, error rate, costs. If something drifted, I raised it before others did.
    → I communicated like a clock: short updates, clear risks, with no surprises.
    → I shared with others on the very paths I had tripped on, and it helped me back.

    Months later, my manager handed me a critical project in payouts: distance-based payout calculations. I didn’t get it because I was the smartest.
    I got it because the team knew I would finish what I started, surface risks early, and keep customers safe.
    We built it like this: shadow traffic first, synthetic tests, a canary with tight alerts, a hard rollback plan, and documentation someone could follow half-asleep. It launched clean. It’s still some of my best work in my career.

    The lesson is simple:
    Fancy work gets attention.
    Reliable work earns trust.

    And trust is the gate to every “unusually early” opportunity you want.
    If you’re in a rough patch, start here today: one honest postmortem, one flaky area stabilized, one metric watched daily, one small PR with a clear rollback. Do that for 90 days.
    People will start planning around you, not past you.

    P.S: I am bringing those same lessons with me over to     layrs.me, as I and Gaurav Pal a better and improved way to learn system design.

    Also, join our Discord, we’re quite active here and would love to hear from you:     https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie

  • Feed post number 222

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • I am an ex-Google Software Engineer with 5+ years of experience. I’ve worked at Flipkart, Google, and Fivetran. I recently quit my job to pursue my passion for system design and build Layrs (layrs.me)
  • The best advice on how to start learning system design that made me fall in love with it and made my career I can give you, if you’re just starting out is this:
  • Most folks start by googling a million buzzwords, “sharding, load balancer, CAP theorem”, thinking they’ll somehow just remember them all.

  • But that’s not how you get better.
    Don’t start with concepts, start with real problems.

    Pick real-life systems and try to design them from scratch, as if someone gave you the project on day one.

    Here are 10 basic problems that’ll teach you everything you need to begin:

    1. URL Shortener (Bitly)

    ➥ You’ll figure out how to generate unique links, store them, and handle billions of requests.
    ➥ Forces you to think about databases, hashing, and what happens when things collide.

    2. Dropbox (Cloud File Storage)

    ➥ How do you upload and sync files across devices?
    ➥ What if two people upload the same file at the same time?
    ➥ Suddenly, file versioning and conflict resolution aren’t just words.

    3. News Feed (Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn)

    ➥ Not just “show posts” but how do you rank, fetch, and deliver the right content, in real-time, for millions of users?

    4. WhatsApp (Chat App)

    ➥ Instant messaging is easy… until you think about offline users, delivery receipts, message history, and scale.

    5. Uber (Ride-Hailing)

    ➥ How do you match drivers and riders, track everyone live, handle payments, and deal with location updates every second?

    (Check 5 more in the comments)

    Here’s exactly how to practice:
    → Sign up On     layrs.me, it’s a platform we are building for system design learners.
    → Pick a problem from the list on     Layrs.
    → Then use the elements on the interactive canvas and design
    → Open up a notepad or whiteboard to make notes.
    → Set a 45-minute-1 hour timer. Start sketching out on the canvas.
    → If you get stuck, write down exactly where
    (e.g. “How do I send notifications if the user is offline?”)
    → After you’re done, get an assessment from     Layrs.
    → Look where you lacked, fill in the gaps, and read up on it.
    → Save your notes in Notion, Google Docs, whatever works.

    You’ll notice that, after 5–6 problems, the same ideas keep recurring: caching, scaling, queues, rate limiting, APIs, and storage.

    Now you have a way better idea of how these concepts fit in, so go in-depth and study all the new concepts.

    No one expects you to be perfect. But if you show you can think about real problems and explain your approach, you’re already ahead.

    If you want more detail on how I’d break down any of these, just ask.
    Happy to share examples or my own notes.

    Join our discord:     https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie

  • Feed post number 223

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • I almost got fired from Flipkart within just 1 week of joining because I launched a feature that uploaded incorrect data into 1 Million Entires!!
  • This is one of the craziest stories of my career, and every time I think of it, it just gets crazier.
  • During my first week at Flipkart, I was assigned a critical task related to cron batch jobs that update entries in a database.

  • I wrote the logic, did testing, and presented results to the team. We did an internal demo and then made the feature live.

    But, little did I know that I missed a corner case, and as a result, about a million entries were updated incorrectly. Yes, 1 million.

    Realizing what had happened, I was sure I was going to get fired; it was my first job out of college, and I was quite new to the corporate world.

    But despite the consequences, I took ownership and identified the bug as well. After getting to the root cause, I communicated everything to my manager, and we discussed everything calmly.

    I was surprised at how it went. He asked me the cause, gave me the task to fix it, and guided me as well. A few hours later, everything was back on track.

    In hindsight, the fix took barely a few lines of code. But what actually mattered was seeing how my manager handled it, with such a great demeanor and just helping me through it.

    That experience changed how I look at work forever.
    I learned that most engineering disasters don’t kill you.
    It’s how you react, fix, and move on that actually counts.

    And sometimes, you just need one calm person in the room for everything to fall back into place.

    Btw, Flipkart is the place where I met my co-founder,     Gaurav Pal, after leaving Flipkart, we both went to separate places but never lost touch.

    Now, we’ve both quit our jobs and are Building     Layrs, It’s Leetcode but for system design.

    If you haven’t already heard about the platform, check it out:     layrs.me
    Also, join our Discord, we’re quite active here and would love to hear from you: https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie

  • Feed post number 224

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • Learning system design without building is just reading slides.
  • Learning system design without feedback is just guessing in the dark.
  • Learning system design without understanding real-world constraints is setting yourself up for failure
  • Drop the habit of drawing boxes without thinking through data flow.
  • Drop the habit of naming components you don’t fully understand.
    Drop the habit of copying diagrams without knowing their limitations.
    Drop the habit of skipping capacity planning because it “won’t be asked.”
    Drop the habit of ignoring failure scenarios in your designs.
    Drop the habit of treating every problem like it needs microservices.
    Drop the habit of never asking “who uses this, and what breaks if it fails?”
    Drop the habit of using buzzwords like “scalability” or “eventual consistency” without real context.

    Staying surface-level is just fooling yourself about your skills.
    This traditional method is not going to help you in 2025, instead, go for interactive learning in just 5 simple steps.

    1. Sign up on     layrs.me (it’s free, I’m the founder )
    2. Pick any problem, go to our interactive canvas
    3. Use the components to plan out a flow, put up constraints
    4. Get proper feedback on your design with our AI assistant
    5. By problem 5-6, you will 100% know what each component is built for and how it goes

    Btw, if you can’t find the problem you want to study, you can create a custom one for yourself with the BYOP (Bring your own problem) feature we have on     Layrs.

     Layrs is designed to be LeetCode for system design. Try it out and you won’t go back to reading endless case studies.

    You can also join our Discord channel and talk to us anytime:     https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie

  • I quit Google 2 months ago to build Layrs (layrs.me) 🚀
  • The journey so far has been amazing - filled with learning, ownership, grit, dedication, challenges, and doing things outside my comfort zone that I wouldn't have imagined.
  • Since our launch 3 weeks ago, we’ve been constantly working on improving the experience, gathering feedback, and releasing new features. We even got our first customers in the very first week! 🥳

  • I was ready to take on this challenge for three main reasons:
    1. My entrepreneurial ambitions were catching up to me
    2. I saw real pain points I wanted to solve
    3. I had incredible trust that between my co-founder     Gaurav Pal and me, we would figure something out

    Having people you want to share your success with - just as much as you want to succeed yourself - is one of the most important things when pursuing independent ventures. Because you also share the failures, the hardships, and the uncertainty. But a “we’ll figure this out” mindset pushes you through.

    PS: We have some amazing feature announcements lined up over the next few days. If you haven’t already, check out our system design practice tool at     layrs.me.
    Also, join our Discord (    https://lnkd.in/gut6zaie) for early access to upcoming features.

    PPS: Hopefully, this post will motivate Gaurav to post more on LinkedIn 😛

  • Feed post number 226

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • Not every system design concept deserves your nights and weekends.
  • Not every resource will move you closer to becoming a better engineer.
  • Not every YouTube video or blog post is created with your challenges in mind.
  • Not every pattern or algorithm will show up in your interviews or in your real projects.
  • Not every “best practice” will actually solve a problem you have.

  • → Some topics are foundational. You need to build with them, not just memorize them.

    → Some ideas will click instantly. Others only make sense after you’ve struggled through a real-world problem.

    → Some diagrams look elegant, but don’t teach you the messy details you’ll face on the job.

    → Some solutions are meant to be skimmed, not mastered, because context matters more than completeness.

    → Some “must-learn” topics are just noise until you face them in your work.

    Study system design with intent, not FOMO.

    Your goal isn’t to memorize everything,
    it’s to build a good understanding where it counts.

    If something isn’t making you sharper or more confident, move on.
    Skim, skip, come back when it matters.

    P.S: I recently quit my job at Google to build     layrs.me, it’s LeetCode for System Design. If you’re a software engineer/student or someone just curious about learning system design, sign up and explore the platform.

    The idea behind     Layrs is simple: we grind Leetcode to understand problem solving, but when it comes to system design, we grind theory and prepare blindly.

     Layrs fills that gap and helps you see design interactively, in action with real-world constraints and problems.
