  • Feed post number 227

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 9mo •
  • Follow
  • From IIT Roorkee ➡️ to Flipkart ➡️ to building Layrs now
  • I recall a lot of stories from my days in IIT Roorkee, where I learned a lot and gained experiences of a lifetime. One interesting story is from Day 1, Slot 1 of placements.
  • I was shortlisted by Amazon, Flipkart, and many others. The rule was simple: if you clear a company, you stop interviewing. Amazon and Flipkart had rounds running in parallel. Back-to-back interviews, no breathing room.

  • I finished Amazon R2 and Flipkart R2. Then I heard Amazon R3 had already started without me. Flipkart R3 was minutes away. I tried convincing the panel and the placement cell to let me jump back to Amazon. No luck.

    So I made a call. I committed to Flipkart. Cleared R3. Tried to sit for     Harness in the next slot, but policy said no. The upside: I walked out with a Flipkart offer.

    That choice shaped everything. At Flipkart I worked on real products, learned fast, and helped build the Payouts team from the ground up.

    What I learned:
    - Build an appetite for calculated risk. FOMO kills focus.
    - Rank your target companies before Day 1 so your priorities guide tough calls.
    - Decide fast under pressure. A late yes often turns into a no.
    - Communicate early with the placement cell when slots clash.

    Own the outcome. Once you choose, go all in and execute.

    If you are heading into placements, prepare hard, know your top choices, and back yourself when the moment comes.

    What was your “two rooms, one decision” moment?

    Checkout     Layrs 👉 layrs.me
    Join the community 👉 https://lnkd.in/g3NYiDfC

  • Feed post number 228

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo • Edited •
  • I was hopeless and getting 0 results with my system design preparation and mock interview results until I focused on these 30 essential fundamentals:
  • If you're just beginning, I would advise you to first understand these and then practice problems. You can go to layrs.me and practice, it's a platform we are building for system design learners.
  • 1. Client-Server Architecture
  • ↝ The backbone of almost every web app, clients (browsers, apps) send requests, servers handle them.
  • ↝ Always comes up in system design interviews and backend discussions.

    2. IP Addresses
    ↝ Every server needs a unique “phone number” to talk to other machines.
    ↝ Knowing this helps debug networking issues or set up infra.

    3. DNS (Domain Name System)
    ↝ Converts human-friendly domain names to IP addresses.
    ↝ Vital for understanding outages, latency issues, or why your site’s not resolving.

    4. Proxy & Reverse Proxy
    ↝ Middlemen that can secure, cache, or route requests.
    ↝ You’ll use these for load balancing, privacy, or when troubleshooting network flow.

    5. Latency
    ↝ The unavoidable delay from physical distance between users and servers.
    ↝ High latency = slow apps. Shows up when discussing global scale.

    6. HTTP/HTTPS Protocols
    ↝ The rulebooks for client-server communication (and why we use HTTPS for security).
    ↝ Every REST API or web interaction depends on this.

    7. APIs (REST, GraphQL)
    ↝ How clients and servers talk without knowing each other’s internals.
    ↝ Comes up in every app, integration, and interview design.

    8. SQL vs NoSQL Databases
    ↝ Structured (SQL) vs. flexible, scalable (NoSQL).
    ↝ Key when choosing DBs for a project or defending your design.

    9. Vertical Scaling
    ↝ Making a server bigger (more RAM/CPU).
    ↝ Good for quick fixes, not for long-term growth.

    10. Horizontal Scaling
    ↝ Adding more servers to share the load.
    ↝ Required for true web scale, always asked in interviews.

    11. Load Balancing
    ↝ Evenly distributes requests across servers for uptime & speed.
    ↝ You’ll design this for traffic spikes, high availability.

    12. Database Indexing
    ↝ Supercharges read speed, like a book’s index for finding data.
    ↝ Required knowledge for query optimization.

    13. Replication
    ↝ Making copies of your database for reads, backups, or failover.
    ↝ Core to any HA or DR architecture.

    14. Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning)
    ↝ Splitting large DBs into chunks to spread load.
    ↝ Critical for high-scale systems (think user tables with millions of rows).

    15. Vertical Partitioning
    ↝ Splitting DBs by columns for better performance on wide tables.
    ↝ Helps optimize slow queries that only need a few fields.

    Continued in Comments ↓

  • Feed post number 229

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 9mo •
  • 90% of people I’ve seen study the same 40-50 case studies, the same 45 design problems when it comes to system design preparation.
  • It usually goes like:
  • - Design Google, Facebook, Instagram, etc.
  • - Design URL shortener, or something that’s already out

  • After spending 3 years obsessed with system design, I’ve asked myself multiple times why are we're stuck on the same questions, like it’s some syllabus we’ve got to memorize.

    Why do we not learn from systems that are scaling in front of our eyes in real time, Like Perplexity, like Open AI, and so many other examples out there?

    This is another gap we saw in system design preparation, and so, like true engineers, we built a feature into Layrs to fix this.

    We’re calling it BYOP - Bring Your Own Problem.

    The concept is simple: just type in what you want to practice, and our system will transform it into a proper challenge with specific requirements and constraints.

    You can bring anything:
    📌 A vague idea.
    📌 A specific architecture issue.
    📌 A company specific problem you want to practice before your big interview

    Layrs understands it, then breaks it down.
    ↓
    Then turns it into a structured, FAANG-grade system design problem
    ↓
    With FRs, NFRs, constraints, edge cases
    ↓
    All inside our interactive canvas.
    ↓
    With AI feedback, scoring, and chat.

    This will help you:
    -Stress-test your systems
    -explore ideas you’re too curious to ignore
    -Rehearse real-world infra and product challenges
    -Assess engineers with domain-relevant design tests

    Go to     https://layrs.me
    Welcome to the future of system design.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 10mo • Edited •
  • 🚨 Still stuck solving 2015 system design questions in 2025?
  • Ever debugged a complex infra issue and thought:
  • "Damn, I wish I could’ve practiced this before I hit prod."
  • Ever wanted to revisit a real-world problem -
    But through the lens of scalability, trade-offs, or alt approaches?

    Or maybe you're hiring engineers and thinking:
    "Why am I assessing them with some 2015 interview prompt that has zero overlap with our stack?"

    🎯 We just one-shot all of that.
    Introducing BYOP: Bring Your Own Problem - inside     Layrs.
    You don’t need to wait for us to create your favourite challenge.

    Just type what you want to practice —
    📌 Be vague or specific
    📌 A small subcomponent or a giant architecture
    📌 A startup infra quirk or a scaling nightmare

    💡     Layrs turns it into a structured, FAANG-level system design challenge:
    With FRs, NFRs, constraints, edge cases - all inside our interactive canvas.
    And yes — AI scoring, feedback, and chat come built-in.

    This unlocks infinite scope.
    No more waiting for someone to add your use case.
    No more generic prompts that don’t reflect your world.
    🚪 Want to test your team?
    🧠 Rehearse your own tech decisions?
    🎮 Or just nerd out on something you solved at 3 AM last week?
    Now you can.

    BYOP goes live tonight at 11:59 PM.
    Bring your own problem. Practice what actually matters.
    👇 Take it for a spin.
    Let’s bury the old way of learning.

    Check out what we're building at     layrs.me
    Join our discord: https://lnkd.in/guhkDdxY

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 10mo • Edited •
  • Follow
  • You practiced system design. But did you practice it the way interviewers expect?
  • Every day on Layrs, we see hundreds of designs being created.
  • It’s inspiring, the creativity, the flow of ideas.
  • But there’s a common pattern we’ve noticed:
    Many designs stop at connecting boxes, without defining the properties and responsibilities of those components.

    A design only becomes interview-ready when it answers:
    - What does it do?
    - How does it interact?
    - How does it fail?
    - How does it scale?

    This context is what separates good designs from hire-me-now designs.

    That’s why we created a walkthrough video, not just to show you how to use     Layrs, but how to use it effectively, so your designs stand out.

    If you’re serious about improving your HLD and LLD game, this might be the small shift that changes everything.

    🎥 Watch the walkthrough here:     https://lnkd.in/gXf9pb3S
    🌐 Try Layrs: https://layrs.me
    Join our discord: https://lnkd.in/g_Q3PnQu

  • Feed post number 232

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • In the last 5 years of my life, I’ve managed to go from Flipkart → Google and then leave it all behind to build Layrs…
  • My story didn’t start on a fancy campus or in some elite network. I came from a Tier-3 college, no real coding culture, no community to lean on, no mentors to show the way.
  • Worst of all, I graduated into a world shut down by a pandemic. Landing that first job felt like climbing a mountain, it was one of the toughest things I’ve done.

  • So if you’re wondering,
    Why give up that hard-won     gmail.com to google.com moment?
    Why walk away from the “dream” when most people spend their whole lives chasing it?

    It’s simple, really:
    I kept seeing the same problem everywhere, no matter the company or the team.

    We all learn to code the same way, by actually coding, failing, improving. Platforms like LeetCode exist so anyone, from any background, can drill DSA and see progress with every attempt.

    But when it came to system design, it was a different world.

    We were stuck reading endless blog posts, watching videos, or memorizing diagrams, never building, never truly learning how those systems came alive.

    Every engineer I knew faced the same frustration. You might ace the theory, but when it came time to build, you fumbled because you had no experience.

    Why isn’t there a platform where you can learn system design by actually doing it, just like DSA? Why aren’t we building, experimenting, breaking things, and fixing them the way real systems demand?

    That question wouldn’t leave me alone. It followed me from project to project, company to company.

    So finally, I did what every engineer eventually does when they can’t find what they need: I built it.

    That’s how     Layrs came to life.

    Think of     Layrs as LeetCode for System Design.
    A safe space to make mistakes and learn what actually works when the stakes are high.

    Because you might understand CAP theorem in theory, but applying it when every decision impacts data consistency, system reliability, and user experience is a whole different challenge.

    You might know about sharding from blog posts, but combining it with caching to scale a real-time messaging app? That’s where real growth happens.

    System design is about decisions under pressure, trade-offs, bottlenecks, and ambiguity.
    It’s about thinking beyond diagrams, thinking like a builder.

    So if you’re tired of being stuck in the “theory” loop, if you want to practice system design the way real engineers do ->

    Check out the platform:     layrs.me

    Join our discord: https://lnkd.in/guhkDdxY

  • Feed post number 233

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • LinkedIn might tell you that all Google Engineers in India do is have Paneer Tikka and nap all day in those comfy nap pods…
  • And I wouldn’t blame you for believing them. It does feel like that from the outside, but those things never surprised me.
  • When I was working at Google
  • I wasn’t shocked by the mere scale
    I wasn’t swooning over the perks of the job

    The thing that most pleasantly shocked me was the work ethic and culture of all Googlers.

    You might think that it’s just all meetings, slides, and tons of bureaucracy and approvals
    But what I observed during my time was this:

    - People who were locked and dialed in for their work.
    - People who respected each hour & minute like a resource
    - People who brought immense discipline day in and day out

    I genuinely admired those qualities.
    And quietly tried to learn from them.

    Even after quitting     Google those habits are helping me build Layrs

     - I only do deep work
    - I avoid distractions
    - I am highly focused and clear

    You fight 10s of fires when working at a startup, but these habits keep you true to the goal and protect your focus.

    I am enjoying the journey. If I hadn’t built those habits, maybe I would’ve just spent a year finding my feet and would be delayed.

    Btw, if you’re learning system design, go check out     layrs.me, it’s Leetcode but for system design and I promise you’ll love it, tell me if you don’t :)

  • 10mo •
  • Follow
  • There’s something dangerously beautiful about what IIT silently gives you.
  • Not the degree.
  • Not the brand.
  • But the curse… of not being able to settle.

  • Walk through any IIT WhatsApp group and you’ll see the pattern:
    - Seniors now CTOs, startup founders, solo builders
    - Batchmates who’ve left it all to chase the improbable
    - Juniors who skipped placements to write their own story

    I’m watching 20+ people I know burn the script —
    - Some bootstrapping in stealth,
    - Some raising rounds in public,
    - Some quietly climbing the ranks at FAANG.

    All infected by the same strain of wanting to build something big.
    It’s not ambition.
    It’s compulsion.

    That’s why so many of us are vanishing from FAANG comfort zones and reappearing in founder battlegrounds.
    - ⁠Quitting high paying jobs
    - ⁠Going all-in on fragile ideas
    - ⁠Racing sleep, doubt, and logic to build what doesn't yet exist

    I did the same.

    And what came out of that madness is now live:
    Introducing     Layrs : the first platform that turns system design into a real, tactical skillset, not just interview fluff.

    - 5 free questions to get your feet wet
    - Go Pro to unlock all content
    - Guided courses + LLD modules in the pipeline

    But here’s the twist:
    We're not selling you success.
    We’re handing you the knife.

    Sharpen it… or stay average.

    Layrs is now public.
    But only the ones who feel the burn will stay.

    💻 Try     Layrs today: layrs.me

    🕳️ Go ahead. Take the first step.

  • Feed post number 235

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • 3 years of hard work during my college days gave me an unfair advantage, and I got into Flipkart…
  • 2 years of hard work again at Flipkart as an SDE gave me an unfair advantage, and I got into Google….
  • During the last 5 years of my life, I’ve seen one thing play out again and again: the best things are built at 3 AM, and those nights full of effort compound well.

  • I have a habit of working late. Sometimes, I’m up at 3 AM, and as the clock strikes 3, I get a flashback to my college days.

    Back then, my lectures used to start late (around 10 or 10:30 AM), so I'd stay up until 2 or 3 in the morning, grinding on CodeChef (didn’t even know much about Codeforces at the time).

    My college didn’t have a competitive programming culture, so there wasn’t much guidance from seniors. The only support was the good content online, though even that wasn’t as rich as what’s available today.

    I made every possible mistake you could make while learning, but funny enough, those mistakes ended up helping me the most.

    When you’re sitting alone at night, with nothing but the glow of your screen and your own thoughts, it’s easy to feel desperate or even disappointed with where you are.

    Especially as a student, grinding for months, you start questioning yourself. I still remember, my quality of sleep was directly proportional to how many test cases I could pass on those practice problems.

    Now that I’ve quit Google and am building     Layrs full-time, I see a lot of myself in the students reaching out for opportunities. It’s not easy, especially if you’re from a college without campus placements or in a market like this. I get it. I’ve lived through it.

    But I want to share: those 2-3 years of late nights, quiet hustle, and humility, they shape you in ways nothing else can.

    The struggle strips away ego and teaches you how to keep going even when nobody’s watching. It teaches you to be humble, to stay patient, and to work without needing applause.

    If you’re in that phase right now, wondering if the grind is worth it, doubting if you’re good enough, or if things will ever work out, just know this: it’s normal to feel that way.

    Give those 2-3 years everything you have, with zero arrogance, only humility and a willingness to learn.

    You’ll come out the other side not just with a better resume, but with a mindset that sets you up for real, lasting success.

    Keep going. I am rooting for you.

    P.S. If you're curious about an interactive platform for practicing system design, check out     layrs.me.

  • Feed post number 237

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • Nobody cares about your stealth startup. Because nobody knows it exists.
  • When we started building Layrs, I thought going stealth was the right move - keep everything under wraps, polish the product, and launch a perfect version to the world.
  • That was just simple-mindedness. The idea was:
  • - It would protect us from failing in public
    - We wouldn’t have to worry about engineering shortcomings
    - We wouldn’t make fools of ourselves
    - Everything would go smoothly

    But there’s a lot wrong with that thinking:
    - Without feedback from real users, your product won’t even be good — let -alone perfect
    - Failures are inevitable (our signups broke just after launch!)
    - If people actually want what you’re building, they’ll help - not tear it down (we’ve seen this firsthand since launch)
    - We got investor meetings simply because we were out there - people noticed. And no one notices stealth.

    You don’t need stealth. You need courage.

    We’ve got a lot planned in the pipeline.
    And as promised, we’ll build all of it in public - not in stealth.
    Check out     layrs.me and let us know what you think.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • 🚨 We built something very ambitious inside Layrs.
  • And we’ve kept it hidden… until now.
  • What if prepping for system design interviews didn’t mean bingeing 2-hour videos or memorizing outdated blog posts?
  • What if you could follow clear, guided steps - and build real systems with real-time feedback along the way?

    Introducing: Guided Mode on     Layrs.

    A mode that walks you through system design, one decision at a time.
    🔹 Start with a real-world problem - like building a URL shortener or rate limiter
    🔹 Follow interactive steps to design the system - from picking components to scaling them

    Guided Mode helps you learn by doing - with a path to follow.
    But here’s the thing:
    We haven’t launched it yet.

    Because we want to ask you first:
    👉 Would this help you prepare better for system design interviews?
    👉 Would step-by-step guidance make you feel more confident in your architecture decisions?

    Layrs is a community-driven product.
    And your voice genuinely shapes what we build next.
    Watch the short demo and let us know your honest thoughts 👇
    Should we launch Guided Mode next - or focus elsewhere?

    Meanwhile, explore the practice section on     layrs.me

  • 🎬 Who’s ready to dominate system design and win big?

  • Feed post number 241

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 10mo • Edited •
  • Follow
  • ✨ We launched Layrs yesterday… and wow.
  • 37,000 visitors.
  • ~3K sign-ups.
  • And more DMs than we could possibly keep up with.
  • (If I’ve been slow to reply — this is why 😄)

    When we quit our cushy jobs at     Google and Cashfree Payments to chase this idea, I knew we were onto something. But the real validation? Seeing thousands of engineers jump in on Day 1 to architect, break, iterate — and let our AI tear their systems apart (blamelessly, of course).

    Layrs isn’t a course.
    It’s not a YouTube guide.
    It’s your arena — to practice system design, drag & drop your architecture, and get brutally honest, AI-powered feedback.

    I’ll also share an interesting insight of what happened, post 10 min of our launch post. We hit a hiccup right out of the gate (thanks to a rogue SQL function updating a credits table across DBs without explicit permissions — rookie mistake). But even with a glitchy start, the response has been overwhelming.

    We’ve had people:
    - share how     Layrs will become their go-to prep tool,
    - suggest early user discounts (👀 makes sense — we hear you!),
    - and flood us with ideas on how to make system design practice even better.

    To everyone who signed up, broke things, gave feedback, or simply cheered us on — thank you. 🙏
    This is just Day 1, and we’ve got so much more coming.

    👉 Try it now →     layrs.me
    (and yes, keep those DMs coming — we’re building this with you, for you.)

  • Feed post number 242

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • I quit Google to build Layrs.
  • And today… it’s real. 🚀
  • Layrs is officially LIVE.
  • No more waitlist. No more “coming soon.”
    Just a canvas, a system to design - and a brutally honest AI to push you to get better.

    When I joined     Google, it felt like I had made it.
    But two years in, I realized I still had one itch I couldn’t shake:
    "Why isn’t there a way to actually practice system design — not just read about it?"
    So we built one.

    🎯 Layrs = system design meets AI + hands-on feedback
    Drag. Drop. Architect.
    And get AI-powered reviews that actually tell you what’s missing — and how to improve.

    This isn’t a course. It’s not a guide.
    It’s your practice arena.

    For every engineer who’s prepped at midnight wondering,
    “Did I nail that interview… or just sound confident?”
     Layrs is for you.

    What started as a late-night idea is now a living product.
    Built with     Google’s engineering DNA - docs, iteration, blameless feedback loops - and driven by the belief that every engineer deserves a fair shot at mastery.

    👉 Try it now →     layrs.me
     📲 And follow Layrs for what’s coming next

    To everyone who supported, tested, or just said “you’ve got this” — thank you.
    This is just Day 1.

  • Feed post number 243

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 10mo •
  • A lot of thought goes into making AI assessments on Layrs genuinely helpful.
  • Here are just a few of the questions we’ve been working through lately:
  • 1. How should we show the results? Do we stick to objective scores? Highlight subjective strengths and weaknesses? (We decided a thoughtful mix of both works best.)
  • 2. How do we make assessments consistent and reliable? We don’t want scores that fluctuate wildly due to small design changes. Stability builds trust.
  • 3. How tough is too tough? Most of our users are young engineers. We want to stretch their skills — not demotivate them into switching careers.
    4. When does more context become too much? We use RAG to provide relevant background to the design problem — but too much injected knowledge can backfire and cause hallucinations.
    5. Should     Layrs try to be a one-stop platform? I personally believe that when learning something new, engineers benefit from seeing multiple perspectives. Our north star is to become the best practice tool. Doing too much at once risks diluting what we do best.

    We don’t have all the answers - and that’s exactly why we keep talking to our users.
    Some of the most useful insights have come not from internal debates, but from engineers already using     Layrs.
    That’s the vision: a community-built product, evolving through honest feedback and shared curiosity.

  • Feed post number 245

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 10mo • Edited •
  • Follow
  • Leaving a stable fintech job at Cashfree Payments wasn’t easy. But building Layrs felt too important to ignore.
  • It’s not just a product — it’s the kind of work that makes you feel alive.
  • Today marks my last day at Cashfree Payments, and I’ve been reflecting on what this journey has meant to me — both as an engineer and as a person.
  • When I first joined, I was excited (and honestly, a little intimidated) to dive into systems that move money at scale.
  • From building and refining critical products, jumping into high-pressure war rooms, to long hours spent untangling problems that didn’t have obvious answers, these moments didn’t just sharpen my technical skills, they taught me resilience, ownership, and how to stay calm when stakes were high.
  • But beyond the systems and the scale, what stands out most are the people.
    Working alongside some of the sharpest, most driven folks I’ve met, who don’t shy away from tough challenges, who debate with passion, and who always keep raising the bar.
    The camaraderie, the late-night Slack threads, the casual banter over Chai, these are memories I’ll always carry with me.
    A special thanks to everyone who’s been part of my journey here:
    Rishab Tated Sushane Yadav Jitendra Dhakad GILLELLA SATHWIK REDDY Neeraj Bagdia Jagadish Reddy Amit Chawla Harsha Vardhan Chandra Yogesh Miglani Omkar Kadam Ramkumar Venkatesan thank you for the trust, the constant challenges, and all the opportunities to learn and grow.
    I’ll carry this mindset and DNA forward into everything I take on next.

    So what’s next?
    As I move on, I’m building something close to my heart,     Layrs : a platform like LeetCode, for system design.
    Because reading case studies is one thing, but actually designing systems? That’s how real learning (and confidence) happens.
    If you’re someone who loves diving into architecture, or if this idea sparks your interest, I’d love to connect, jam, or even just swap stories.
    Thank you once again to everyone for the trust, the challenges, and the memories.
    Wishing you all continued success, scale, and impact 🚀

    If you haven't already checked out     Layrs go checkout as our beta is still open and we are moving closer to our launch.
    link:     https://layrs.me

  • Feed post number 247

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 10mo •
  • Follow
  • 48 hours. 100s of automated attacks. 0 breaches.
  • Just wrapped up a real-world security defense sprint on Layrs production servers.
  • Wanted to share what today’s internet threat landscape actually looks like — and how layered defenses made the difference.
  • 🚨 What We Encountered:
    In just 48 hours:
    - Probing for .env files & hidden configs
    - Directory traversal attempts (../../../ exploits)
    - SSL/TLS protocol injections on non-secure ports
    - Automated scans targeting known framework endpoints
    - Database enumeration & brute-force attempts

    🛡️ How We Defended:
    A multi-layered security stack:
    Network Layer: Dynamic iptables rules & persistent blacklists
    Application Layer: Hardened nginx configs, strict headers, rate limits, pattern-based filtering
    Infrastructure Layer: Isolated containers, locked-down security groups, encryption everywhere
    Monitoring & Automation: Real-time log parsing, auto-bans via fail2ban, custom detection scripts

    💡 Key Takeaways:
    If you have a public endpoint, you’re under attack — whether you know it or not. Automation isn’t optional.
    Layered defenses are critical — each layer covers for gaps in others.
    Proactive blocking reduces both security risks and app load.
    Always monitor, always iterate. Security is a moving target.

    📈 Results:
    Zero breaches. Reduced server load. Stronger overall posture.

    Thanks to the anonymous attackers, for helping us test our security measures ✌️

    Curious how others are layering their defenses — what’s one tool you swear by?

    If you haven't already checked out     Layrs, go checkout as our beta is still open and we are moving closer to our launch.
    link:     https://layrs.me

  • Feed post number 248

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 11mo •
  • Life is not a fairytale.
  • That’s what an uncle of mine told me when I said I wanted to switch and join Big Tech… like Google
  • And honestly, I don’t blame him.
  • One of the biggest mistakes we make early in our careers is listening too much to advice that tells us to play safe.
    “Stay put. Wait for the right time.”

    I didn’t take that advice.
    I worked psychotically hard.
    I took the risk.
    And I made it happen.

    But here’s the part people miss:
    I was also mentally okay with the worst-case scenario.
    I was comfortable with the idea of failure… because in our early 20s, the cost of failure is low.
    But the upside? Massive.

    That same mindset is driving me today with     Layrs (layrs.me).
    Leaving a stable job.
    Building something from scratch.
    Chasing something uncertain… again.

    Because if you get too comfortable, you stop growing.
    And if you’re young and comfortable… maybe that’s your signal.

    Take the risk. Do the work. Be okay with failing.

    Life’s not a fairytale. But with enough risk and work… the outcome can be.

  • Feed post number 250

  • Sameer Bhardwaj reposted this
  • Gaurav Pal   • 2nd
  • Co-founder @Layrs | IIT Roorkee
  • 11mo • Edited •
  • Follow
  • Why you fail most of your system design interviews — and what we’re doing to fix that!
  • In most mock interviews or review sessions, design feedback sounds like:
  • ❌ “This won’t scale”
  • ❌ “Add a cache here”
    ❌ “Use Kafka instead of HTTP”

    Sure — you’ve heard what to fix.
    But do you really understand why?

    At     Layrs, we’re building something better:
    ✅ Contextual, layered feedback
    ✅ Step-by-step rationale for improvements
    ✅ Real-world tradeoff explanations like you'd get on the job

    Layrs doesn’t just flag weak points.
    It helps you reason about costs, consistency, reliability, latency, and more — the way real engineers do in real systems.

    Because understanding why is the only way to truly level up.

    If you’ve ever left a system design session more confused than before,     Layrs was built for you.

    Rebuild. Refactor. Rethink.
    Practice with guidance that sharpens your thinking.
    Join the waitlist →     layrs.me

  • The best part of shipping early? The feedback that breaks your assumptions.
  • No matter how much you refine something, there’s nothing like putting it in the hands of real users.
  • When you’re building your own product, it often feels near-perfect, because you carry the full context in your head. But that’s exactly why it's hard to see the blind spots.

  • The feedback we’ve received during the     Layrs beta has been gold.
    It’s challenged our assumptions - about what engineers actually value, what really looks clean, and how people think through system design.

    We’re still in beta →     layrs.me

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 11mo • Edited •
  • I quit Google to build Layrs 🚀
  • Google was my dream company, and finally earning that badge felt like winning the lottery. But ambitions evolve.
  • After 2 incredible years designing and debugging some of the world’s largest systems, I felt a tug I couldn’t ignore - the urge to create from scratch.

  • Layrs is the first practice platform for system design.
    Drag, drop, architect - and get real feedback on every iteration.

    Google taught me the power of clear docs, blameless post-mortems, and relentless iteration. That DNA is baked into Layrs so any engineer can sharpen skills the way Googlers do: hands-on, feedback-driven, and yes - fun.

    If you’ve ever left a system-design interview wondering, “Did I nail it or just sound confident?” -     Layrs is for you.

    🔗 Join the waitlist for beta access →     layrs.me
     📲 Follow Layrs for launch updates

    (BTW, that photo? I forgot to grab a solo shot, so my sister and brother in law agreed to play “unpaid extras” in the Google lobby. Proof I actually worked there - plus free entourage!) 😅

  • Feed post number 258

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 11mo •
  • System Design would never be the same!
  • 🎯  Announcing Layrs — the first platform built for practicing system design.
  • System design is one of the most critical skills in tech—
  • But most people “learn” it by reading threads or watching videos.

    The real problem?
    You don’t get better by watching. You get better by doing.

    That’s why we built Layrs—a space to practice system design actively, with the kind of feedback and structure you’d get on the job.

    🧱 Drag, drop, and build real architectures
    🤖 AI reviews every design thoroughly—so weak spots surface early
    💬 Ask questions and get clarity with Layrs Chat
    🔁 Save, revisit, and iterate like a real engineering workflow

    Layrs is for engineers who want to go beyond theory.
    To design, debug, and refine—not just consume content.

    📹 Check out the demo ↓

  • Feed post number 259

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • At Google, I learned this from the best engineers: protect your time.
  • They had clear work hours, and they stuck to them. No messages at 10pm. No weekend firefighting unless it was absolutely necessary.
  • They were fully present during work, but equally present in their personal lives. That balance wasn’t accidental, it was a choice.
  • When I was starting out, I was the opposite. Always available, eager to help, staying late just to keep learning or finish that one last thing. And honestly, it was exciting.
    But over time, I realized that pace wasn’t sustainable. Especially in high-energy environments, where the work is intense and the bar is high, it’s easy to burn out without even realizing it.

    What changed for me was this: some of my best thinking started happening after I logged off.
    Ideas came while walking. Fixes popped into my head at the gym. Turns out, rest isn’t a reward - it’s part of the process.

    Here are a few small things that made a big difference:
    Set boundaries. I try to be strict about my work hours now. If it’s not urgent, it can wait.
    Prioritize recovery. Walks, workouts, reading, cooking - whatever helps you switch off.
    Work with your rhythm. I do my hard thinking work early in the day. Afternoons are for lighter stuff.

    We glorify hustle a lot in tech, but I’ve learned that balance is not weakness.
    It’s what keeps you going, and growing, for the long run.
  • …more

  • Feed post number 260

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • I cracked Google interviews as a SWE - then imposter syndrome hit hard.
  • If you’ve worked at a big company where your peers are more experienced or come from impressive backgrounds, chances are, you’ve faced imposter syndrome.
  • That feeling that you somehow cracked the interview game, only to realize that the real job is something else entirely: fast, complex, and uniquely challenging.

  • One reason we struggle with it?
    We think we have to maintain the image of an “all-knowing engineer.”

    But here’s what I’ve learned:
    The sooner you accept that you're not the smartest person in the room - and that it’s okay to ask for help - the better you’ll grow.

    Big tech environments run on collaboration.
    Isolating yourself to “figure it all out on your own” doesn’t make you better - it can actually hurt your growth.

    Of course, you shouldn’t rely on others for every small thing. But knowing when to ask for help? That’s a skill.

    Check the ego. Ask questions. Learn faster.
  • …more

  • Feed post number 261

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • In college, Google was my dream company - like many of my peers, I was drawn to the brand and the prestige.
  • But when I finally landed a role there years later, I realized something had shifted for me.
  • Early on, it’s easy to chase big names for the validation, like saying, “I work at XYZ”—without thinking about the actual work.
  • For me, I started caring more about the kind of problems I wanted to tackle, and how that work would help me grow as an engineer.

    In tech, where things change fast, tying your identity to one company can be risky - I’ve seen too many people prep years for a FAANG role only to lose sight of what helps them grow in their field.

    There’s nothing wrong with aspiring to work at a top company.
    But don’t let that be the only goal
  • …more

  • Feed post number 262

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • At Google, I learned a hard truth: perception matters more than you think
  • Engineers often grind late nights or weekends to fix bugs or ship projects.
  • But in competitive workplaces, where several projects run simultaneously, expecting everyone to inquire and remember your contributions isn't practical.
  • Shortcomings? They leave a lasting impression.

    Your perception affects everything — the kind of work you get, the trust your manager has in you, even how your peers treat you.

    I saw a brilliant teammate deliver crucial projects under tight deadlines, unblock teammates (myself included), and lead initiatives end to end. Their impact was recognized — but they were still passed over for opportunities.
    Meanwhile, another engineer — albeit talented — received glowing praise.

    The reason? Undercommunication.

    The fix is simple but game-changing: communicate your impact.

    Don’t assume your extra effort speaks for itself — it gets buried in the noise.
    Being humble is great, but staying silent can leave you feeling overlooked.

    It’s not self-promotion. It’s visibility.

    Start this week: make one contribution visible. Log it or mention in a meeting.
    It’s a small step that compounds into greater recognition and long-term career growth
  • …more

  • Feed post number 263

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • One mistake that I made, and I see a lot of new folks make, is hesitating to ask questions.
  • One of the worst things you can do to yourself as a junior engineer is to stay silent out of fear of being judged.
  • I often see new engineers trying their best to solve tasks entirely on their own. It shows ownership, which is great, but when you’re new to a team or company, collaboration is far more important.

  • Without enough context or support, your progress slows. And this ultimately leads to burnout. Open communication is necessary for a healthy and productive team.

    I know many early-career folks hesitate to reach out because they don’t want to disturb others. They are under the impression that they are respecting their peers' time. While their intention is good, one thing they fail to realise is that helping junior engineers is part of what senior engineers are expected to do. And they usually make time to support others and don’t mind helping.

    Ask. Collaborate. Your mental health and long-term performance depend on it.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st

  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • I’m noticing a trend: folks outside of big tech are adopting AI tools faster than those inside.
  • I was recently talking to a friend who mentioned that he uses Cursor, Taskade, and other AI tools to boost his productivity.
  • Meanwhile, engineers at companies that build AI models, like Google Gemini, mostly use internal tools tailored around the company’s ecosystem.

  • It brings up a question: perhaps people in smaller companies have an easier time adopting the latest AI tools, especially considering how fast this field is evolving.

    Curious - which AI tools are you using on a daily basis for work?

  • Feed post number 265

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • Speed can make or break your Google interview.
  • When I take interviews, I often see candidates able to break down the problem and explain the solution, but they are not able to code it in time.
  • Big tech interviews last for just 45 minutes. Most folks either:
  • - Don’t practice mocks
    - Practice under wrong time constraints

    During my prep I always timed myself. Being able to solve the problem isn’t enough if you can’t implement it in time. And speed will only come with practice.

    So before your next interview:
    - Pick one programming language
    - Practice under time pressure

    This could be the differentiating factor between a ‘hire’ and ‘no hire’ rating.

  • Feed post number 266

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 1yr •
  • I noticed a pattern when I joined Google.
  • The highest-rated engineers weren’t always the smartest in the room — they were the best note-takers.
  • - Every project kickoff? They opened a doc.
  • - Every bug fix? They logged decisions and metrics.

  • Then came performance review season. While the rest of us scrambled to remember what we’d shipped, these engineers had everything documented and ready to go. Their impact looked 10x bigger — not because it was, but because it was visible.

    Now, mentoring junior engineers at startups, I see the opposite problem: they tend to move fast but leave no trail. Since the teams are very small, the managers still know about the impact, but that doesn’t work in big tech.

    Document as you go.

    Keep artifacts for every major task done and update it weekly with links, kudos, and metrics.
    Do this consistently, and your future self (and your manager) will thank you during the performance review.

  • Sameer Bhardwaj   • 1st
  • Co-founder @Layrs | Ex Google
  • Visit my website
  • 3yr •
  • Current job market is particularly tough for freshers. I have been receiving numerous requests for referrals and guidance. I have noticed a recurring pattern and would like to address a few key points, specifically for freshers:
  • 1. Conduct thorough research before seeking a referral. Avoid requesting a referral for 'any' available opening, as the person you approach may not be better equipped than you to find a suitable role.
  • 2. When requesting a referral, kindly include your updated resume and the job ID. This simplifies the process for the person you are reaching out to.

  • 3. While networking on LinkedIn, some of you may have come across guides suggesting the importance of "building a relationship" before seeking referrals. While this approach may work for establishing long-term connections, it's worth noting that many people, like yourself, approach numerous individuals for referrals. In such cases, it can be challenging to respond to generic messages like "Hi, how are you?" It's more effective to be direct and concise in your requests.

    Also, feel free to reach out if you find an opening suitable for you in my org.
